- ☐ A **parse tree** has the start symbol at the root, terminals at leaves, and the yield of leaves (left to right) equals the derived string.
- ☐ A grammar is **ambiguous** if $\exists$ a string with $\geq 2$ parse trees (equivalently, $\geq 2$ LMDs or $\geq 2$ RMDs).
- ☐ **Top-down parsing** = root → leaves; produces LMD; **cannot** handle left recursion.
- ☐ **Bottom-up parsing** = leaves → root; produces reverse RMD (reductions); handles left recursion.
- ☐ **Left recursion** $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ transforms to $A \rightarrow \beta A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$.
- ☐ Always introduce a **fresh non-terminal** $A'$ when eliminating left recursion.
- ☐ **Never forget** the $\varepsilon$ production in the new non-terminal — it represents the base case.
- ☐ The standard arithmetic expression grammar is **left-recursive** — must be transformed before LL parsing.
- ☐ **Operator precedence** is enforced by grammar structure: lower precedence operators appear higher in the parse tree.
- ☐ The language of a CFG is $L(G) = \{w \in T^* \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$.
- ☐ For KTU exams, always **show all derivation steps** with the production used; do not skip steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU 2024 Scheme Tips:**
> 1. Module 2 carries significant weight in Part B questions — expect at least one 7-mark or 14-mark question on CFG transformations.
> 2. The **left recursion elimination algorithm** is a **favourite KTU question** — practice the worked examples until you can solve them in under 5 minutes.
> 3. **Ambiguity proof** requires **two distinct parse trees** (not just two derivations).
> 4. When verifying grammar transformations, **always demonstrate one or two derivations** to prove language equivalence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Left Factoring, Top-Down Parsing - Recursive Descent Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Left Factoring, Top-Down Parsing - Recursive Descent Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Left Factoring — Formal Definition

**Left Factoring** is a grammar transformation technique used in compiler design to rewrite a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) so that it becomes suitable for **predictive top-down parsing**. It is applied when two or more alternative productions for a non-terminal share a common prefix string of grammar symbols, thereby creating ambiguity in deciding which production to apply during parsing.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> *Left Factoring* is a transformation that enables a grammar to be rewritten by deferring the decision about which alternative production to expand until sufficient lookahead has been consumed. Formally, for a non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \mid \ldots \mid \alpha\beta_n \mid \gamma$, the left-factored grammar replaces these with:
> $$A \rightarrow \alpha A' \quad \text{and} \quad A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n \mid \gamma$$
> where $\alpha$ is the longest common prefix (LCP) and $\gamma$ represents any alternatives that do not begin with $\alpha$.

## 1.2 Top-Down Parsing — Formal Definition

**Top-Down Parsing** is the class of syntax analysis strategies in which a parse tree is constructed starting from the **root node** (the start symbol) and progressively expanding non-terminals downward until the leaves match the input token stream. It is the most intuitive parsing approach because it mirrors the leftmost derivation of the input string.

> [!NOTE] **Classification of Top-Down Parsers (KTU Module 2)**
> - **Recursive Descent Parsing** (with or without backtracking)
> - **Predictive Parsing** (LL(1), table-driven, non-recursive)

## 1.3 Recursive Descent Parsing — Formal Definition

**Recursive Descent Parsing** is a specific implementation of top-down parsing in which a set of **mutually recursive procedures** (or functions) is created — one procedure for each non-terminal of the grammar. Each procedure is responsible for recognizing all strings derivable from its corresponding non-terminal. The parsing process begins by invoking the procedure for the start symbol and proceeds by recursively expanding non-terminals based on the current lookahead token.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Critical Constraints (Board-Favorite)**
> - The grammar must be **free of left recursion**
> - The grammar must be **left-factored** (for backtrack-free variants)
> - Each non-terminal maps to **one procedure**

## 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — The Three-Headed Fork in the Road

> [!NOTE] **Plain English Intuition**
> Imagine you are at a **traffic junction** with three roads, all starting with a common 1 km segment (the "common prefix"). Without a signboard, you cannot decide which road to take until you have driven at least 1 km forward (the "lookahead"). **Left factoring** is like installing a signboard at the 1 km mark: you travel the common segment first, then you are presented with a new junction (the new non-terminal $A'$) with three clearly distinct options. Similarly, a recursive descent parser initially doesn't know which production to pick when prefixes collide; left factoring makes the path unambiguous.

## 1.5 Why These Topics Matter — The Engineering Context

In real-world compiler construction (e.g., GCC, LLVM, Clang front-ends, JavaCC, ANTLR), the front-end parser must consume tokens from the lexical analyzer and validate syntactic structure **in linear time** for production-grade performance. Recursive descent parsers are the industry-favored choice because they are:
- **Easy to implement** in any high-level language
- **Debuggable** (each procedure corresponds to a grammar rule)
- **Fast** when paired with proper left factoring
- Used in tools like **JavaCC, ANTLR, LLVM's hand-written parsers**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Left Factoring — Operational Algorithm

The transformation proceeds in **two structured phases**:

### Phase 1: Identification
For every non-terminal $A$ in the grammar:
1. Group all productions of $A$ that begin with the same terminal/non-terminal token.
2. Within each group, identify the **Longest Common Prefix (LCP)** $\alpha$.

### Phase 2: Rewriting
1. Replace $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2 \mid \ldots \mid \alpha\beta_n$ with $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$.
2. Introduce a fresh non-terminal $A'$ with $A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \ldots \mid \beta_n$.
3. Productions not beginning with $\alpha$ are retained as-is under $A'$.

## 2.2 Top-Down Parsing — Two Variants

### Variant A: Backtracking Recursive Descent
- Tries each alternative production sequentially.
- If a match fails, it **rewinds** the input pointer and tries the next alternative.
- **Time complexity:** Exponential in worst case ($O(k^n)$ where $k$ is the branching factor).
- **Drawback:** Impractical for production compilers.

### Variant B: Predictive Parsing (LL(1))
- Uses a **predictive parsing table** indexed by $[Non\text{-}Terminal, Terminal]$.
- Requires exactly **1 token of lookahead**.
- **Time complexity:** Linear $O(n)$ in input length.
- **Drawback:** Grammar must be transformed (left recursion removed, left-factored).

## 2.3 Recursive Descent Parsing — Operational Mechanics

The parsing engine maintains:
- A global input buffer (token stream from lexer)
- A pointer `ip` (input pointer) tracking the current token
- A procedure stack mirroring the call stack

For each non-terminal $A$ with productions $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_k$:

| Token $X_i$ | Action of Procedure for $A$ |
|:---:|:---|
| Terminal $a$ | Match $a$ with current input; advance `ip` if matched, else report error |
| Non-terminal $B$ | **Recursively invoke** the procedure for $B$ |
| $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) | Return without consuming input (empty match) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Cheat Sheet — Board Examination Quick Reference**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Left Factoring Transformation | $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ becomes $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta\beta_2$ | Eliminate common prefix ambiguity |
| Left Recursion Removal | $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ becomes $A \rightarrow \beta A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ | Eliminate left recursion |
| Predictive Parser Complexity | $O(n)$ where $n$ = input length | Linear time guarantee |
| Backtracking Parser Complexity | $O(k^n)$ in worst case | Exponential time |
| FIRST Set Computation | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{a \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta\} \cup \{\varepsilon \text{ if } \alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon\}$ | Determines parsing decisions |
| FOLLOW Set Computation | $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta\}$ | Used when $\varepsilon$ is in FIRST |
| LL(1) Grammar Condition | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$ for all distinct productions | Sufficient for predictive parsing |
| Number of Procedures | One per non-terminal in grammar | Recursive descent requirement |

> [!NOTE] **Strict No-Pipe Rule Reminder**
> The vertical bar `$\vert$` is used in tables to denote "such that" because the standard `|` character is reserved for column separators in markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **JavaCC & ANTLR**: Recursive descent with lookahead is the foundation of these widely-used parser generators.
- **LLVM Front-End**: Hand-written recursive descent parser for C++/Rust syntax.
- **Python (CPython)**: Uses PEG (Parser Expression Grammar) — a direct descendant of recursive descent.
- **V8 JavaScript Engine (Chrome/Node.js)**: Employs recursive descent parsing in its front-end.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Left Factoring — Worked Example

**Original Grammar $G$:**
$$S \rightarrow aAd \mid aBe$$
$$A \rightarrow c \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow d \mid c$$

**Step 1 — Identify the non-terminals with common prefixes.**

For non-terminal $S$, the two alternatives are $aAd$ and $aBe$. Both begin with the terminal $a$. The common prefix is $\alpha = a$. The remaining suffixes are $\beta_1 = Ad$ and $\beta_2 = Be$.

**Step 2 — Apply the transformation rule.**

We introduce a new non-terminal $S'$ and rewrite $S$ as:
$$S \rightarrow aS'$$
$$S' \rightarrow Ad \mid Be$$

**Step 3 — Verify no further factoring is required.**

For $S'$, the two alternatives $Ad$ and $Be$ begin with non-terminals $A$ and $B$ respectively. Since $A \neq B$, no common prefix exists. The transformation is complete.

**Step 4 — Final Left-Factored Grammar $G'$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aS' \\
S' &\rightarrow Ad \mid Be \\
A &\rightarrow c \mid \varepsilon \\
B &\rightarrow d \mid c
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Comprehensive Recursive Descent Parsing — Full Worked Example

**Consider the following left-factored grammar:**
$$E \rightarrow TE'$$
$$E' \rightarrow +TE' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$T \rightarrow FT'$$
$$T' \rightarrow *FT' \mid \varepsilon$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$$

**Token stream to parse:** `id + id * id` ($ represents end of input)

### Recursive Procedure Definitions (Pseudocode)

```
procedure E():
    T()
    E'()

procedure E'():
    if lookahead = '+' then
        match('+')
        T()
        E'()
    else
        // epsilon production: do nothing
        return

procedure T():
    F()
    T'()

procedure T'():
    if lookahead = '*' then
        match('*')
        F()
        T'()
    else
        // epsilon production: do nothing
        return

procedure F():
    if lookahead = 'id' then
        match('id')
    else if lookahead = '(' then
        match('(')
        E()
        match(')')
    else
        error()

procedure match(t):
    if lookahead = t then
        lookahead = nextToken()
    else
        error()
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

| Step | Procedure Call | Lookahead (Before) | Action | Lookahead (After) |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|
| 1 | E() | `id` | Calls T() | `id` |
| 2 | T() | `id` | Calls F() | `id` |
| 3 | F() | `id` | match(`id`) | `+` |
| 4 | T'() | `+` | `+` is not `*`, so epsilon (return) | `+` |
| 5 | E'() | `+` | Sees `+`, calls match(`+`) | `id` |
| 6 | E'() (recursive) | `id` | Calls T() | `id` |
| 7 | T() (recursive) | `id` | Calls F() | `id` |
| 8 | F() (recursive) | `id` | match(`id`) | `*` |
| 9 | T'() (recursive) | `*` | match(`*`) | `id` |
| 10 | F() (recursive) | `id` | match(`id`) | `$` |
| 11 | T'() (recursive) | `$` | Not `*`, epsilon return | `$` |
| 12 | E'() (recursive) | `$` | Not `+`, epsilon return | `$` |
| 13 | E() (root) | `$` | Return successfully | `$` |

**Result:** Input `id + id * id` is successfully parsed.

### Parse Tree Generated

```
            E
           / \
          T   E'
         /|    \
        F T'    + T E'
        |  |      /|  \
       id  ε     F T'   ε
                |  |
               id  *
                   |
                   F
                   |
                  id
```

## 3.3 Identifying When Left Factoring is Required — Decision Matrix

| Grammar Property | Action Required | Reason |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Two alternatives start with the **same terminal** | **Left Factor** | Parser cannot decide with 1-token lookahead |
| Two alternatives start with **different terminals** | **No factoring needed** | Lookahead is sufficient |
| Grammar has **left recursion** $A \rightarrow A\alpha$ | **Remove left recursion first**, then factor | Left recursion causes infinite loop |
| All productions of a non-terminal are unique-prefixed | **Grammar is already LL(1)-compatible** | No transformation required |

## 3.4 Failure Mode Analysis — What Happens Without Left Factoring

**Grammar:** $S \rightarrow aSb \mid aS \mid \varepsilon$

**Input:** `aab`

**Trace Without Factoring (backtracking required):**

| Step | Try Production | Input Position | Match? | Backtrack? |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $S \rightarrow aSb$ | `aab`, `ip` at 0 | Match `a` (consume) | No |
| 2 | (recursive) $S \rightarrow aSb$ | `ab`, `ip` at 1 | Match `a` (consume) | No |
| 3 | (recursive) $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | `b`, `ip` at 2 | Cannot match `b` | **Yes** |
| 4 | Reset `ip` to 1, try $S \rightarrow aS$ | `ab`, `ip` at 1 | Match `a` (consume) | No |
| 5 | (recursive) $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | `b`, `ip` at 2 | Cannot match `b` | **Yes** |
| 6 | Reset `ip` to 1, try $S \rightarrow aSb$ ... | ... | ... | **Yes** |
| ... | (continues with exponential blowup) | ... | ... | ... |

This demonstrates why left factoring is **essential** for efficient predictive parsing.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Recursive Descent Parser Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Source Code Stream] --> lexA[Lexical Analyzer]
    lexA --> tokBufA[Token Buffer]
    tokBufA --> lkA[Current Lookahead Token]
    
    lkA --> procE[Procedure for E]
    lkA --> procEprime[Procedure for Eprime]
    lkA --> procT[Procedure for T]
    lkA --> procTprime[Procedure for Tprime]
    lkA --> procF[Procedure for F]
    
    procE --> procT
    procE --> procEprime
    procEprime --> procT
    procEprime --> epsDecA{Epsilon or Plus}
    procT --> procF
    procT --> procTprime
    procTprime --> procF
    procTprime --> epsDecB{Epsilon or Star}
    procF --> parenDec{Paren or ID}
    
    epsDecA --> procT
    epsDecB --> procF
    parenDec --> procE
    
    procF --> matchA[Match Utility]
    procEprime --> matchA
    procTprime --> matchA
    matchA --> lkA
    matchA --> advA[Advance Token Pointer]
    advA --> lkA
```

## 4.2 Left Factoring Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Original Grammar G] --> scanA[Scan All Productions of Nonterminal A]
    scanA --> grpA{Group by Common Prefix}
    grpA -->|No Common Prefix| keepA[Retain Productions As Is]
    grpA -->|Common Prefix Found| lcpA[Identify Longest Common Prefix alpha]
    lcpA --> introA[Introduce Fresh Nonterminal APrime]
    introA --> rule1A[Add Rule A arrow alpha APrime]
    introA --> rule2A[Add Rule APrime arrow beta1 OR beta2 OR dot dot dot]
    keepA --> nextA{More Nonterminals to Check}
    rule2A --> nextA
    nextA -->|Yes| scanA
    nextA -->|No| doneA[Final Left Factored Grammar GPrime]
```

## 4.3 Top-Down Parsing Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topDownA[Top Down Parsing] --> recDesA[Recursive Descent Parsing]
    topDownA --> predA[Predictive Parsing LL1]
    
    recDesA --> backA[With Backtracking]
    recDesA --> noBackA[Without Backtracking]
    
    backA --> slowA[Exponential Time Onk power n]
    noBackA --> reqA[Requires Left Factoring and No Left Recursion]
    
    predA --> tabA[Table Driven LL1]
    tabA --> stackA[Uses Stack and Parsing Table]
    tabA --> linearA[Linear Time On]
    
    reqA --> predA
```

## 4.4 Recursive Descent Execution Stack — Trace Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as Main Driver
    participant E as Procedure E
    participant T as Procedure T
    participant F as Procedure F
    participant Eprime as Procedure Eprime
    participant Match as Match Utility
    
    Main->>E: invoke for input id plus id star id
    E->>T: invoke
    T->>F: invoke
    F->>Match: match id token
    Match-->>F: success lookahead now plus
    F-->>T: return
    T->>T: epsilon production chosen
    T-->>E: return
    E->>Eprime: invoke
    Eprime->>Match: match plus token
    Match-->>Eprime: success lookahead now id
    Eprime->>T: invoke
    T->>F: invoke
    F->>Match: match id token
    Match-->>F: success lookahead now star
    F-->>T: return
    T->>T: match star token
    T->>F: invoke
    F->>Match: match id token
    Match-->>F: success lookahead now endmarker
    F-->>T: return
    T-->>Eprime: return
    Eprime-->>E: epsilon production chosen
    E-->>Main: parsing complete successfully
```

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Left Factoring with a suitable example. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

Left factoring is a grammar transformation technique applied when two or more alternative productions of a non-terminal share a common prefix. It defers the parsing decision by introducing a new non-terminal.

**Example:**
$$A \rightarrow abB \mid aC \mid a$$

**After left factoring:**
$$A \rightarrow aA'$$
$$A' \rightarrow bB \mid C \mid \varepsilon$$

This enables a predictive parser to choose the correct production using a single lookahead token.

---

### Question 2: List the two essential conditions for a grammar to be parsed by a recursive descent parser without backtracking. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

1. The grammar must be **free from left recursion** (no production of the form $A \rightarrow A\alpha$).
2. The grammar must be **left-factored** (no two alternatives of any non-terminal should share a common prefix).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a)** Consider the following grammar:
$$S \rightarrow iEtS \mid iEtSeS \mid a$$
$$E \rightarrow b$$

**(i)** Show that the grammar is **not LL(1)**. **(4 marks)**
**(ii)** Apply **left factoring** to make the grammar LL(1)-compatible. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **recursive descent parser** (procedure set) for the left-factored grammar obtained in part (a). Show the parse tree for the input `ibeas`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a)(i) Showing the grammar is not LL(1):**

The non-terminal $S$ has three productions:
- $S \rightarrow iEtS$
- $S \rightarrow iEtSeS$
- $S \rightarrow a$

Computing the FIRST sets:
- $\text{FIRST}(iEtS) = \{i\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(iEtSeS) = \{i\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$

Since $\text{FIRST}(iEtS) \cap \text{FIRST}(iEtSeS) = \{i\} \neq \emptyset$, the grammar is **not LL(1)**.

**(a)(ii) Left Factoring:**

The two productions beginning with $iEtS$ share the common prefix $iEtS$. We introduce a new non-terminal $S'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow iEtSS' \mid a \\
S' &\rightarrow eS \mid \varepsilon \\
E &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:**
- $\text{FIRST}(iEtSS') = \{i\}$
- $\text{FIRST}(a) = \{a\}$
- These are disjoint. The grammar is now **LL(1)**.

**(b) Recursive Descent Parser Procedures:**

```
procedure S():
    if lookahead = 'i' then
        match('i')
        match('E')
        match('t')
        S()
        S'()
    else if lookahead = 'a' then
        match('a')
    else
        error()

procedure S'():
    if lookahead = 'e' then
        match('e')
        S()
    else
        // epsilon production
        return

procedure E():
    if lookahead = 'b' then
        match('b')
    else
        error()

procedure match(t):
    if lookahead = t then
        lookahead = nextToken()
    else
        error()
```

**Parse Tree for `ibeas`:**

```
                S
               / \
              i   E   t   S   S'
              |   |       /|\   \
              i   b      a S'   e S
                          |      /|\
                          a     ε  S'
                                   \
                                    ε
```

Input tokens consumed: `i`, `b` (E→b), `t`, `a` (S→a), `e`, `a` (S→a) — **all six tokens matched successfully**.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain **top-down parsing** in detail. Differentiate between **recursive descent parsing** and **predictive parsing**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Given the grammar:
$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid id$$

**(i)** Identify why this grammar **cannot** be used directly for recursive descent parsing. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Transform the grammar to remove the identified issue. **(4 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Top-Down Parsing — Detailed Explanation:**

Top-down parsing constructs the parse tree starting from the **root node** (start symbol) and proceeds downward by expanding non-terminals. It corresponds to finding the **leftmost derivation** of the input string.

**Two Main Variants:**

| Feature | Recursive Descent Parsing | Predictive Parsing |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Implementation | Set of mutually recursive procedures | Table-driven, uses explicit stack |
| Backtracking | May or may not require backtracking | No backtracking |
| Lookahead | May use multiple tokens | Exactly 1 token (LL(1)) |
| Grammar Restrictions | Must be free of left recursion | Must be LL(1) |
| Time Complexity | Exponential with backtracking, linear without | Always linear $O(n)$ |
| Memory | Call stack (automatic) | Explicit stack + parsing table |
| Ease of Implementation | Very simple | Requires table construction |

**(b)(i) Identification of the Issue:**

The grammar contains **direct left recursion** in both $E$ and $T$:
- $E \rightarrow E + T$ (non-terminal $E$ appears as the leftmost symbol on the RHS)
- $T \rightarrow T * F$ (non-terminal $T$ appears as the leftmost symbol on the RHS)

A recursive descent parser would call the procedure for $E$, which immediately calls $E$ again, leading to **infinite recursion** without consuming any input token.

**(b)(ii) Grammar Transformation — Removing Left Recursion:**

Applying the standard algorithm $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ becomes $A \rightarrow \beta A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$:

For $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$:
- $\alpha = +T$, $\beta = T$
- $E \rightarrow TE'$, $E' \rightarrow +TE' \mid \varepsilon$

For $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$:
- $\alpha = *F$, $\beta = F$
- $T \rightarrow FT'$, $T' \rightarrow *FT' \mid \varepsilon$

**Resulting Left-Recursive-Free Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow TE' \\
E' &\rightarrow +TE' \mid \varepsilon \\
T &\rightarrow FT' \\
T' &\rightarrow *FT' \mid \varepsilon \\
F &\rightarrow (E) \mid id
\end{aligned}
$$

This grammar is now suitable for **backtrack-free recursive descent parsing**.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Before Writing!**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write left-factored rules like $S \rightarrow iEtSS'$ but **forget to define $S'$**. The fresh non-terminal MUST have its own production rule. **Deduction: 2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** When removing left recursion, students forget to add the **$\varepsilon$ (epsilon) production** for the new non-terminal. Without $\varepsilon$, the grammar becomes incomplete and rejects valid inputs. **Deduction: 1–2 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the **left-recursion removal** algorithm with **left factoring**. These are TWO different transformations. Left recursion removal deals with $A \rightarrow A\alpha$; left factoring deals with $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$. Examiners will deduct heavily for mixing them up. **Deduction: 3+ marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #4:** In recursive descent procedure writing, students often omit the **lookahead check** before choosing a production. Without `if lookahead = X` guards, the parser cannot disambiguate. **Deduction: 2–3 marks.**
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Forgetting to **advance the lookahead** in the `match()` function — this stalls the parser. **Deduction: 1 mark.**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE] **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam**

- ☐ **Left Factoring**: Rewrites $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta_1 \mid \alpha\beta_2$ as $A \rightarrow \alpha A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2$. Eliminates common-prefix ambiguity.
- ☐ **Top-Down Parsing**: Builds parse tree from **root → leaves**. Two variants: **Recursive Descent** and **Predictive (LL(1))**.
- ☐ **Recursive Descent**: One **procedure per non-terminal**. Procedures are mutually recursive. Simple but slow with backtracking.
- ☐ **Predictive Parsing**: Table-driven, **$O(n)$ linear time**, requires LL(1) grammar.
- ☐ **Two Mandatory Grammar Pre-conditions** for predictive/recursive descent: (1) **No left recursion**, (2) **Left-factored**.
- ☐ **Left Recursion Removal**: $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ becomes $A \rightarrow \beta A'$ and $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$.
- ☐ **FIRST Set Disjointness**: $\text{FIRST}(\alpha_i) \cap \text{FIRST}(\alpha_j) = \emptyset$ is the LL(1) condition.
- ☐ **Epsilon Productions**: Always represent "do nothing" in parser procedures; they require FOLLOW set checks.
- ☐ **match() Function**: Compares lookahead with expected terminal; advances pointer on success, errors on failure.
- ☐ **Parse Tree Order**: Root at top, leaves at bottom; leaves spell the input string read left-to-right.
- ☐ **Industry Relevance**: LLVM, GCC, JavaCC, ANTLR, Python PEG — all use recursive descent or its derivatives.
- ☐ **Board Pattern**: For 14-mark questions, expect (a) theory/concept (7 marks) + (b) numerical/tracing (7 marks).
- ☐ **Time Allocation**: 7-mark sub-part should take ~15–18 minutes; 14-mark question should take ~30–35 minutes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Predictive Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Predictive Parsing # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Predictive Parsing** is a special case of recursive-descent parsing (a top-down parser) in which the choice of the production rule to apply for a non-terminal is uniquely determined by looking at the **current input symbol** (the lookahead symbol). It is a **deterministic, non-backtracking, table-driven** parsing technique that requires no backtracking during derivation. Formally, a predictive parser is a **recursive-descent parser** whose prediction of the next production rule is made possible by reading the next **k** lookahead input symbols. When **k = 1**, it is specifically called an **LL(1) Parser** — the most widely used predictive parsing scheme in compilers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **LL(1) Convention (KTU 2024 Syllabus):** > - **First L** → Scans input **Left to Right** > - **Second L** → Produces a **Leftmost Derivation** > - **1** → Uses **1 symbol** of lookahead to decide the production A grammar that can be parsed by an LL(1) parser (without ambiguity) is called an **LL(1) Grammar**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The GPS Navigation Analogy Imagine you are driving in a city using a **GPS navigator** that shows you the next turn *in advance*, based solely on the road sign you are currently approaching: - The **road sign ahead** = the current input token (lookahead). - The **GPS route sheet** = the **LL(1) Parsing Table**. - The **rules "if sign A → take road 3"** = the production rules stored in the table. - **No reversing or backtracking** is required — once a sign is read, the decision is final. If two different rules produce the same first token (like two different turns both pointing "Straight"), the GPS fails — analogous to a grammar that is **NOT LL(1)**. > [!NOTE] > **Core Idea:** Predictive parsing = *predicting the correct production rule before committing to it*, using only one lookahead symbol and the help of two precomputed sets — **FIRST** and **FOLLOW**. --- ## 1.3 Prerequisites Before Predictive Parsing For any grammar to be parsed predictively, it **must first be transformed**: 1. **Eliminate Left Recursion** (immediate and indirect) 2. **Perform Left Factoring** 3. **Compute FIRST** and **FOLLOW sets** for all non-terminals 4. **Construct the LL(1) Parsing Table** 5. **Verify the table contains no multiple entries** (to confirm LL(1) compatibility) These steps ensure the grammar is unambiguous and deterministic for top-down parsing. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Predictive Parsing? The Motivation Bottom-up parsers (like SLR, LALR) are powerful but complex. Top-down parsers are intuitive but naive recursive-descent parsers suffer from **backtracking**. Predictive parsing eliminates backtracking using: - **FIRST Sets** → tells what terminals can BEGIN a string derived from a non-terminal. - **FOLLOW Sets** → tells what terminals can IMMEDIATELY FOLLOW a non-terminal in a sentential form. - **Parsing Table M** → a 2D matrix `M[Non-terminal, Terminal]` that stores the production to apply. --- ## 2.2 FIRST Set — Formal Definition For any grammar symbol $\alpha$, **FIRST($\alpha$)** is the set of terminals that begin any string derivable from $\alpha$. $$ \text{FIRST}(\alpha) = \{ a \in (T \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \mid \alpha \Rightarrow^* a\beta \} $$ Where $T$ is the set of terminals and $\varepsilon$ denotes the empty string. ### Rules to Compute FIRST(X) | Rule | Condition | Action | |------|-----------|--------| | 1 | If $X$ is a terminal | $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$ | | 2 | If $X \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is a production | Add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$ | | 3 | If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ | Add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) - \{\varepsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$ | | 4 | If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_i)$ for all $i$ from 1 to $k$ | Add $\varepsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$ | --- ## 2.3 FOLLOW Set — Formal Definition For any non-terminal $A$, **FOLLOW($A$)** is the set of terminals that can appear immediately to the right of $A$ in some sentential form. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \in T \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \} $$ ### Rules to Compute FOLLOW(A) | Rule | Condition | Action | |------|-----------|--------| | 1 | $A$ is the start symbol $S$ | Add $\$$ (end-of-input marker) to $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | | 2 | Production: $B \rightarrow \alpha A \beta$ | Add $\text{FIRST}(\beta) - \{\varepsilon\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | | 3 | Production: $B \rightarrow \alpha A$, OR $B \rightarrow \alpha A \beta$ where $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$ | Add $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | --- ## 2.4 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following table is a high-yield, exam-ready summary of all FIRST/FOLLOW rules and table construction logic.** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Use in Predictive Parsing | |---------|----------------|----------------------------| | LL(1) Grammar Condition | For each non-terminal $A$ and terminal $a$: at most one production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ with $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Decides grammar eligibility | | FIRST Computation | $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{ a \mid X \Rightarrow^* a\beta \}$ | Used for production selection | | FOLLOW Computation | $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{ a \mid S \Rightarrow^* \alpha A a \beta \}$ | Used for $\varepsilon$-productions | | Table Entry Rule | For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$: for every $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, a]$ | Builds LL(1) table | | $\varepsilon$-production Handling | If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, b]$ | Handles empty derivations | | Conflict | More than one entry in $M[A, a]$ | **Grammar is NOT LL(1)** | | Parser Stack Top | Top of stack symbol $X$ matched with lookahead $a$ | Drives parser actions | --- ## 2.5 LL(1) Parsing Algorithm (Pseudo-Code) $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Initialize stack with } \$S \text{ (top is } S \text{)} \\ &\text{Initialize input buffer with input followed by } \$ \\ &\text{Repeat until stack top } = \$ \text{ AND input symbol } = \$: \\ &\quad \text{Let } X = \text{top of stack, } a = \text{current input symbol} \\ &\quad \textbf{if } X = a = \$: \textbf{ accept} \\ &\quad \textbf{if } X = a: \text{ pop } X, \text{ advance input pointer} \\ &\quad \textbf{if } X \text{ is a terminal: error} \\ &\quad \textbf{if } M[X, a] = \{X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k\}: \\ &\quad\quad \text{pop } X, \text{ push } Y_k, Y_{k-1}, \dots, Y_1 \text{ (in that order)} \\ &\quad \textbf{else: error (call error recovery routine)} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Predictive parsing is extensively used in: - **Hand-written compilers** for educational languages (e.g., Wirth's Pascal compilers). - **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** where grammar simplicity matters. - **Expression evaluators** in spreadsheets, calculators, and configuration parsers. - **HTML/XML parser generators** like ANTLR (which uses adaptive LL(*) parsing — a generalized form of LL(k)). - **SQL query front-ends** in database engines. --- ## 2.7 Limitations of LL(1) Parsers - Cannot handle **left-recursive** grammars (must eliminate first). - Cannot parse **ambiguous** grammars directly. - Limited to **1-token lookahead** — fails on inherently ambiguous constructs. - May require extensive **left factoring** to make grammar LL(1) compatible. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Reference Grammar (Used Throughout the Notes) Consider the classic arithmetic expression grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} &E \rightarrow E + T \mid T \\ &T \rightarrow T * F \mid F \\ &F \rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ This grammar is **left-recursive** and is NOT LL(1). We must transform it first. --- ## 3.2 Step 1: Eliminate Left Recursion Using the standard transformation rule: If $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$, replace with: $A \rightarrow \beta A'$, $A' \rightarrow \alpha A' \mid \varepsilon$ Applying to our grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} &E \rightarrow T E' \\ &E' \rightarrow + T E' \mid \varepsilon \\ &T \rightarrow F T' \\ &T' \rightarrow * F T' \mid \varepsilon \\ &F \rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > This new grammar is **free of left recursion**. Now we compute FIRST and FOLLOW. --- ## 3.3 Step 2: Compute FIRST Sets We apply the FIRST rules iteratively until no change occurs. **Iteration 1:** - $\text{FIRST}(F)$: From $F \rightarrow ( E )$, add $\{$`(`, `id`$\}$. No $\varepsilon$. - $\text{FIRST}(T')$: From $T' \rightarrow * F T'$, add $\{ * \}$. From $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$, add $\{ \varepsilon \}$. - $\text{FIRST}(T)$: From $T \rightarrow F T'$, add $\text{FIRST}(F) = \{ $(`, `id` $\}$. - $\text{FIRST}(E')$: From $E' \rightarrow + T E'$, add $\{ + \}$. From $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$, add $\{ \varepsilon \}$. - $\text{FIRST}(E)$: From $E \rightarrow T E'$, add $\text{FIRST}(T) = \{ $(`, `id` $\}$. **Iteration 2:** No new symbols added. Iteration terminates. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FIRST}(E) &= \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \\ \text{FIRST}(E') &= \{+\,,\ \varepsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(T) &= \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \\ \text{FIRST}(T') &= \{*\,,\ \varepsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(F) &= \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.4 Step 3: Compute FOLLOW Sets **Rule Applied:** Start symbol gets `\$`. Then iteratively propagate. **Iteration 1:** - $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$: $E$ is the start symbol → add $\$$. Production $F \rightarrow ( E )$: $\text{FIRST}(\ ) = \{ ) \}$ → add $\{ ) \}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(E)$. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\,,\ )\} $$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$: Production $E \rightarrow T E'$: nothing follows $E'$ in $E$'s RHS directly except end-of-derivation → add $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\,,\ )\}$. Production $E' \rightarrow + T E'$: nothing follows → add $\text{FOLLOW}(E')$. (Self-loop, resolved later.) $$ \text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$\,,\ )\} $$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(T)$: Production $E \rightarrow T E'$: $E'$ follows $T$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, add $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$\,,\ )\}$. Production $E' \rightarrow + T E'$: $E'$ follows $T$, again $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(E')$, add $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$\,,\ )\}$. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\} $$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(T')$: Production $T \rightarrow F T'$: $T'$ at end → add $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\}$. Production $T' \rightarrow * F T'$: similar → add $\text{FOLLOW}(T')$. Self-loop. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\} $$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(F)$: Production $T \rightarrow F T'$: $T'$ follows $F$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, add $\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\}$. Production $T' \rightarrow * F T'$: $T'$ follows $F$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(T')$, add $\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\}$. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\$\,,\ ),\ +,\ *\} $$ **Iteration 2:** No changes. Final FOLLOW sets: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(E) &= \{\$\,,\ )\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(E') &= \{\$\,,\ )\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(T) &= \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(T') &= \{\$\,,\ ),\ +\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(F) &= \{\$\,,\ ),\ +,\ *\} \end{aligned} $$ --- ## 3.5 Step 4: Construct the LL(1) Parsing Table For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$: - For every terminal $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, set $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$. - If $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, for every $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, set $M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha$. ### Analytical Table — Production Placement | Production | FIRST($\alpha$) | FOLLOW (if $\varepsilon$) | Table Cells Filled | |------------|------------------|----------------------------|---------------------| | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | $\{(,\ \text{id}\}$ | — | $M[E, (]$, $M[E, \text{id}]$ | | $E' \rightarrow + T E'$ | $\{+\}$ | — | $M[E', +]$ | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\{\$\,,\ )\}$ | $M[E', \$]$, $M[E', )]$ | | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | $\{(,\ \text{id}\}$ | — | $M[T, (]$, $M[T, \text{id}]$ | | $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ | $\{*\}$ | — | $M[T', *]$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\{\$\,,\ ),\ +\}$ | $M[T', \$]$, $M[T', )]$, $M[T', +]$ | | $F \rightarrow ( E )$ | $\{( \}$ | — | $M[F, (]$ | | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | $\{\text{id}\}$ | — | $M[F, \text{id}]$ | ### Final LL(1) Parsing Table | | **id** | **+** | **\*** | **(** | **)** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | **E** | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | | | **E'** | | $E' \rightarrow + T E'$ | | | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | **T** | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | | | **T'** | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ | | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | **F** | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | | | $F \rightarrow ( E )$ | | | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No multiple entries** → The grammar is verified as **LL(1)**. Each cell contains at most one production. --- ## 3.6 Step 5: Trace the Parser on Input `id + id * id` Using the **Stack / Input / Action** trace format: | Step | Stack (top → bottom read) | Input (read pointer) | Action | |------|---------------------------|----------------------|--------| | 1 | `$ E` | `id + id * id $` | $E \rightarrow T E'$ | | 2 | `$ E' T` | `id + id * id $` | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | 3 | `$ E' T' F` | `id + id * id $` | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | | 4 | `$ E' T' id` | `id + id * id $` | Match `id` | | 5 | `$ E' T'` | `+ id * id $` | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (since $+ \in \text{FOLLOW}(T')$) | | 6 | `$ E'` | `+ id * id $` | $E' \rightarrow + T E'$ | | 7 | `$ E' T +` | `+ id * id $` | Match `+` | | 8 | `$ E' T` | `id * id $` | $T \rightarrow F T'$ | | 9 | `$ E' T' F` | `id * id $` | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | | 10 | `$ E' T' id` | `id * id $` | Match `id` | | 11 | `$ E' T'` | `* id $` | $T' \rightarrow * F T'$ | | 12 | `$ E' T' F *` | `* id $` | Match `*` | | 13 | `$ E' T' F` | `id $` | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ | | 14 | `$ E' T' id` | `id $` | Match `id` | | 15 | `$ E' T'` | `$` | $T' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | 16 | `$ E'` | `$` | $E' \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | 17 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** | The input is successfully parsed with **no backtracking**, confirming deterministic LL(1) parsing. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LL(1) Predictive Parser ```mermaid flowchart TB inputA["Source Program
Token Stream"] --> buf["Input Buffer
id + id * id $"] buf --> lookahead["Lookahead Pointer
Current Token = id"] lookahead --> driver{"Parser Driver
Stack Top vs Lookahead"} driver --> match["MATCH Action
Pop X, Advance Input"] driver --> expand["EXPAND Action
Pop X, Push RHS of M[X,a]"] driver --> accept["ACCEPT
Stack = $ and Input = $"] driver --> err["ERROR
No Entry in M[X,a]"] stackA["Parser Stack
$ E"] --> driver subgraph TableLL1["LL(1) Parsing Table M"] t1["M[E, id] = E -> T E'"] t2["M[E', +] = E' -> + T E'"] t3["M[T, id] = T -> F T'"] t4["M[T', *] = T' -> * F T'"] t5["M[F, id] = F -> id"] t6["M[F, (] = F -> ( E )"] t7["M[T', +] = T' -> epsilon"] t8["M[T', $] = T' -> epsilon"] end TableLL1 --> driver driver --> outputA["Output
Leftmost Derivation / Parse Tree"] err --> recovery["Error Recovery
Panic Mode / Synchronize"] ``` --- ## 4.2 Algorithm Flowchart — Parser Driver Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["Start Parser"] --> initA["Push $ then Push Start Symbol S
Point Input to first token"] initA --> loopCheck{"Stack Top X
= Input Symbol a
= $ ?"} loopCheck -->|"Yes"| acceptOut["ACCEPT
Parsing Successful"] loopCheck -->|"No"| topCheck{"Stack Top X
= Input a ?"} topCheck -->|"Yes"| matchAct["Pop X
Advance Input Pointer"] topCheck -->|"No"| termCheck{"X is
a Terminal ?"} termCheck -->|"Yes"| errOut["ERROR
Unexpected Symbol"] termCheck -->|"No"| tableLookup{"M[X, a]
exists ?"} tableLookup -->|"Yes"| prod["Pop X
Push RHS Y_k ... Y_1
(k symbols right-to-left)"] tableLookup -->|"No"| errOut prod --> loopCheck matchAct --> loopCheck ``` --- ## 4.3 Process Map — Grammar → Table → Parser ```mermaid flowchart LR g1["Original Grammar
E -> E + T | T
T -> T * F | F
F -> ( E ) | id"] --> g2["Transform Grammar
Remove Left Recursion
Left Factor"] g2 --> g3["Compute FIRST Sets
FIRST(X)"] g3 --> g4["Compute FOLLOW Sets
FOLLOW(A)"] g4 --> g5["Build LL(1) Table M
Check for Conflicts"] g5 --> decision{"Grammar is
LL(1) ?
(No Conflicts)"} decision -->|"Yes"| parser["Use Predictive Parser
with Stack + Input Buffer"] decision -->|"No"| revise["Revise Grammar
Apply Left Factoring
or Rewrite Rules"] revise --> g2 parser --> result["Parse Tree /
Syntax Error Report"] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is predictive parsing? Explain why LL(1) grammars are significant in compiler design.** **Model Answer:** Predictive parsing is a top-down, recursive-descent parsing technique that uses **one lookahead symbol** to uniquely choose which production rule to apply for a non-terminal, eliminating the need for backtracking. LL(1) grammars are significant because: 1. They can be parsed using a simple **deterministic table-driven algorithm**. 2. Parsing time is **linear O(n)** in the length of the input. 3. They are easy to **implement by hand** and well-suited for **automatic parser generators** like ANTLR. 4. They enable clear, efficient **error reporting and recovery** using stack-based mechanisms. --- ### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **Define FIRST and FOLLOW sets. Why is the FOLLOW set necessary if a production contains $\varepsilon$?** **Model Answer:** - **FIRST($\alpha$)**: The set of terminals that begin any string derivable from $\alpha$. If $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$, then $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$. - **FOLLOW($A$)**: The set of terminals that can appear **immediately after** non-terminal $A$ in some sentential form. The end-marker $\$$ is included if $A$ can be the rightmost symbol. The FOLLOW set is necessary when a production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ has $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$. In such a case, $A$ may derive the empty string, and the parser must decide what to do — it pops $A$ from the stack and allows the input token (which must be in FOLLOW($A$)) to be matched next. --- ## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't skip the conflict check.** If two productions enter the same cell, the grammar is **NOT** LL(1) — students frequently miss this and lose **2–3 marks**. > 2. **Always show intermediate iterations** for FIRST and FOLLOW. Final answers without derivation steps get **partial credit only**. > 3. **Order matters** when pushing production RHS onto the stack — push **rightmost symbol first** so leftmost ends up on top. > 4. **Include `\$` (end marker)** in the FOLLOW set of the start symbol. Omitting `\$` loses **1 mark**. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Consider the following grammar:** $$ \begin{aligned} &S \rightarrow A B \\ &A \rightarrow a A \mid \varepsilon \\ &B \rightarrow b B \mid \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute FIRST and FOLLOW for every non-terminal, and construct the LL(1) parsing table. Verify that the grammar is LL(1). **(b) [7 Marks)** Trace the predictive parser for the input string `a b $`, showing the stack contents, input buffer, and actions at each step. State whether the input is accepted. #### **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Step 1: Compute FIRST sets** Apply rules iteratively: - $\text{FIRST}(A)$: From $A \rightarrow aA$: add $\{a\}$. From $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$: add $\{\varepsilon\}$. - $\text{FIRST}(B)$: From $B \rightarrow bB$: add $\{b\}$. From $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$: add $\{\varepsilon\}$. - $\text{FIRST}(S)$: From $S \rightarrow AB$: add $\text{FIRST}(A) - \{\varepsilon\} = \{a\}$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(A)$, also add $\text{FIRST}(B) - \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(B)$ as well, add $\varepsilon$. $$ \text{FIRST}(S) = \{a,\ b,\ \varepsilon\} $$ **Final FIRST sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FIRST}(S) &= \{a,\ b,\ \varepsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(A) &= \{a,\ \varepsilon\} \\ \text{FIRST}(B) &= \{b,\ \varepsilon\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Compute FOLLOW sets** - $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$: $S$ is the start symbol → add $\$$. No other rule has $S$ on the RHS. - $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$: Production $S \rightarrow AB$: $B$ follows $A$. Add $\text{FIRST}(B) - \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$. Since $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(B)$, also add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{b,\ \$\} $$ - $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$: Production $S \rightarrow AB$: $B$ is at the end of RHS → add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(B) = \{\$\} $$ **Final FOLLOW sets:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{FOLLOW}(S) &= \{\$\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(A) &= \{b,\ \$\} \\ \text{FOLLOW}(B) &= \{\$\} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Build the LL(1) Parsing Table** | Production | FIRST | FOLLOW (if $\varepsilon$) | Cells | |------------|-------|---------------------------|-------| | $S \rightarrow AB$ | $\{a,\ b,\ \varepsilon\}$ | $\{\$\}$ | $M[S, a]$, $M[S, b]$, $M[S, \$]$ | | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $\{a\}$ | — | $M[A, a]$ | | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\{b,\ \$\}$ | $M[A, b]$, $M[A, \$]$ | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $\{b\}$ | — | $M[B, b]$ | | $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\{\$\}$ | $M[B, \$]$ | **Final LL(1) Parsing Table:** | | **a** | **b** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---| | **S** | $S \rightarrow AB$ | $S \rightarrow AB$ | $S \rightarrow AB$ | | **A** | $A \rightarrow aA$ | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | | **B** | | $B \rightarrow bB$ | $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | > **Verification:** Every cell has at most one production. The grammar is **LL(1)**. ✓ --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Trace for input `a b $`:** | Step | Stack (top → bottom) | Input | Action | |------|----------------------|-------|--------| | 1 | `$ S` | `a b $` | $S \rightarrow AB$ | | 2 | `$ B A` | `a b $` | $A \rightarrow aA$ | | 3 | `$ B A a` | `a b $` | Match `a` | | 4 | `$ B A` | `b $` | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (since $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$) | | 5 | `$ B` | `b $` | $B \rightarrow bB$ | | 6 | `$ B b` | `b $` | Match `b` | | 7 | `$ B` | `$` | $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (since $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(B)$) | | 8 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** | **Result:** The input `a b` is **successfully accepted** by the predictive parser. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Consider the following augmented grammar after left recursion elimination:** $$ \begin{aligned} &S \rightarrow a S b S \mid b S \mid \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **(a) [7 Marks]** Compute FIRST and FOLLOW sets for $S$, and construct the LL(1) parsing table. Is the grammar LL(1)? Justify. **(b) [7 Marks]** Show the parser trace for the input `a b b $` using the predictive parsing algorithm. #### **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Step 1: FIRST set computation** - $S \rightarrow aSbS$: $\text{FIRST}(aSbS)$ starts with $a$ → add $\{a\}$. - $S \rightarrow bS$: starts with $b$ → add $\{b\}$. - $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$: add $\{\varepsilon\}$. $$ \text{FIRST}(S) = \{a,\ b,\ \varepsilon\} $$ **Step 2: FOLLOW set computation** - $S$ is the start symbol → add $\$$. - Production $S \rightarrow aSbS$: The second $S$ follows $b$. Add $\text{FIRST}(bS) - \{\varepsilon\} = \{b\}$. The third $S$ is at end → add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$. - Production $S \rightarrow bS$: $S$ at end → add $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$. $$ \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{a,\ b,\ \$\} $$ **Step 3: LL(1) Parsing Table** | Production | FIRST | FOLLOW (if $\varepsilon$) | Cells | |------------|-------|---------------------------|-------| | $S \rightarrow aSbS$ | $\{a\}$ | — | $M[S, a]$ | | $S \rightarrow bS$ | $\{b\}$ | — | $M[S, b]$ | | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\{a,\ b,\ \$\}$ | $M[S, a]$, $M[S, b]$, $M[S, \$]$ | **LL(1) Parsing Table:** | | **a** | **b** | **\$** | |---|---|---|---| | **S** | $S \rightarrow aSbS$ | $S \rightarrow bS$ | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | > **But wait!** For terminal $a$: two entries! > - From $S \rightarrow aSbS$: $a \in \text{FIRST}(aSbS)$ > - From $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$: $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ **The grammar is NOT LL(1)** because $M[S, a]$ has a conflict. This is an example of a grammar that requires **more than 1 lookahead** to disambiguate. --- #### **Model Solution for Part (b):** Since the grammar is not LL(1), the parser will encounter an error. | Step | Stack | Input | Action | |------|-------|-------|--------| | 1 | `$ S` | `a b b $` | **CONFLICT** in $M[S, a]$: both $S \rightarrow aSbS$ and $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ apply | If the parser arbitrarily chooses $S \rightarrow aSbS$: | Step | Stack | Input | Action | |------|-------|-------|--------| | 1 | `$ S` | `a b b $` | $S \rightarrow aSbS$ | | 2 | `$ S b S a` | `a b b $` | Match `a` | | 3 | `$ S b S` | `b b $` | $S \rightarrow bS$ (only choice for $b$) | | 4 | `$ S b S b` | `b b $` | Match `b` | | 5 | `$ S b S` | `b $` | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (since $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$) | | 6 | `$ S b` | `b $` | Match `b` | | 7 | `$ S` | `$` | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (since $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$) | | 8 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** | **Lesson:** The grammar is semantically unambiguous (it accepts strings of the form $a^n b^n$), but it requires **inherently ambiguous 1-token lookahead**. This is a classic case motivating **LR parsers** over LL(1). --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Predictive Parsing = LL(1) Parsing** (1-token lookahead, top-down, non-backtracking). - **Always** perform **Left Recursion Elimination** and **Left Factoring** before LL(1) parsing. - **FIRST($X$)** contains terminals that can **start** a string derived from $X$; $\varepsilon$ may be present. - **FOLLOW($A$)** contains terminals that can **immediately follow** $A$ in any sentential form; always contains `\$` for the start symbol. - **LL(1) Table Rule:** $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ if $a \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$; if $\varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then $M[A, b] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ for all $b \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$. - **Multiple entries in any table cell → Grammar is NOT LL(1)**. - **Parser Stack Convention:** Push RHS **right-to-left** so that the leftmost symbol ends up on top. - **Time complexity:** $O(n)$ where $n$ = length of input token stream. - **Match action** pops terminal when stack top equals input symbol. - **Accept condition:** Stack top `$` AND input symbol `$` simultaneously. - **Error recovery:** Use FOLLOW($A$) as synchronization tokens for non-terminal $A$ — pop $A$ if the lookahead is not in FOLLOW($A$). - **Limitation:** LL(1) **cannot parse** left-recursive or ambiguous grammars — these require LR-family parsers. - **ANTLR** (used in industry) is based on adaptive **LL(\*)** — a generalization of LL(1) with arbitrary lookahead.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LL(1) Grammars**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of LL(1) Grammar

An **LL(1) Grammar** is a context-free grammar for which a **top-down predictive parser** can be constructed by examining the input from **Left to Right**, producing a **Leftmost derivation**, and using **1 (one)** lookahead symbol to make parsing decisions at every step without backtracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decoding the Acronym "LL(1)":**
> - The **First "L"** $\rightarrow$ **Left-to-right scan** of the input string.
> - The **Second "L"** $\rightarrow$ Derives the **Leftmost derivation** of the sentential form.
> - The **"1"** $\rightarrow$ Uses exactly **one input symbol of lookahead** to decide which production to apply.

A grammar $G$ is **LL(1)** if and only if whenever a non-terminal $A$ has two distinct productions $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$, the following conditions hold:

$$
\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) = \emptyset
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim-style):** A context-free grammar is said to be **LL(1)** if its **LL(1) parsing table** can be constructed **without any multiply-defined entries** (no conflicts). The single lookahead symbol must unambiguously determine which production rule to apply at every parsing step.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Recipe + Next Ingredient" Model

Imagine you are a **chef** reading a cooking recipe for the first time. The recipe says:

```
Recipe → Step1 Step2 Step3
Step1  → "Chop Onions" | "Chop Garlic"
Step2  → "Add Salt"   | "Add Pepper"
```

You are cooking, and you want to know what to do **right now**. Instead of memorizing the whole recipe, you simply **peek at the next ingredient on your counter (lookahead = 1 item)**:

- If you see **"Onion"** on the counter $\rightarrow$ execute `Step1 → "Chop Onions"`.
- If you see **"Garlic"** on the counter $\rightarrow$ execute `Step1 → "Chop Garlic"`.

You **never need to flip back** to re-check the recipe. The single peek is enough to make a confident, deterministic choice.

> **This is exactly what an LL(1) parser does:** it peeks at the **next input token** (lookahead) and uses it to choose the **correct production rule** deterministically — **no backtracking, no ambiguity**.

---

## 1.3 Components of LL(1) Parsing

| Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| **FIRST($\alpha$) Set** | The set of terminal symbols that can begin a string derivable from $\alpha$ |
| **FOLLOW($A$) Set** | The set of terminal symbols that can immediately follow non-terminal $A$ in a sentential form |
| **Parsing Table $M$** | A 2D table $M[\text{Non-Terminal}, \text{Terminal}]$ that maps parser state to production rule |
| **Predictive Parser Driver** | A stack-based algorithm that uses the table to parse input without backtracking |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Distinction:** The symbol **"1"** in LL(1) refers strictly to **one lookahead token**, not one production, not one non-terminal. Any grammar requiring more than one symbol of lookahead (LL(2), LL(k)) is **out of scope for LL(1) parsing**.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars: FIRST and FOLLOW Sets

The entire LL(1) parsing framework rests on **two set-construction algorithms**. Without computing them correctly, no parsing table can be built.

### 2.1.1 FIRST Set — Definition

> **Definition:** $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ is the set of terminal symbols that can appear as the **leftmost symbol** in any string derivable from $\alpha$ in one or more steps. If $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \epsilon$, then $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$.

**Why do we need FIRST?** Because when the parser sees a non-terminal $A$ on the stack and a terminal $a$ on the input, it must know: *"If I apply the production $A \rightarrow \alpha$, will the input symbol $a$ match the first terminal $\alpha$ can produce?"* The answer lies in FIRST($\alpha$).

### 2.1.2 FOLLOW Set — Definition

> **Definition:** $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is the set of terminal symbols that can **immediately follow** non-terminal $A$ in some sentential form. If $A$ can be the rightmost symbol in a sentential form, then **\$** (the end-of-input marker) is in FOLLOW($A$).

**Why do we need FOLLOW?** Because if a production is $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ (i.e., $A$ derives the empty string), then the parser must know *"What terminals can legally appear when $A$ is on the stack but produces nothing?"* The answer is FOLLOW($A$).

---

## 2.2 Algorithms for Computing FIRST and FOLLOW

### 2.2.1 Algorithm: Computing FIRST Sets

> [!NOTE]
> **This is a KTU University Exam Favorite. Memorize the 4 rules.**

**Rule 1:** If $X$ is a **terminal**, then $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{\,X\,\}$.

**Rule 2:** If $X \rightarrow \epsilon$ is a production, then add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

**Rule 3:** If $X \rightarrow Y_1\,Y_2\,Y_3 \cdots Y_k$ is a production:
- Add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) \setminus \{\epsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
- If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, then add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2) \setminus \{\epsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
- Continue this way. If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1) \cap \text{FIRST}(Y_2) \cap \cdots \cap \text{FIRST}(Y_k)$, then add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

**Rule 4:** Keep applying rules until **no new symbol** can be added to any FIRST set (**fixed-point iteration**).

---

### 2.2.2 Algorithm: Computing FOLLOW Sets

**Rule 1:** Place **\$** in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$, where $S$ is the **start symbol**.

**Rule 2:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ is a production, then add everything in $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\epsilon\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

**Rule 3:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ is a production (i.e., $B$ is at the end, so $\beta$ is empty), **or** if $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ and $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then add everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

**Rule 4:** Keep applying rules until **no new symbol** can be added (fixed-point).

---

## 2.3 Construction of the LL(1) Parsing Table

Once all FIRST and FOLLOW sets are computed, the parsing table $M[\text{Non-Terminal}, \text{Terminal}]$ is built using the following algorithm:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Algorithm: LL(1) Table Construction (KTE — 100% Board-Exam Mark Yielding)**
>
> For every production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ of the grammar:
>
> **Step 1:** For each terminal $a$ in $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, a]$.
>
> **Step 2:** If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then for each terminal $b$ in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, b]$.
>
> **Step 3:** If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, \$]$.
>
> If a table cell receives **more than one production**, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)**.

---

## 2.4 The Two Types of LL(1) Conflicts

| Conflict Type | Formal Condition | Intuitive Meaning | Example Trigger |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **FIRST / FIRST Conflict** | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha) \cap \text{FIRST}(\beta) \neq \emptyset$ for two productions $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$ | The parser sees the same lookahead, but two productions both "claim" it | Common prefixes (e.g., `if-then` vs `if-then-else`) |
| **FIRST / FOLLOW Conflict** | A production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \epsilon$ shares terminals between FIRST($\alpha$) and FOLLOW of another production | Empty-string production collides with another choice | Left recursion + $\epsilon$-productions |

> [!WARNING]
> If **either conflict** appears in the table, the grammar is **not LL(1)** and you must **left-factor** or **eliminate left recursion** first.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Definition | Used In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals that begin strings derived from $\alpha$ | Rule for table cell $M[A, a]$ |
| $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals that can immediately follow $A$ | Filling cells when $A \rightarrow \epsilon$ |
| $\$ | End-of-input marker; always in $\text{FOLLOW}(\text{Start Symbol})$ | Base case of FOLLOW |
| $\epsilon$ | Empty string; added to FIRST if a production derives nothing | Trigger for FOLLOW-based rule |
| $M[A, a] = A \rightarrow \alpha$ | Parsing table entry: non-terminal $A$ with lookahead $a$ uses production $\alpha$ | Predictive parsing algorithm |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

LL(1) parsing is the **theoretical foundation** behind virtually every real-world compiler front-end, including:

- **GCC** (GNU Compiler Collection) and **Clang/LLVM** use variants of LL/LR parsing for languages like C, C++, Rust.
- **JavaCC, ANTLR, YACC/Bison** all generate parsers whose correctness depends on the **LL(1) / LALR(1) table-construction principles** taught in this module.
- **IDE syntax highlighters** and **static analyzers** use parser tables to detect malformed code in real-time.

Understanding LL(1) is the gateway to understanding **all table-driven parsing** in computer science.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: A Complete LL(1) Table Construction

We will now walk through a **complete, end-to-end KTU-style problem**. Consider the following grammar for arithmetic expressions (already left-factored and left-recursion-free):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow T\,E' \\
E' &\rightarrow +\,T\,E' \;\mid\; \epsilon \\
T &\rightarrow F\,T' \\
T' &\rightarrow *\,F\,T' \;\mid\; \epsilon \\
F &\rightarrow (\,E\,) \;\mid\; \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

The terminal symbols are: $\Sigma = \{\,+\,,\ *\,,\ (\,,\ )\,,\ \text{id},\ \epsilon,\ \$\,\}$.
The non-terminal symbols are: $V_N = \{\,E,\ E',\,T,\,T',\,F\,\}$.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Computation of FIRST Sets

We apply the 4 rules iteratively.

**Step 1 — Apply to non-terminal $E$ (Rule 3):**

$$
\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) \quad \text{(since } E \rightarrow T\,E'\text{)}
$$

This is a recursive call; we need FIRST of $T$ first. Move to $T$.

**Step 2 — Apply to $T$ (Rule 3):**

$$
\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) \quad \text{(since } T \rightarrow F\,T'\text{)}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to $F$ (Rule 3):**

$F$ has two productions: $F \rightarrow (\,E\,)$ and $F \rightarrow \text{id}$. Both right-hand sides begin with a **terminal** (Rule 1):

$$
\text{FIRST}(F) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}
$$

**Step 4 — Back-propagate to $T$ and $E$:**

$$
\text{FIRST}(T) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\} \qquad \text{FIRST}(E) = \{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to $E'$ (Rule 3 + Rule 2):**

$E'$ has two productions: $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ and $E' \rightarrow \epsilon$.

- The first production begins with terminal `+` $\rightarrow$ add $\{+\}$ to FIRST($E'$).
- The second production is $\epsilon$ $\rightarrow$ add $\epsilon$ to FIRST($E'$).

$$
\text{FIRST}(E') = \{\,+\,,\ \epsilon\,\}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply to $T'$ (analogous reasoning):**

$T'$ has productions $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$ and $T' \rightarrow \epsilon$:

$$
\text{FIRST}(T') = \{\,*\,,\ \epsilon\,\}
$$

### Final FIRST Set Table

| Non-Terminal | FIRST Set |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | $\{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{\,+\,,\ \epsilon\,\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{\,*\,,\ \epsilon\,\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{\,(\,,\ \text{id}\,\}$ |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Computation of FOLLOW Sets

**Step 1 — Base case (Rule 1):**

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\,\$\,\}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Rule 2 & 3 to production $E \rightarrow T\,E'$:**

Here $A = E$, $B = E'$, $\beta$ is empty. So we add FOLLOW($E$) to FOLLOW($E'$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\,\$\,\}
$$

We also consider $T$ in this production. $T$ is followed by $E'$. Since $E'$ is not empty, we add FIRST($E'$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\} = \{+\}$ to FOLLOW($T$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\,+\,\}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Rule 2 to production $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$:**

Inside this production, $T$ is followed by $E'$. Add FIRST($E'$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\} = \{+\}$ to FOLLOW($T$).

Also, $E'$ (the inner one) is at the end of the RHS, so we add FOLLOW($E'$) to FOLLOW($E'$) — no change.

No update needed for $T$ beyond $\{+\}$.

**Step 4 — Apply Rule 2 to $T \rightarrow F\,T'$:**

$F$ is followed by $T'$. Add FIRST($T'$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\} = \{*\}$ to FOLLOW($F$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\,*\,\}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Rule 2 to $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$:**

$F$ is followed by $T'$. Add FIRST($T'$) $\setminus \{\epsilon\} = \{*\}$ to FOLLOW($F$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\,*\,\} \quad \text{(already present)}
$$

**Step 6 — Propagate FOLLOW($T$) back to $T'$ and $F$ (Rule 3, $T' / F$ at the end):**

$T'$ is at the end of production $T \rightarrow F\,T'$, so add FOLLOW($T$) to FOLLOW($T'$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\,+\,\}
$$

Similarly, for $F \rightarrow (E)$, $E$ is followed by `)`. Add `)` to FOLLOW($E$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\,\$\,,\ )\,\}
$$

**Step 7 — Cascade the new FOLLOW($E$) back to $E'$:**

$E'$ inherits FOLLOW($E$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\,\$\,,\ )\,\}
$$

**Step 8 — Cascade FOLLOW($E'$) back through $E' \rightarrow + T E'$:**

The inner $E'$ is at the end; we add FOLLOW($E'$) to itself — no change. But the $T$ in this production is followed by the inner $E'$. Since FIRST($E'$) contains $\epsilon$, we also add FOLLOW($E'$) to FOLLOW($T$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{\,+\,,\ \$\,,\ )\,\}
$$

**Step 9 — Cascade FOLLOW($T$) back to $T'$ and $F$:**

$T'$ is at the end of $T \rightarrow F T'$ $\rightarrow$ add FOLLOW($T$) to FOLLOW($T'$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(T') = \{\,+\,,\ \$\,,\ )\,\}
$$

$F$ is at the start of $T \rightarrow F T'$: it is followed by $T'$. Since FIRST($T'$) contains $\epsilon$, we add FOLLOW($T$) to FOLLOW($F$):

$$
\text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{\,*\,,\ +\,,\ \$\,,\ )\,\}
$$

### Final FOLLOW Set Table

| Non-Terminal | FOLLOW Set |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E$ | $\{\,\$\,,\ )\,\}$ |
| $E'$ | $\{\,\$\,,\ )\,\}$ |
| $T$ | $\{\,+\,,\ \$\,,\ )\,\}$ |
| $T'$ | $\{\,+\,,\ \$\,,\ )\,\}$ |
| $F$ | $\{\,*\,,\ +\,,\ \$\,,\ )\,\}$ |

---

### 3.4 Construction of the LL(1) Parsing Table

For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$, we apply the 3-step table-filling rule.

**Production 1: $E \rightarrow T\,E'$**

FIRST($T\,E'$) = FIRST($T$) = $\{(,\ \text{id}\}$. So:

$$
M[E,\ (] = E \rightarrow T\,E' \qquad M[E,\ \text{id}] = E \rightarrow T\,E'
$$

**Production 2: $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$**

FIRST($+T\,E'$) = $\{+\}$. So:

$$
M[E',\,+] = E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'
$$

**Production 3: $E' \rightarrow \epsilon$**

$\epsilon \in$ FIRST($\epsilon$), so we look at FOLLOW($E'$) = $\{\$,\ )\}$:

$$
M[E',\,\$] = E' \rightarrow \epsilon \qquad M[E',\,)] = E' \rightarrow \epsilon
$$

**Production 4: $T \rightarrow F\,T'$**

FIRST($F\,T'$) = FIRST($F$) = $\{(,\ \text{id}\}$. So:

$$
M[T,\ (] = T \rightarrow F\,T' \qquad M[T,\ \text{id}] = T \rightarrow F\,T'
$$

**Production 5: $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$**

FIRST($*\,F\,T'$) = $\{*\}$. So:

$$
M[T',\,*] = T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'
$$

**Production 6: $T' \rightarrow \epsilon$**

FOLLOW($T'$) = $\{+,\ \$\,,\ )\}$. So:

$$
M[T',\,+] = T' \rightarrow \epsilon \quad M[T',\,\$] = T' \rightarrow \epsilon \quad M[T',\,)] = T' \rightarrow \epsilon
$$

**Production 7: $F \rightarrow (\,E\,)$**

FIRST($(E)$) = $\{( \}$. So:

$$
M[F,\ (] = F \rightarrow (\,E\,)
$$

**Production 8: $F \rightarrow \text{id}$**

FIRST($\text{id}$) = $\{\text{id}\}$. So:

$$
M[F,\ \text{id}] = F \rightarrow \text{id}
$$

### Final LL(1) Parsing Table

|  | `+` | `*` | `(` | `)` | `id` | `$` |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **E** |  |  | $E \rightarrow T\,E'$ |  | $E \rightarrow T\,E'$ |  |
| **E'** | $E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$ |  |  | $E' \rightarrow \epsilon$ |  | $E' \rightarrow \epsilon$ |
| **T** |  |  | $T \rightarrow F\,T'$ |  | $T \rightarrow F\,T'$ |  |
| **T'** | $T' \rightarrow \epsilon$ | $T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T'$ |  | $T' \rightarrow \epsilon$ |  | $T' \rightarrow \epsilon$ |
| **F** |  |  | $F \rightarrow (\,E\,)$ |  | $F \rightarrow \text{id}$ |  |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examine the table:** Every cell has **at most one entry**. Therefore, the grammar is confirmed to be **LL(1)**. There are **no FIRST/FIRST or FIRST/FOLLOW conflicts**.

---

## 3.5 Predictive Parsing Algorithm (Stack-Based Simulation)

The predictive parser uses an **input buffer** (with `$` at the end) and a **stack** (initially containing `$E$, where $E$ is the start symbol, $E$ on top).

> **Algorithm Steps:**
> 1. Let $X$ = top of stack, $a$ = current input symbol.
> 2. If $X = a = \$$, **parsing is successful** — accept.
> 3. If $X = a \neq \$$, **pop** $X$ and **advance** input pointer.
> 4. If $X$ is a non-terminal, consult table $M[X, a]$:
>    - If $M[X, a] = X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \cdots Y_k$, **pop** $X$ and **push** $Y_k \cdots Y_2 Y_1$ (rightmost first).
>    - If $M[X, a] = \text{error}$, invoke the **error recovery routine**.
>    - If $M[X, a] = X \rightarrow \epsilon$, simply **pop** $X$ (no push).

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LL(1) Predictive Parser — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Buffer] --> B{Input Pointer}
    B --> C[Current Input Symbol a]
    D[Stack] --> E{Stack Top X}
    E --> F{X equals a ?}
    F -- Yes and both are dollar --> G[ACCEPT and HALT]
    F -- Yes but X not dollar --> H[Pop X and Advance Pointer]
    F -- No, X is nonterminal --> I[Look up MX,a in Parsing Table]
    I -- MX,a equals X to Y1 Y2 Yk --> J[Pop X, Push Yk down to Y1]
    I -- MX,a equals X to epsilon --> K[Pop X only]
    I -- MX,a is empty --> L[Error Recovery Routine]
    H --> M[Loop Back to Top]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    C --> F
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The parser maintains a tight loop — it inspects the **top of the stack** ($X$) and the **current input symbol** ($a$) in lockstep. A table lookup $M[X, a]$ resolves every non-terminal-to-terminal transition deterministically, with **no backtracking**.

---

## 4.2 LL(1) Grammar — Construction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Original Context Free Grammar] --> S2{Is it Left Recursive?}
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Eliminate Left Recursion]
    S2 -- No --> S4{Does it have Common Prefixes?}
    S4 -- Yes --> S5[Apply Left Factoring]
    S4 -- No --> S6[Compute FIRST sets of all symbols]
    S3 --> S4
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Compute FOLLOW sets of all non terminals]
    S7 --> S8[Build LL1 Parsing Table MX,a]
    S8 --> S9{Any Multiply Defined Cell?}
    S9 -- Yes --> S10[Grammar is NOT LL1, Revisit]
    S9 -- No --> S11[Grammar is LL1 — Parser Constructed]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A grammar is **not automatically** LL(1). It must be **preprocessed** (left recursion removal + left factoring) **before** the sets are even computed. Skipping this step is the most common reason students fail in KTU exams.

---

## 4.3 Stack Trace Simulation for Input `id + id * id $`

To see the parser in action, the following table summarizes each step. The **stack** column shows the stack contents from bottom to top, with the topmost symbol on the right.

| Step | Stack (bottom $\rightarrow$ top) | Input (remaining) | Action |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\$ E$ | `id + id * id $` | $M[E,\ \text{id}] = E \rightarrow T\,E'$, pop $E$, push $E',T$ |
| 2 | $\$ E' T$ | `id + id * id $` | $M[T,\ \text{id}] = T \rightarrow F\,T'$, pop $T$, push $T',F$ |
| 3 | $\$ E' T' F$ | `id + id * id $` | $M[F,\ \text{id}] = F \rightarrow \text{id}$, pop $F$, push $\text{id}$ |
| 4 | $\$ E' T' \text{id}$ | `id + id * id $` | Match $\text{id}$ with $\text{id}$, pop, advance |
| 5 | $\$ E' T'$ | `+ id * id $` | $M[T',\,+] = T' \rightarrow \epsilon$, pop $T'$ |
| 6 | $\$ E'$ | `+ id * id $` | $M[E',\,+] = E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E'$, pop $E'$, push $E',T,+$ |
| 7 | $\$ E' T +$ | `+ id * id $` | Match `+`, pop, advance |
| 8 | $\$ E' T$ | `id * id $` | … (continues analogously) |
| … | … | … | … |
| 13 | $\$ E' T' F$ | `id $` | $M[F,\ \text{id}] = F \rightarrow \text{id}$ |
| 14 | $\$ E' T' \text{id}$ | `id $` | Match |
| 15 | $\$ E' T'$ | `$` | $M[T',\,\$] = T' \rightarrow \epsilon$ |
| 16 | $\$ E'$ | `$` | $M[E',\,\$] = E' \rightarrow \epsilon$ |
| 17 | $\$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** |

> **The parser never backtracks.** Every choice is made with **one symbol of lookahead**, exactly as the LL(1) framework demands.
