**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is an activation record? List any four fields typically stored in it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **activation record (AR)** — also called a **stack frame** — is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the run-time stack for each invocation of a procedure. It stores all information required to:
1. Execute the procedure correctly, and
2. Restore the state of the caller upon return.

**Four essential fields:**
1. **Return Address** — address of the instruction in the caller to resume after return.
2. **Dynamic Link (Control Link)** — pointer to the activation record of the caller.
3. **Static Link (Access Link)** — pointer to the AR of the lexically enclosing procedure (for nested scopes).
4. **Local Variables** — storage for variables declared in the procedure body.

*(Other valid fields: parameters, return value, saved registers, temporaries.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Sample Paper]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **three storage allocation strategies** (static, stack, heap) with diagrams. Discuss the situations in which each is suitable. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design the **activation record** for the following C-like procedure. Compute the total size and the offset of each local from the frame pointer. Assume `int = 4 bytes`, `char = 1 byte`, `pointer = 8 bytes`, and bookkeeping $K = 20$ bytes. **(7 marks)**

```c
int compute(int a, int b, char *msg) {
    int x, y;
    char buffer[20];
    long *ptr;
    // ... temporaries = 8 bytes
}
```

### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

**Static Allocation:**
- All bindings are fixed at compile time.
- Layout: code, static data, no stack/heap.
- **Suitable for:** embedded systems, simple languages (FORTRAN I), boot loaders.
- **Limitation:** no recursion, no dynamic structures.

**Stack Allocation:**
- AR is pushed on every `CALL` and popped on every `RET`.
- Stack pointer (SP) and frame pointer (FP) track the top.
- **Suitable for:** most modern languages, recursive procedures, automatic storage class.
- **Limitation:** locals cannot outlive the function.

**Heap Allocation:**
- Pool of free memory, allocated in arbitrary order.
- **Suitable for:** linked lists, trees, OOP objects, dynamic arrays.
- **Limitation:** fragmentation, need for GC or manual `free`.

*(Diagram of memory layout — refer to Section 4.1.)*

### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Parameter Space:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{params} &= \text{sizeof}(a) + \text{sizeof}(b) + \text{sizeof}(msg) \\
&= 4 + 4 + 8 = 16 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Local Variable Space:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{locals} &= \text{sizeof}(x) + \text{sizeof}(y) + \text{sizeof}(buffer) + \text{sizeof}(ptr) \\
&= 4 + 4 + 20 + 8 = 36 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Frame Size:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{frame} &= K + S_{params} + S_{locals} + T_{temp} \\
&= 20 + 16 + 36 + 8 = 80 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Offsets from FP (with bookkeeping above FP):**

| Variable | Offset from FP | Size (bytes) |
|---|---|---|
| `a` (param) | $-12$ | 4 |
| `b` (param) | $-8$ | 4 |
| `msg` (param) | $-16$ | 8 |
| `x` (local) | $+0$ | 4 |
| `y` (local) | $+4$ | 4 |
| `buffer[20]` | $+8$ | 20 |
| `ptr` | $+28$ | 8 |
| Temporaries | $+36$ | 8 |

*(Sign convention: parameters at negative offsets below FP, locals and bookkeeping at non-negative offsets above FP — KTU standard.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**(a)** What is **scope** in a programming language? Differentiate between **static (lexical) scope** and **dynamic scope** with a clear example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following Pascal-like nested program. Show the **static link chain** at the moment when procedure `Q` is executing, and compute the address of variable `y` declared in `P` from Q's frame, given that each level adds 1 to depth and `y` is at offset `+12` from the FP of `P`. **(7 marks)**

```pascal
program Main;
var x: integer;
procedure P;
  var y: integer;
  procedure Q;
    var z: integer;
    begin
      { Q is executing here }
    end;
  begin
    Q;
  end;
begin
  P;
end.
```

### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Scope** is the textual region of a program where a particular name (variable, function, type) is valid and refers to the same binding.

- **Static (Lexical) Scope:** The scope is determined by the **physical layout of the program text**. The compiler resolves a name by searching outward through enclosing blocks until the declaration is found. *Example: Pascal, C, Java, Python.*
- **Dynamic Scope:** The scope is determined by the **run-time call stack**. A name refers to the most recent active binding at execution time. *Example: early Lisp, shell variables in some shells, Emacs Lisp.*

**Example (Pascal-style):**

```pascal
program M;
var a: integer;
procedure P;
  var a: integer;  (* shadows outer a *)
  begin
    print(a);  (* Lexical: prints inner a = 10 *)
               (* Dynamic: prints main's a = 5 *)
  end;
begin
  a := 5; P;
end.
```

### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Determine depth of each procedure:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
d(\text{Main}) &= 0 \\
d(P) &= d(\text{Main}) + 1 = 1 \\
d(Q) &= d(P) + 1 = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Static link chain during Q's execution:**
- Q's `SL` → P's AR
- P's `SL` → Main's AR
- Main's `SL` → NULL

**Step 3 — Depth difference to reach `y` in P:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta d &= d(Q) - d(P) = 2 - 1 = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

So we follow the static link **once** from Q's FP to reach P's AR.

**Step 4 — Address of `y` from Q's frame:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{addr}(y) &= \text{FollowSL}^{1}(\text{FP}_Q) + 12 \\
&= \text{FP}_P + 12
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Confusing dynamic link with static link** — many students use the dynamic (caller) link to find `y`. This is **wrong** when the lexical parent differs from the caller. KTU specifically tests this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the direction of stack growth** — offsets for parameters are *negative* relative to FP; locals and bookkeeping are *positive*. Mixing the sign costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring alignment padding** — KTU may award partial credit if you mention that the ABI may insert padding bytes to align 8-byte types.
> 4. **Skipping the temporaries in frame size** — even if the problem doesn't list them, mention that the compiler reserves space for expression-evaluation temporaries.
> 5. **Not drawing a memory diagram** — for 7-mark questions, a labeled diagram of the AR or memory layout is **mandatory**; it typically fetches 1–2 free marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Three memory regions** of a process: **Code (text)**, **Static Data**, **Stack ↓ / Heap ↑**.
- [ ] **Static allocation** = compile-time, fixed addresses, **no recursion**.
- [ ] **Stack allocation** = `CALL` pushes AR, `RET` pops it; **FP** anchors the frame.
- [ ] **Heap allocation** = arbitrary-order pool for dynamic data; needs **GC or manual free**.
- [ ] **Activation Record** contains: **return address, dynamic link, static link, return value, saved regs, locals, temporaries**.
- [ ] **Parameters** live at **negative offsets** from FP; **locals** at **non-negative offsets**.
- [ ] **Frame size formula:** $S = K + \sum \text{params} + \sum \text{locals} + T_{temp}$.
- [ ] **Static (lexical) scope** = text-based; **dynamic scope** = call-stack-based.
- [ ] **Static link** points to the **lexical parent's AR**; depth difference $\Delta d$ equals the number of hops.
- [ ] **Stack grows down** (high → low), **Heap grows up** (low → high) — a **standard KTU convention**.
- [ ] **Recursion** requires **stack allocation**; **dynamic data structures** require **heap allocation**.
- [ ] **Parameter passing**: call-by-value (copy), call-by-reference (address), call-by-name (thunk), call-by-value-result (copy-in/copy-out).
- [ ] **ABI** = Application Binary Interface — defines the exact AR layout for a given architecture.
- [ ] **Garbage Collection** (mark-sweep, mark-compact, generational) reclaims unreachable heap objects.
- [ ] **KTU-typical question pattern:** "Draw the activation record and compute the offset of variable X" — practice this drill.
- [ ] **Watch out for:** shadowing of variables in nested scopes, aliasing in call-by-reference, dangling pointers in heap.

> **End of Module 3 — Storage Organization Notes** (PEERT634, KTU 2024 Scheme)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage-Allocation Strategies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storage-Allocation Strategies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Storage-Allocation Strategies — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of compiler design, **Storage-Allocation Strategies** refer to the systematic methodology employed by the compiler's run-time environment to **bind (assign) memory locations to program objects** such as variables, parameters, temporaries, and intermediate results throughout the execution lifetime of a program. The compiler must decide *when*, *where*, and *how long* each data object resides in memory.

The three canonical strategies recognized in the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 3)** are:

| Strategy | Lifetime Binding | Storage Region |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Allocation** | Compile-time / Fixed | Fixed memory region in the executable |
| **Stack Allocation** | Activation-based (LIFO) | Stack / Stack-allocated frames |
| **Heap Allocation** | Explicit / Arbitrary | Free-store (heap) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** The focus is on the *run-time storage organization*, *activation records*, *activation trees*, *calling sequences*, and the **Display Table mechanism** for non-local data access. Mastery of these topics guarantees marks in Part A (3-markers) and the structural part of Part B (14-markers).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are managing a **large corporate office building** and you need to assign workspaces to employees:

- **Static Allocation** is like giving an employee a **permanent, embossed nameplate on a fixed office door**. From the day the building opens until it is demolished, that office is theirs. The address is known even before the employee arrives — and the compiler knows it before the program even runs.

- **Stack Allocation** is like the **hot-desking system** in a coworking space. When an employee (function call) begins work, they check in at the front desk and are assigned a temporary desk. When they leave (function returns), the desk is automatically reclaimed by the next person who checks in. The desks are arranged in a *Last-In-First-Out* tower — the most recent arrival sits on top of the pile.

- **Heap Allocation** is like **renting a flexible warehouse unit on demand**. You walk in, ask the warehouse manager for a unit of any size, use it for as long as you wish, and explicitly return it when done. The size and duration are unpredictable, so a flexible allocator is needed.

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Constants

- The **activation record size** for a procedure is determined at **compile time** (for fixed-size locals) and is typically a multiple of the machine word size (commonly **4 bytes** or **8 bytes**).
- The **maximum nesting depth** of the activation tree governs the maximum number of activation records simultaneously resident in the stack.
- A **pointer** to an activation record is typically **1 word (4 bytes on 32-bit, 8 bytes on 64-bit)**.

> [!NOTE]
> The compiler is *not* responsible for allocating memory at run-time — that is the job of the **operating system loader** for the static region and the **run-time support library** (often called the *run-time system* or *executor*) for the stack and heap. The compiler, however, *generates* the code and metadata that make this allocation correct.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Allocation Strategies in Depth

### 2.1.1 Static Allocation

- **When:** Bindings are fixed at *compile time*; the address of every data object is known before execution begins.
- **What goes here:** Global variables, static local variables, constants, the executable code itself, fixed-size tables.
- **Limitations:** No recursion allowed; no dynamic data structures whose size depends on input.
- **Implementation:** All addresses are *absolute* or *relative to a fixed base register* (e.g., the code segment base).

### 2.1.2 Stack Allocation

- **When:** A new procedure is *called*, an activation record (or *stack frame*) is pushed onto the run-time stack. When the procedure *returns*, the frame is popped.
- **What goes here:** Local variables, parameters, return addresses, temporaries, saved registers, the *control link* and *access link*.
- **Why a stack?** Procedure calls and returns obey a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** discipline, mirroring the call stack. A stack data structure is the natural implementation.
- **Requirement:** The *size* of the activation record must be computable at *compile time* (or be a fixed upper bound). This is why C local arrays of variable length used to be forbidden (in C99 they are allowed with `alloca`).

### 2.1.3 Heap Allocation

- **When:** Memory is requested and released in *arbitrary* order (e.g., `malloc` / `free` in C, `new` / `delete` in C++, `new` in Java with GC).
- **What goes here:** Dynamic data structures (linked lists, trees, graphs), objects whose lifetime survives the procedure that created them.
- **Why needed?** Some objects must outlive the procedure that allocated them (e.g., a node created in one function and returned to the caller). The LIFO discipline of the stack cannot support this.
- **Drawback:** Slower allocation and deallocation; prone to fragmentation and dangling pointers.

## 2.2 Activation Trees and Activation Records

### 2.2.1 Activation Tree

The dynamic behaviour of a program can be modelled as a tree called the **activation tree**:

- Each **node** represents an activation of a procedure.
- The **root** is the activation of `main`.
- A node $a$ is the **parent** of node $b$ if the control flow is *in* the activation of $a$ when the call to $b$ is made.
- The activation of a procedure is *unique* — a node for a procedure can appear multiple times only if the procedure is recursive.

### 2.2.2 Activation Record (Stack Frame)

For every procedure call, the run-time system creates an **activation record** containing the following fields (order may vary by compiler, but the logical content is universal):

1. **Returned value** — space for the value the procedure returns.
2. **Actual parameters** — values or addresses passed by the caller.
3. **Optional control link (dynamic link)** — pointer to the activation record of the caller.
4. **Optional access link (static link)** — pointer to the activation record of the *enclosing static scope* (for non-local access).
5. **Saved machine status** — saved program counter, registers, condition codes.
6. **Local data** — local variables, temporaries.
7. **Temporaries** — scratch storage for intermediate expression evaluation.

## 2.3 Calling Sequence and Return Sequence

The **calling sequence** is the code executed at *entry* to a procedure:

- Allocates the activation record on the stack.
- Stores the actual parameters into the new frame.
- Stores the return address and saved registers.
- Updates the **stack pointer (SP)** and **frame pointer (FP)**.

The **return sequence** is the mirror process on exit:

- Restores saved registers and machine state.
- Stores the return value.
- Pops the activation record by resetting SP and FP.
- Transfers control back to the caller via the saved return address.

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, the division of work between *caller* and *callee* for each task in the calling sequence is a frequently tested concept. The standard split is: **caller evaluates the actual parameters; callee allocates the frame, saves machine status, and updates local data; caller uses the return value after the callee returns.**

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stack Pointer movement on call | $SP_{new} = SP_{old} - \text{sizeof}(\text{AR})$ | Stack grows *downward* on most architectures (e.g., x86, ARM). |
| 2 | Activation Record offset | $\text{offset}(x) = \text{addr}(x) - \text{addr}(\text{AR}_{\text{base}})$ | All field accesses are computed as a fixed offset from the FP. |
| 3 | Display Table size | $\vert D \vert = d_{max}$ | Number of entries equals the **maximum static nesting depth** of the program. |
| 4 | Non-local access cost (Display) | $O(1)$ | Display[depth] is fetched in one memory reference. |
| 5 | Non-local access cost (access link) | $O(d)$ | Walk $d_{scope} - d_{caller}$ links along the static chain. |
| 6 | Total memory per call (static) | $M_{static} = \sum_{i} \text{sizeof}(v_i)$ | Sum of all global/static variable sizes — fixed. |
| 7 | Total memory per call (stack) | $M_{stack} = n_{calls} \times \text{sizeof}(\text{AR}_{avg})$ | $n_{calls}$ = peak number of simultaneously live activations. |
| 8 | Frame Pointer | $FP = SP + \text{sizeof}(\text{header})$ | FP points just above the saved PC / control link area. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Offset of a Local Variable Inside an Activation Record

The compiler computes the **byte offset** of every local variable, parameter, and temporary from the base of the activation record. This offset is a *compile-time constant* and is encoded directly in the emitted machine instructions.

**Problem:** Given the following activation-record layout for procedure `P`, compute the offset of `local_var_2`.

| Field | Size (bytes) |
|---|---|
| Return value | 4 |
| Parameter 1 (passed by value) | 4 |
| Parameter 2 (passed by reference) | 4 |
| Control link (saved FP) | 4 |
| Saved return address (PC) | 4 |
| Saved register `rbx` | 4 |
| Local variable `local_var_1` (int) | 4 |
| Temporary `t1` (int) | 4 |
| Local variable `local_var_2` (double) | 8 |

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We compute cumulative offsets starting from offset $= 0$ at the base of the activation record. Since the stack grows downward, fields are typically stored at *positive* offsets from the frame pointer (FP), but the logical ordering is the same.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{offset}(\text{return value}) &= 0 \\
\text{offset}(\text{param}_1) &= 0 + 4 = 4 \\
\text{offset}(\text{param}_2) &= 4 + 4 = 8 \\
\text{offset}(\text{control link}) &= 8 + 4 = 12 \\
\text{offset}(\text{PC}) &= 12 + 4 = 16 \\
\text{offset}(\text{saved } rbx) &= 16 + 4 = 20 \\
\text{offset}(\text{local\_var}_1) &= 20 + 4 = 24 \\
\text{offset}(t_1) &= 24 + 4 = 28 \\
\text{offset}(\text{local\_var}_2) &= 28 + 4 = 32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The instruction that loads `local_var_2` will be encoded as `movsd xmm0, [FP + 32]` (on x86-64, where FP = RBP).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Display Table — Non-Local Access in $O(1)$

Consider the following static nesting of procedures with maximum depth $d_{max} = 3$:

```
procedure Main
    procedure A
        procedure A1
            ... accesses x declared in Main
```

When `A1` is executing, the **Display Table** $D$ is an array maintained by the run-time system such that:

$$
D[i] = \text{pointer to the activation record of the most recent activation of the procedure at static depth } i
$$

**Derivation of the access path to a non-local `x` declared in `Main` from within `A1`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{depth}(A1) &= 3 \\
\text{depth}(Main) &= 1 \\
\text{index into display} &= \text{depth of declaration of } x = 1 \\
\text{access path} &= D[1] + \text{offset}(x \text{ in Main's AR}) \\
\text{addresses} &= D[1] \rightarrow \text{AR of Main} \rightarrow \text{local}_x
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **one indirection** (one pointer dereference plus one offset) regardless of how deep the nesting goes — hence the celebrated $O(1)$ cost.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Activation Record Size with Padding

The compiler must often **align** fields to natural word boundaries for performance. Suppose we have:

- `char c` (1 byte)
- `int i` (4 bytes)
- `char d` (1 byte)
- `double x` (8 bytes)

On a 4-byte-aligned architecture with 8-byte alignment for `double`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{offset}(c) &= 0, \quad \text{size used} = 1 \\
\text{offset}(i) &= 4, \quad \text{(3 bytes padding after } c\text{)} \\
\text{offset}(d) &= 8, \quad \text{size used} = 1 \\
\text{offset}(x) &= 16, \quad \text{(7 bytes padding after } d\text{ for 8-byte alignment)} \\
\text{Total AR size} &= 16 + 8 = 24 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical explanation:** The compiler inserts *padding bytes* to ensure each scalar is aligned to its natural boundary. Misaligned accesses on some architectures (older ARM, SPARC) cause hardware traps; on x86 they merely slow down. The total stack consumption is therefore `24 bytes` per call, not `14 bytes`.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Allocation Strategies

| Property | Static | Stack | Heap |
|---|---|---|---|
| Binding time | Compile | Call / Return | `malloc` / `free` |
| Access cost | $O(1)$ direct | $O(1)$ via FP + offset | $O(1)$ via pointer (extra indirection) |
| Supports recursion? | No | Yes | Yes |
| Lifetime | Entire program | Activation lifetime | Explicit programmer-controlled |
| Allocation cost | $0$ (pre-allocated) | $O(1)$ pointer bump | Variable, may be $O(n)$ |
| Fragmentation | None | None | Possible (internal & external) |
| Suitable for | Globals, constants | Locals, parameters | Dynamic data structures |
| Garbage collection needed? | No | No (LIFO frees it) | Yes (if language is managed) |
| KTU weight (typical) | 1–2 marks | 6–8 marks | 2–3 marks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Layout of a Running Program (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LOW["LOW ADDRESSES"]
        code["CODE / TEXT SEGMENT\n(static, read-only)"]
        data["STATIC / DATA SEGMENT\n(global vars, static locals, constants)"]
    end
    subgraph MID["MIDDLE ADDRESSES (free)"]
        heap["HEAP\n(grows upward ↑)\nmalloc, new, dynamic objects"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH ADDRESSES"]
        stack["STACK\n(grows downward ↓)\nactivation records, return addresses"]
    end
    env["ENV / RUNTIME SUPPORT\n(command-line args, env vars,\nstack overflow guard pages)"]
    LOW --> MID --> HIGH
    style code fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style data fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style heap fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#b45f06
    style stack fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style env fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00
```

## 4.2 Activation Tree for a Recursive Call Sequence

```mermaid
graph TD
    main["main()"] --> f1["fact(3)"]
    f1 --> f2["fact(2)"]
    f2 --> f3["fact(1)"]
    f3 --> f4["fact(0) → returns 1"]
    f4 -.return.-> f3
    f3 -.return.-> f2
    f2 -.return.-> f1
    f1 -.return.-> main
    style main fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style f1 fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style f2 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style f3 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style f4 fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Detailed Activation Record Layout (Stack Frame Anatomy)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AR["ACTIVATION RECORD for procedure P (grows downward)"]
        direction TB
        retVal["Return value (4 bytes)"]
        paramA["Actual Parameter 1 (4 bytes)"]
        paramB["Actual Parameter 2 (4 bytes)"]
        ctrlLink["Control Link / Dynamic Link\n→ caller's AR (4 bytes)"]
        accLink["Access Link / Static Link\n→ enclosing scope's AR (4 bytes)"]
        savedPC["Saved PC / Return Address (4 bytes)"]
        savedReg["Saved Registers (rbx, r12..r15) (variable)"]
        locals["Local Variables (compile-time fixed)"]
        temps["Temporaries for expression eval (variable)"]
        retVal --> paramA --> paramB --> ctrlLink --> accLink --> savedPC --> savedReg --> locals --> temps
    end
    fp["FRAME POINTER (FP / RBP) points here"] -.-> ctrlLink
    sp["STACK POINTER (SP) points here"] -.-> temps
    style fp fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00
    style sp fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11
    style ctrlLink fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style accLink fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style retVal fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
```

## 4.4 Display Table Mechanism (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DT["DISPLAY TABLE D  (size = max nesting depth d_max)"]
        D0["D[0] → AR of depth-0 proc (main)"]
        D1["D[1] → AR of depth-1 proc (A)"]
        D2["D[2] → AR of depth-2 proc (A1)"]
        D3["D[3] → AR of depth-3 proc (A1x)"]
    end
    procA1x["Currently executing A1x\nwants to access x declared in Main"]
    procA1x -- "depth of x = 1" --> D1
    D1 -- "fetch via D[1]" --> ARmain["AR of Main\nlocal x at offset k"]
    ARmain -- "return value of x" --> procA1x
    style procA1x fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style D0 fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style D1 fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#b45f06
    style D2 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style D3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf8f00
    style ARmain fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Division of Work in a Calling Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CALLER["CALLER (caller's code)"]
        c1["1. Evaluate actual parameters"]
        c2["2. Store return address on stack"]
        c3["3. Jump to callee"]
        c4["4. After return: retrieve return value"]
    end
    subgraph CALLEE["CALLEE (callee's prologue)"]
        p1["1. Push old FP → control link"]
        p2["2. Set FP = SP"]
        p3["3. Save callee-saved registers"]
        p4["4. Update display if needed"]
        p5["5. Reserve space for locals/temps"]
    end
    subgraph RETURN["RETURN (callee's epilogue)"]
        r1["1. Store return value in agreed location"]
        r2["2. Restore saved registers"]
        r3["3. Restore FP from control link"]
        r4["4. Pop frame, return to caller"]
    end
    CALLER --> CALLEE --> RETURN --> CALLER
    style CALLER fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f4e79
    style CALLEE fill:#e2efda,stroke:#548235
    style RETURN fill:#fde9d9,stroke:#b45f06
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between static, stack, and heap storage allocation strategies. State one limitation of each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Static Allocation:** Memory is bound to objects at *compile time*. All data objects have fixed absolute addresses for the entire program duration. *Limitation:* It does not support recursion — the compiler cannot generate code for a procedure that calls itself an unknown number of times, because the local variables of concurrent activations would clash.

- **Stack Allocation:** Memory is bound to objects for the duration of an *activation* of a procedure; activation records are pushed/popped on a run-time stack in LIFO order. *Limitation:* It cannot support data structures whose lifetime exceeds that of the creating procedure (e.g., a node allocated inside one function and returned to the caller), because the stack frame is reclaimed on return.

- **Heap Allocation:** Memory is allocated and deallocated in *arbitrary* order on demand (`malloc`/`free` in C, `new` in Java). *Limitation:* Allocation is more expensive and may suffer from *fragmentation* (both internal and external), and manual deallocation can lead to memory leaks and dangling pointers.

*(Full credit requires naming the binding time for each, one example of what is stored in each region, and one clear limitation each. 1 mark each for the three parts — definition, example, limitation.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is an activation record? List any four fields stored in it and explain the purpose of the control link.**

**Model Answer:**

An **activation record (AR)** is a contiguous block of memory allocated on the run-time stack for each invocation of a procedure. It holds all the information necessary to execute that activation and to return control to the caller upon completion.

**Four fields** (any four from the list below):
1. **Return value** — the value passed back to the caller.
2. **Actual parameters** — arguments supplied by the caller.
3. **Control link (dynamic link)** — pointer to the AR of the caller.
4. **Access link (static link)** — pointer to the AR of the enclosing static scope.
5. **Saved machine status** — saved PC and registers.
6. **Local data** — local variables and temporaries.

**Purpose of the control link:** The control link points to the activation record of the *caller* (i.e., the most recent activation that is still live and invoked the current procedure). It is used in two ways:
- To **restore the frame pointer (FP)** of the caller during the return sequence, so that the caller's locals are once again addressable.
- To **debug, trace, or print the call stack** (e.g., for runtime error messages or back-traces).

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 3 carries ONE 14-mark question (with sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks). The student is given an internal choice between two alternatives. The following are two completely independent alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the run-time storage organization with a neat block diagram showing the code, static, stack, and heap segments. How does each segment grow during program execution? (7 marks)

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **Display Table** mechanism for accessing non-local data. Show how the time complexity is $O(1)$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

The run-time memory of a typical compiled program is organized into four logical regions, shown in the block diagram below.

| Segment | Contents | Direction of Growth |
|---|---|---|
| **Code (Text)** | Compiled machine instructions, read-only constants | Fixed at load time |
| **Static / Data** | Global variables, static local variables, string literals, jump tables | Fixed at load time |
| **Heap** | Dynamically allocated objects (`malloc`, `new`) | Grows **upward** toward higher addresses |
| **Stack** | Activation records, return addresses, saved registers | Grows **downward** toward lower addresses |

The two free regions (stack and heap) are placed at opposite ends of the address space with the entire unused area between them, so that neither overflows the other under normal use. As the program runs, **the stack pointer decreases** with every procedure call (frame pushed) and increases on every return (frame popped). **The heap pointer increases** with every successful allocation. The OS typically places *guard pages* near each end to detect overflow.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **Display Table** is an array $D[0 \ldots d_{max}-1]$ maintained by the run-time system, where $d_{max}$ is the maximum static nesting depth of any procedure in the program. When a procedure at depth $k$ is executing, $D[k]$ points to the activation record of the most recent activation at that depth.

**Example:** Consider the following Pascal-like nesting:

```
procedure Main;          (* depth 0 *)
    var x : integer;
    procedure A;          (* depth 1 *)
        procedure B;      (* depth 2 *)
            begin
                ... use x ...   (* x is non-local, declared in Main *)
            end;
    end;
end;
```

When `B` is executing, the display is:

$$
D = [\; \text{AR}(\text{Main}),\; \text{AR}(A),\; \text{AR}(B)\;]
$$

To access `x` declared in `Main`:
- The compiler knows at compile time that `x` is at *static depth 0* (in `Main`).
- At run time, it does a **single indirection**: fetch $D[0]$, then add the *fixed compile-time offset* of `x` within `Main`'s AR.

$$
\text{addr}(x) = D[0] + \text{offset}(x \text{ in AR of Main})
$$

This is **one memory access** through a pointer plus one constant-offset add — independent of the static depth gap between `B` and `Main`. Hence the complexity is $O(1)$, in contrast to the access-link approach which would require $d$ pointer traversals.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **control link** (points to the *caller* — dynamic) with **access link** (points to the *enclosing static scope* — lexical). The control link walks the *call stack*; the access link walks the *static scope chain*.
> 2. In the Display question, students forget to state that the display must be **updated on every procedure call and return** — a common follow-up sub-part. The compiler emits code to save the old $D[k]$ and store the new AR pointer in $D[k]$ on entry, and restore the old value on exit.
> 3. In the memory-layout question, students often omit that the **code segment is read-only** and **write-protect it**. Mentioning this earns an extra mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is an activation tree? Construct the activation tree for a program where `main` calls `P`, `P` calls `Q` recursively twice, and `Q` calls `R`. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the division of work between the **caller** and the **callee** in a typical calling sequence. State why the division is balanced the way it is. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

An **activation tree** is a representation of the dynamic calling behaviour of a program during execution. Each node of the tree represents a single activation of a procedure. The root corresponds to the activation of `main`. A node $a$ is the parent of node $b$ in the tree if the control flow is in the activation of $a$ at the moment the call to $b$ is initiated.

**Properties of the activation tree:**
- A new node is added every time a procedure is *called*.
- A node is removed (its activation ends) when the procedure *returns*.
- A procedure can appear in multiple nodes (recursion) but each node represents a unique, distinct activation.

**Construction of the activation tree for the given call sequence:**

Let the call sequence be: `main → P → Q → Q → R` (where the second `Q` is the recursive call, and `R` is called from within `Q`).

```mermaid
graph TD
    M["main()"] --> P["P()"]
    P --> Q1["Q() — first call"]
    Q1 --> Q2["Q() — recursive call"]
    Q2 --> R["R()"]
    style M fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#2e75b6,color:#ffffff
    style Q1 fill:#5b9bd5,color:#ffffff
    style Q2 fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000000
    style R fill:#bdd7ee,color:#000000
```

**Tree-walk (depth-first):** main → P → Q₁ → Q₂ → R → returns → Q₂ returns → Q₁ returns → P returns → main returns.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **calling sequence** is the sequence of instructions executed at the boundary between the caller and the callee. The work is divided so that *neither* side does too much, and so that the callee is re-entrant. A typical split is:

**Done by the CALLER (before the call):**
1. Evaluate the actual parameters and place them where the callee can find them (in registers or pushed on the stack in a fixed order).
2. Store the return address (the address of the instruction after the call) in a known location.
3. Save any caller-saved registers whose values must survive the call.

**Done by the CALLEE (in the prologue):**
1. Push the old frame pointer (FP) onto the stack — this is the **control link**.
2. Set the new FP = current SP.
3. Save the callee-saved registers that the procedure will use.
4. Reserve space on the stack for local variables and temporaries by decrementing SP.
5. Update the display table entry for the current static depth (if a Display is being used).

**Done by the CALLEE (in the epilogue, on return):**
1. Place the return value in the agreed register or memory location.
2. Restore the callee-saved registers.
3. Restore the old FP from the control link.
4. Reset SP to FP (or above the return address).
5. Restore the display table entry (if needed).
6. Execute the return instruction (which uses the saved PC).

**Done by the CALLER (after the call):**
1. Retrieve the return value from the agreed location.
2. Pop the actual parameters from the stack (in caller-cleanup conventions like stdcall; in cdecl, the caller cleans up).

**Why this division?**
- **Parameter evaluation** is the caller's job because parameter expressions are written in the *caller's* scope and may themselves involve function calls whose results must be valid before control transfers.
- **Frame allocation and local storage** is the callee's job because only the callee knows the *size and layout* of its own locals.
- **Register saving** is split based on calling-convention: *caller-saved* registers (used to pass arguments) are saved by the caller; *callee-saved* registers (preserved across calls) are saved by the callee.
- **Return value placement** is the callee's job (it is computing the value); *retrieval* is the caller's.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Question B):**
> 1. **Tree vs. syntax tree confusion:** The activation tree is built from *run-time calls*, not from the static call graph. A procedure that calls itself twice will have **two distinct nodes** for the two activations — do not merge them.
> 2. **Direction of growth:** In the tree diagram, the root is at the top and leaves are at the bottom. The depth-first traversal corresponds to the order in which activation records are pushed onto the stack.
> 3. **Calling-sequence split:** A common error is to say that the *caller* allocates the frame. The frame is allocated by the *callee*. The caller prepares the *parameters* and the *return address*; the callee builds the *frame*.
> 4. **Display update:** Students often forget the display update entirely. Loss of marks is guaranteed unless it is explicitly mentioned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Storage Allocation Strategies**

- **Three strategies:** Static (compile-time, fixed), Stack (activation-based, LIFO), Heap (explicit, arbitrary lifetime).
- **Activation record** = return value + parameters + control link + access link + saved state + locals + temporaries.
- **Activation tree** captures the *dynamic* call structure; a node per *invocation*, not per *procedure*.
- **Control link (dynamic link)** points to the *caller's* AR → restores FP on return.
- **Access link (static link)** points to the AR of the *enclosing static scope* → used for non-local access via scope chain.
- **Display table** is a global array of size $d_{max}$ giving $O(1)$ non-local access at the cost of update overhead on every call/return.
- **Calling sequence split:** Caller evaluates parameters & saves caller-saved registers; callee allocates the frame, saves callee-saved registers, updates the display.
- **Stack grows down, heap grows up** in nearly all modern architectures; the unused middle is the dynamic growth zone.
- **Static allocation forbids recursion**; **stack allocation cannot outlive the activation**; **heap allocation can leak and fragment**.
- **Offset computation** in an AR is a compile-time constant: $\text{offset} = \sum_{i<k} \text{size}(\text{field}_i)$, with padding inserted for alignment.
- **Frame pointer (FP/EBP/RBP)** points to a fixed location in the AR, allowing all locals/params to be accessed as `[FP + offset]`.
- **Maximum nesting depth** of the static scope chain determines the display size and the worst-case cost of access-link traversal.
- **Guard pages** placed by the OS near the stack and heap ends detect overflows (segmentation faults, `SIGSEGV`).
- **For KTU Part A:** Memorize the three-way table (binding time, contents, limitation).
- **For KTU Part B:** Always draw a *neat labelled diagram* — activation tree, AR layout, or display mechanism — examiners reward visual clarity generously.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Intermediate Code Generation - Intermediate Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Intermediate Code Generation - Intermediate Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intermediate Code Generation (ICG)** is the phase of a compiler that translates the annotated **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** produced during semantic analysis into a machine-independent, low-level representation of the source program. This intermediate representation (IR) sits strategically between the **front-end** (analysis) and the **back-end** (synthesis) of the compiler, enabling **retargetability** — the same front-end can be reused for multiple target architectures.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEERT634), the canonical intermediate languages covered under Module 4 are:

1. **High-Level Intermediate Code** (HIR) — closer to source language constructs.
2. **Low-Level Intermediate Code** (LIR) — closer to target machine code.
3. **Three-Address Code (TAC)** — the industry-standard IR used in GCC, LLVM, and most academic compilers.

### Definition Box — KTU Board Pattern

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Address Code (TAC)** is an intermediate language in which each instruction contains **at most three operands**, typically of the form:
> $$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$
> where `x`, `y`, `z` are variables, constants, or **temporaries** generated by the compiler. At most **one operator** appears on the right-hand side, which is why it is "three-address" — one address for the destination and up to two for the operands.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **Intermediate Code Generation** like translating a recipe written in French into a **standardized list of instructions** (like an IKEA-style manual) before converting it into the final product.

| Stage | Analogy | Compiler Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| **Source Program** | French recipe | High-level language (C, Java) |
| **Intermediate Language** | Universal IKEA-style steps | TAC, Postfix, Bytecode |
| **Target Code** | Specific instructions for an Indian kitchen | x86, ARM, MIPS machine code |

### Why do we need an Intermediate Language?

> [!NOTE]
> **Without IR** → We need $m \times n$ translators (m sources × n targets).
> **With IR** → We need only $m + n$ translators (m front-ends + n back-ends), all meeting at a common IR hub.

This is the foundational concept behind **modular compiler architecture** and **LLVM's success**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Maximum operands per TAC instruction:** **3** (one destination + two sources).
- **Maximum operators per TAC instruction:** **1** (fundamental property of SSA-style IR).
- **Temporary variable naming convention:** `t1, t2, t3, …` (compiler-generated, distinct from user variables).
- **Label naming convention:** `L1, L2, L3, …` for jump targets.
- **Typical IR file extensions:** `.ll` (LLVM), `.bc` (LLVM bitcode), `.tac` (academic).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Examiners frequently test the **properties** of TAC and the **translation schemes** that produce TAC from AST nodes. Memorize the exact template for each AST construct.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Intermediate Languages

Intermediate languages are classified along **two axes**:

### Axis 1: Level of Abstraction

| Level | Examples | Distance from Source | Distance from Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High-Level IR (HIR)** | AST, Source-to-source | Close | Far |
| **Medium-Level IR (MIR)** | Three-Address Code, SSA | Medium | Medium |
| **Low-Level IR (LIR)** | Register Transfer Language (RTL) | Far | Close |

### Axis 2: Structural Representation

| Structural Form | Description | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **Postfix Notation** | Operator follows operands; stack-machine friendly | High |
| **Syntax Tree / DAG** | Graphical IR with shared subexpressions | High |
| **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Linearized pseudo-assembly | Very High |
| **Quadruples** | Record-based form of TAC | Very High |
| **Triples** | Position-based form of TAC | High |
| **Indirect Triples** | Pointer-based form of Triples | Moderate |
| **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** | Each variable assigned exactly once | Moderate |
| **Bytecode** | Stack-based binary IR (Java, Python) | Moderate |

---

## 2.2 The Big Four: TAC Quadruple Forms

### 2.2.1 Three-Address Code (TAC)

Each statement obeys the canonical form:
$$x = y \;\text{op}\; z$$

where `x` is the result, and `y`, `z` are operands. Allowed extended forms include:

| TAC Form | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | Binary operation |
| $x = \text{op}\; y$ | Unary operation |
| $x = y$ | Simple copy |
| $\text{goto}\; L$ | Unconditional jump |
| $\text{if}\; x \;\text{relop}\; y \;\text{goto}\; L$ | Conditional jump |
| $\text{ifFalse}\; x \;\text{goto}\; L$ | Conditional jump on false |
| $\text{param}\; x$, $\text{call}\; p, n$, $\text{return}\; y$ | Procedure linkage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Three-Address"?** The name comes from early architectures with **3-address instruction formats** (e.g., MIPS R-type: `op rd, rs, rt`). In TAC, each instruction references at most **3 memory addresses** (destination + 2 sources).

---

### 2.2.2 Quadruples (Records with 4 Fields)

A **quadruple** is a 4-tuple structure: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`

**Definition:** The quadruple representation of TAC uses one record per instruction, with explicit fields for operator, two operands, and destination.

$$\text{Quadruple} = \big\langle \text{op},\; \text{arg}_1,\; \text{arg}_2,\; \text{result} \big\rangle$$

**Example:** For `t1 = a + b * c` → TAC first decomposes into:
```
t1 = b * c
t2 = a + t1
```
Quadruple table:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c | t1 |
| (1) | `+` | a | t1 | t2 |

**Note:** Unary operations leave `arg2` blank; copy operations leave `arg2` blank; parameter passing uses `param` op with result blank.

---

### 2.2.3 Triples (Position-Based, No Temporary Names)

A **triple** avoids compiler-generated temporaries by **referencing other instructions by position number**.

$$\text{Triple} = \big\langle \text{op},\; \text{arg}_1,\; \text{arg}_2 \big\rangle$$

The result is **implicit** — it is the position of the triple itself in the list.

**Same example** `t1 = a + b * c`:

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c |
| (1) | `+` | a | (0) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Trade-off:** Triples save space (no temporary names) but **reordering instructions invalidates references** — the position-based pointers break. This makes **code optimization difficult**.

---

### 2.2.4 Indirect Triples (Pointer List + Triple List)

To fix the reordering problem, an **indirection array** is added:

$$\text{IndirectTripleList} = \big[\; p_0, p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n \;\big]$$

Each `p_i` is a pointer to the `i`-th executable triple. Reordering is done by **swapping pointer entries**, not the actual triples — preserving all internal references.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Property | Constraint / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TAC statement form | $x = y \;\text{op}\; z$ | $\vert \text{operands} \vert \le 3$ |
| 2 | Quadruple tuple size | $(op, arg_1, arg_2, result)$ | 4 fields |
| 3 | Triple tuple size | $(op, arg_1, arg_2)$ | 3 fields; result = position |
| 4 | Temp variables for `a+b*c` | $\text{temp count} = n - 1$ | where $n$ = # of leaves |
| 5 | DAG node reduction ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{tree}}}{N_{\text{DAG}}}$ | $\eta \ge 1$ |
| 6 | Postfix length | $\vert P \vert = \vert T \vert + \vert \text{operands} \vert$ | $P$ = postfix string |
| 7 | SSA dominance frontier | $DF(N) = \big\{ M \mid N \in \text{idom}(M) \rightrightarrows \}$ | For $\phi$-function placement |
| 8 | Indirect triple indirection | Access cost = $O(1)$ via pointer | Space overhead $\approx n$ pointers |

---

## 2.4 Why These Representations Matter — Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **LLVM IR (a modern TAC variant)** is the backbone of Clang, Rust, Swift, and Julia compilers. **Quadruples** are used in GCC's GIMPLE representation. **Triples** were historically used in early optimizing compilers like Bliss-11. **Bytecode** powers JVM (Java), CLR (C#), and CPython.

The choice of IR representation directly affects:
- **Optimization effectiveness** (DAG enables common subexpression elimination).
- **Code generation complexity** (SSA simplifies register allocation via *Chaitin's algorithm*).
- **Compilation speed** (triples save space; quadruples allow easy reordering).

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Translation Scheme: AST Node → TAC

The standard KTU board question is to convert an expression into TAC. Let us derive the translation scheme for a binary expression `E → E1 op E2`.

### Translation Scheme Derivation

We define a **synthesized attribute** `E.place` — the name of the temporary/location holding the value of `E`.

**Production:** $E \rightarrow E_1 \;\text{op}\; E_2$

**Semantic Action:**

```
E.place = newtemp()
emit(E.place ' =' E1.place 'op' E2.place)
```

**Detailed derivation** with side-effect-free intermediate variable generation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Convert `a + b * c - d` to TAC

### Step 1: Apply operator precedence
The expression parses as: `(a + (b * c)) - d`

### Step 2: Build AST
```
         (-)
        /   \
      (+)    d
     /   \
    a    (*)
        /   \
       b     c
```

### Step 3: Post-order traversal to generate TAC

**Step 3a:** Visit left subtree of `(-)` → compute `a + (b*c)`:
- Visit subtree `b * c` first
- Visit leaf `a`
- Apply `+`

**Step 3b:** Visit right subtree of `(-)` → leaf `d`
- Apply `-`

### Step 4: Emit TAC statements

$$\text{Step A: } t_1 = b * c$$

The TAC statement for the deepest left operation `b * c`:

$$t_1 = b \ast c$$

This means: multiply `b` and `c`, store in new temporary `t1`.

$$\text{Step B: } t_2 = a + t_1$$

After computing `b*c`, we add `a`:

$$t_2 = a + t_1$$

The left operand `a` is a leaf; the right operand `t1` is the result of the previous TAC.

$$\text{Step C: } t_3 = t_2 - d$$

Finally, subtract `d` from the result of the addition:

$$t_3 = t_2 - d$$

### Step 5: Final TAC sequence

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= b \ast c \\
t_2 &= a + t_1 \\
t_3 &= t_2 - d
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the canonical **3-instruction TAC** for the expression.

---

## 3.3 Conversion of TAC → Quadruples, Triples, Indirect Triples

Using the same example `a + b * c - d`:

### 3.3.1 Quadruple Representation

For each TAC line, we create one quadruple record. The operator is placed in column 1, operands in columns 2 and 3, and the destination temporary in column 4.

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c | t1 |
| (1) | `+` | a | t1 | t2 |
| (2) | `-` | t2 | d | t3 |

**Rule application:**
- For instruction 0: `*` is the operator, `b` and `c` are the arguments, `t1` is the result.
- For instruction 1: `+` is the operator, `a` and `t1` are the arguments, `t2` is the result.
- For instruction 2: `-` is the operator, `t2` and `d` are the arguments, `t3` is the result.

### 3.3.2 Triple Representation

Triples eliminate the need for explicit temporary names by referring to the *position* of prior instructions. The result is implicit in the instruction's own position.

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c |
| (1) | `+` | a | (0) |
| (2) | `-` | (1) | d |

**Rule application:**
- Instruction 0 produces a value implicitly at position 0.
- Instruction 1 references position `(0)` to access the result of `b*c`.
- Instruction 2 references position `(1)` to access the result of `a + (b*c)`.

### 3.3.3 Indirect Triple Representation

An **instruction list** (pointer array) is added. The triples themselves are stored separately. Reordering instructions means swapping pointers, not rewriting triple contents.

**Instruction List (pointers):** `[0, 1, 2]`

**Triple Pool (same as above):**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `*` | b | c |
| (1) | `+` | a | (0) |
| (2) | `-` | (1) | d |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: Control Flow in TAC

### Source code:
```c
if (x < y)
    z = x + y;
else
    z = x - y;
```

### Step-by-step TAC generation:

**Step 1:** Evaluate the condition operands into temporaries (defensive translation):

$$t_1 = x$$

**Step 2:** Place temporaries for the right-hand side:

$$t_2 = y$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the relational operator using `if x relop y goto L`:

$$\text{if } t_1 < t_2 \text{ goto } L_1$$

**Step 4:** Translate the `else` branch:

$$t_3 = t_1 - t_2$$

**Step 5:** Jump over the `then` branch:

$$\text{goto } L_2$$

**Step 6:** Place the label for the `then` branch:

$$L_1: \; t_3 = t_1 + t_2$$

**Step 7:** Place the label after both branches:

$$L_2: \; z = t_3$$

### Final TAC:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= x \\
t_2 &= y \\
\text{if } t_1 < t_2 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
t_3 &= t_1 - t_2 \\
\text{goto } L_2 \\
L_1 &: t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
L_2 &: z = t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **intermediate temporaries** `t1`, `t2` for operands `x` and `y` before the comparison. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for direct use of `x < y` without decomposition.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — IR Representation Selection

| Criterion | Quadruples | Triples | Indirect Triples | Postfix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Space efficiency** | Low (needs names) | High | High | Medium |
| **Optimization ease** | High (reorderable) | Low (positions fixed) | High (pointer reorder) | Low |
| **Code motion** | Easy | Hard | Easy | Hard |
| **Implementation** | Simple array of structs | Simple array | Two parallel arrays | Stack-based |
| **Used by** | GCC, LLVM GIMPLE | Academic | Academic | Stack VMs (JVM) |
| **KTU exam frequency** | Very High | Very High | High | High |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Compiler Pipeline with IR Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Source Code"] --> lex["Lexical Analysis"]
    lex --> tokens["Tokens"]
    tokens --> parse["Syntax Analysis"]
    parse --> ast["Abstract Syntax Tree"]
    ast --> sem["Semantic Analysis"]
    sem --> annot["Annotated AST"]
    annot --> icg["Intermediate Code Generation"]
    icg --> ir["Intermediate Representation: TAC"]
    ir --> opt["Optimization"]
    opt --> optir["Optimized IR"]
    optir --> cg["Code Generation"]
    cg --> tgt["Target Machine Code"]
```

## 4.2 IR Representation Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph source["SOURCE LEVEL"]
        expr["Expression: a + b * c - d"]
    end
    subgraph tacform["TAC LINEAR FORM"]
        t1["t1 = b * c"]
        t2["t2 = a + t1"]
        t3["t3 = t2 - d"]
    end
    subgraph quadform["QUADRUPLE RECORDS"]
        q1["0: * b c t1"]
        q2["1: + a t1 t2"]
        q3["2: - t2 d t3"]
    end
    subgraph tripleform["TRIPLE POSITION FORM"]
        tr1["0: * b c"]
        tr2["1: + a 0"]
        tr3["2: - 1 d"]
    end
    source --> tacform
    tacform --> quadform
    tacform --> tripleform
```

## 4.3 DAG Representation for Common Subexpression Elimination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    n1["a"] --> n4["+"]
    n2["b"] --> n3["*"]
    n3 --> n4
    n4 --> n5["-"]
    n3b["c"] --> n3
    n6["d"] --> n5
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture:** The DAG identifies that the subexpression `b*c` is computed **once** and reused. The node `n3` (the `*` operator) has **two parents** (`+` and `-`), making it a shared subexpression.

## 4.4 Control Flow TAC with Labels

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["Start"] --> eval1["t1 = x"]
    eval1 --> eval2["t2 = y"]
    eval2 --> cond["if t1 < t2 goto L1"]
    cond -->|false| elseBody["t3 = t1 - t2"]
    elseBody --> jumpEnd["goto L2"]
    cond -->|true| thenBody["L1: t3 = t1 + t2"]
    jumpEnd --> merge["L2: z = t3"]
    thenBody --> merge
    merge --> finish["End"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Intermediate Code. List any four advantages of using an intermediate code in a compiler.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** Intermediate code is a machine-independent, low-level representation of the source program that lies between the high-level source language and the target machine code. Common forms include three-address code, postfix notation, and syntax trees.
>
> **Advantages (½ mark each, any four):**
> 1. **Retargetability:** Same front-end can generate code for multiple target machines.
> 2. **Modularity:** Separates analysis (front-end) from synthesis (back-end).
> 3. **Optimization:** Machine-independent optimizations are easier to perform on IR.
> 4. **Reusability:** Reuse optimization passes across different source languages.
> 5. **Portability:** Easier porting to new architectures.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is Three-Address Code? Give two examples.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (2 marks):** Three-Address Code (TAC) is an intermediate representation in which each instruction contains at most **three operands** (one destination + up to two sources) and at most **one operator**. The general form is: $x = y \; \text{op} \; z$, where `x`, `y`, `z` are variables, constants, or compiler-generated temporaries.
>
> **Examples (1 mark):**
> - `t1 = b * c`
> - `if x < y goto L1`

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Question A and Question B below are **fully independent alternatives**. Choose **ONE**.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full Module Choice A

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of intermediate representations (IR) used in compilers. Discuss in detail any **two** structural representations with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following C-like source code:
```c
if (a < b)
    x = a + b;
else
    x = a - b;
while (x > 0)
    x = x - 1;
```
Generate the **Three-Address Code**, **Quadruple table**, and **Triple table** for the above code. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of IRs (3 marks):**
> Intermediate representations are classified based on:
> 1. **Level of Abstraction:** High-Level IR (HIR), Medium-Level IR (MIR), Low-Level IR (LIR).
> 2. **Structural Form:** Postfix, Syntax Tree/DAG, TAC, Quadruples, Triples, Indirect Triples, SSA, Bytecode.
>
> **Detailed discussion of two representations (4 marks):**

**1. Three-Address Code (TAC):**
- Canonical form: $x = y \; \text{op} \; z$
- Maximum one operator per instruction
- Uses compiler-generated temporaries: `t1, t2, t3, …`
- Example for `a + b * c`:
  $$\begin{aligned} t_1 &= b * c \\ t_2 &= a + t_1 \end{aligned}$$

**2. Quadruples:**
- Record-based form: `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`
- Each instruction occupies one row in a 4-column table.
- Temporary names are explicit, allowing easy reordering and optimization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Other IRs (brief mention):**
> - **Postfix:** Stack-friendly; `a + b * c` → `a b c * +`
> - **DAG:** Detects common subexpressions.
> - **SSA:** Each variable assigned exactly once; uses $\phi$-functions.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: TAC Generation (4 marks)**

For the `if-else` block, generate temporaries for operands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a \\
t_2 &= b \\
\text{if } t_1 < t_2 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
t_3 &= t_1 - t_2 \\
\text{goto } L_2 \\
L_1 &: t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
L_2 &: t_4 = t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

For the `while` loop, start a new label:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_3 &: \text{if } t_4 \le 0 \text{ goto } L_4 \\
t_4 &= t_4 - 1 \\
\text{goto } L_3 \\
L_4 &: x = t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final TAC Sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a \\
t_2 &= b \\
\text{if } t_1 < t_2 \text{ goto } L_1 \\
t_3 &= t_1 - t_2 \\
\text{goto } L_2 \\
L_1 &: t_3 = t_1 + t_2 \\
L_2 &: t_4 = t_3 \\
L_3 &: \text{if } t_4 \le 0 \text{ goto } L_4 \\
t_4 &= t_4 - 1 \\
\text{goto } L_3 \\
L_4 &: x = t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Quadruple Table (2 marks)**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `=` | a | | t1 |
| (1) | `=` | b | | t2 |
| (2) | `<` | t1 | t2 | |
| (3) | `-` | t1 | t2 | t3 |
| (4) | `goto` | | | L2 |
| (5) | `+` | t1 | t2 | t3 |
| (6) | `=` | t3 | | t4 |
| (7) | `<=` | t4 | 0 | |
| (8) | `-` | t4 | 1 | t4 |
| (9) | `goto` | | | L3 |
| (10) | `=` | t4 | | x |

**Step 3: Triple Table (1 mark)**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | `=` | a | |
| (1) | `=` | b | |
| (2) | `<` | (0) | (1) |
| (3) | `-` | (0) | (1) |
| (4) | `goto` | | |
| (5) | `+` | (0) | (1) |
| (6) | `=` | (5) | |
| (7) | `<=` | (6) | 0 |
| (8) | `-` | (6) | 1 |
| (9) | `goto` | | |
| (10) | `=` | (6) | |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (3-marks deduction zone):**
> 1. **Forgetting operand temporaries** `t1`, `t2`: Examiners deduct **1 mark** for writing `if a < b goto L1` directly.
> 2. **Misplaced `goto L2`**: The unconditional jump after the `else` body is **mandatory** to skip the `then` body. Missing it → **½ mark cut**.
> 3. **Loop termination condition**: Using `<` instead of `<=` or vice-versa. Verify the source: `while (x > 0)` → continue when `> 0`, exit when `<= 0` → use `<= 0 goto L_end`.
> 4. **Quadruple vs Triple confusion**: Quadruples have **4 fields including `result`**. Triples have only **3 fields**, and the result is the row index. Examiners are strict about table dimensions.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Full Module Choice B

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Translation Scheme** for generating Three-Address Code from an expression. Apply this scheme to convert the expression `x = (a + b) * (c - d) + e` into TAC. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Quadruples, Triples, and Indirect Triples** as representations of Three-Address Code. Which representation is most suitable for **code optimization**? Justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

> [!NOTE]
> **Translation Scheme (3 marks):**
> For a binary expression production $E \rightarrow E_1 \; \text{op} \; E_2$, the synthesized attribute `E.place` is computed as:
>
> ```
> E.place = newtemp()
> emit(E.place ' =' E1.place 'op' E2.place)
> ```
> The `newtemp()` function generates a fresh temporary name (`t1, t2, …`), and `emit()` appends a TAC instruction to the output stream. For unary expressions, `arg2` is omitted.

**Step-by-step application (4 marks):**

**Expression:** `x = (a + b) * (c - d) + e`

**Parsed form:** $x = \big((a+b) \ast (c-d)\big) + e$

**Step 1:** Compute `a + b`:
$$t_1 = a + b$$

**Step 2:** Compute `c - d`:
$$t_2 = c - d$$

**Step 3:** Multiply the two results:
$$t_3 = t_1 \ast t_2$$

**Step 4:** Add `e`:
$$t_4 = t_3 + e$$

**Step 5:** Assign to `x`:
$$x = t_4$$

**Final TAC:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= a + b \\
t_2 &= c - d \\
t_3 &= t_1 \ast t_2 \\
t_4 &= t_3 + e \\
x &= t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Quadruples | Triples | Indirect Triples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | 4-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` | 3-tuple `(op, arg1, arg2)` | 3-tuple + pointer list |
| **Result field** | Explicit (named temp) | Implicit (position number) | Implicit (position number) |
| **Space overhead** | Higher (names stored) | Lower | Moderate (extra pointer array) |
| **Reordering** | Easy (rename temps) | Hard (positions change) | Easy (swap pointers) |
| **Optimization** | Excellent | Difficult | Excellent |
| **Used in** | GCC, modern compilers | Academic texts | Academic texts |

**Best for code optimization (2 marks):**

> Quadruples are most widely used for code optimization in practice (e.g., GCC's GIMPLE, LLVM IR), because they support **easy reordering, renaming, and common subexpression elimination**. Indirect Triples offer comparable flexibility but introduce an extra indirection layer, slightly increasing access overhead. Triples, while space-efficient, are **not preferred** because instruction reordering invalidates position-based references.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Exam:**
> 1. **Operator precedence error:** Writing `a + b * c - d` as `t1 = a+b; t2 = t1*c; t3 = t2-d` instead of the correct `t1 = b*c; t2 = a+t1; t3 = t2-d`. KTU strictly tests precedence.
> 2. **Triple result column confusion:** Students add a 4th column to the triple table — this is **wrong**. Triples have only 3 columns.
> 3. **Translation scheme not stated:** Forgetting to write `E.place = newtemp()` before the `emit` call. Examiners expect the **synthesized attribute** explicitly.
> 4. **Missing `newtemp` generation logic:** Students write `t1` directly without explaining that the compiler *generates* these names.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- [ ] **TAC canonical form:** $x = y \; \text{op} \; z$ — maximum 3 operands, 1 operator.
- [ ] **Quadruple:** `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` — 4 fields per row.
- [ ] **Triple:** `(op, arg1, arg2)` — 3 fields; result = row index.
- [ ] **Indirect Triple:** Triple pool + instruction pointer list.
- [ ] **Temporary naming:** Always use `newtemp()` in semantic actions; KTU expects explicit synthesized attributes.
- [ ] **Operator precedence:** `*` and `/` bind tighter than `+` and `-`. Always decompose bottom-up.
- [ ] **Control flow TAC:** Use `if x relop y goto L` and `goto L`. Always close `if-else` with a `goto` over the `then` branch.
- [ ] **While loop TAC:** Place test label at **start**, jump back to it after body.
- [ ] **DAG benefit:** Identifies common subexpressions — `b*c` computed once, reused.
- [ ] **SSA property:** Each variable assigned **exactly once** — uses $\phi$-functions at join points.
- [ ] **Why IR matters:** Enables retargetability — same front-end, multiple back-ends.
- [ ] **Industry examples:** LLVM IR (TAC variant), Java Bytecode (stack-based), GCC GIMPLE (quadruples).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Strategy Tip:** In the 14-mark questions, examiners typically allocate **7 marks** for the procedural derivation (translation scheme + TAC) and **7 marks** for the representation (quadruples/triples). Practice converting **at least 5 expressions and 3 control-flow snippets** to TAC before the exam. Always show the **parsed form** before emitting TAC — this earns you **methodology marks** even if a final line is wrong.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graphical Representations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Graphical Representations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In Compiler Design, **Graphical Representations** are intermediate data structures used to model the structure and flow of programs between the parsing phase and code generation. They act as a "visual blueprint" that exposes redundancy, control flow, and data dependencies, which can then be optimized before final code emission. The principal graphical structures covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are the **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**, the **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**, and the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** built over **Basic Blocks**.

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for Expressions**
> A DAG is a directed graph with no cycles, in which leaves represent identifiers or constants, interior nodes represent operators, and the children of an interior node are pointers to the operands. Unlike a syntax tree, a DAG identifies **common sub-expressions** by allowing nodes to have multiple parents, ensuring each distinct sub-expression is constructed only once.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)**
> A tree representation of the abstract syntactic structure of source code. Each interior node denotes an operator, and the children of the node represent the operands. A pure AST does **not** share nodes — every sub-expression is represented by a distinct subtree.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Basic Block (BB)**
> A maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions in which flow of control enters at the first instruction and leaves at the last without any halt or branch except possibly at the end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Flow Graph (CFG)**
> A directed graph $G = (N, E)$ whose nodes $N$ are the basic blocks of a program and whose edges $E$ represent possible transfer of control between blocks. A distinguished node `ENTRY` has no predecessors, and `EXIT` has no successors.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The City Map

Imagine you are a **delivery driver** in a city:
- The **AST** is your *first naive route sketch* where every shop and every fork in the road is drawn fresh — even if you pass the same roundabout five times, you draw it five times.
- The **DAG** is the *optimized route*: every roundabout is drawn once, and arrows from different paths converge onto it. Common detours are merged.
- The **Basic Blocks** are the *stops* in your itinerary, and the **CFG** is the *road network* connecting them with one-way arrows showing which stop comes after which.

This is precisely what a compiler does: it converts raw source code (a long list of instructions) into graphical structures that expose redundancy and flow, then optimizes the journey before generating machine code.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Symbols

- **V** = set of vertices (nodes) in the graph
- **E** = set of directed edges
- **In-degree** of a node $n$ = number of edges entering $n$
- **Out-degree** of a node $n$ = number of edges leaving $n$
- **|V|** = total vertex count
- **|E|** = total edge count
- Standard complexity bounds are **O(|V| + |E|)** for graph traversals.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Graphical Representations

| # | Representation | Purpose | Sharing Allowed? | Cycles Allowed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Parse Tree** | Show full syntactic derivation | No | No |
| 2 | **AST (Abstract Syntax Tree)** | Compact syntactic structure | No | No |
| 3 | **DAG** | Identify common sub-expressions | **Yes** (multi-parent) | **No** |
| 4 | **Basic Blocks** | Linear sequences of TAC | No | No |
| 5 | **CFG** | Inter-procedural control flow | Yes (multi-parent) | **Yes** |
| 6 | **Data Flow Graph** | Track variable defs and uses | Yes | No |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU's Module 4 weightage focuses primarily on **AST → DAG**, **DAG → Three Address Code (TAC)**, and **Code → Basic Blocks → CFG**. Expect one full 14-mark question on these topics every examination cycle.

## 2.2 Construction Logic — DAG from Three-Address Statements

For each statement of the form `x = y op z`, the standard KTU algorithm executes the following ordered steps:

1. **Lookup `y`** — If `y` is already defined to some node $N_y$, use it; otherwise create a leaf node labelled `y`.
2. **Lookup `z`** — If `z` is already defined to some node $N_z$, use it; otherwise create a leaf node labelled `z`.
3. **Check reuse** — Search existing nodes for a node labelled `op` whose left and right children are $N_y$ and $N_z$ (in that order).
4. **Decide** — If such a node exists, mark `x` as pointing to it. If not, **create a new interior node** labelled `op` with children $N_y$ and $N_z$, and bind `x` to it.
5. **Undefine killed names** — If the LHS `x` was previously defined, delete the prior definition of `x` from the symbol table (to honor the "killed" property of definitions).

## 2.3 Construction Logic — Basic Blocks from TAC

1. **Determine leaders** — The first statement of the program is a leader. The *target* of any conditional or unconditional jump is a leader. The statement *immediately following* any conditional/unconditional jump is a leader.
2. **Form blocks** — A block consists of a leader and all subsequent statements up to (but not including) the next leader.
3. **Build CFG edges** — Block $B_i$ has an edge to $B_j$ if control may transfer from the last statement of $B_i$ to the first statement of $B_j$ (i.e., $B_j$ is the jump target, or $B_j$ is the immediate successor and $B_i$ does not end in an unconditional jump).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Relation | Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| $G = (V, E)$ | Graph with vertices $V$ and edges $E$ | All representations |
| $\text{indeg}(v) \geq 2$ | A node is shared iff its in-degree is $\geq 2$ | DAG optimization |
| $\|E\|_{AST} = \|E\|_{DAG}$ for distinct sub-expr | Edge count comparison | AST vs DAG |
| $\text{Edges}_{DAG} < \text{Edges}_{AST}$ | Strict inequality when CSE exists | Optimization |
| $\|BB\| = n$ | Number of basic blocks in CFG | CFG construction |
| $\|E\|_{CFG} \le 2 \cdot \|BB\|$ | Bounded by conditional branches | CFG analysis |
| $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Traversal complexity (DFS/BFS) | Reachability, liveness |
| $O(n \cdot k)$ | DAG construction (n statements, k ops) | TAC → DAG |
| $T(n) = T(n-1) + T(n-2) + O(1)$ | Tree recursion recurrence (parsetree size) | Complexity analysis |
| $L_v = \bigcup_{(u,v) \in E} L_u \cup \text{use}(v)$ | Data flow: available expressions | Optional advanced |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar symbol $\vert$ above denotes *absolute value* / *cardinality* of a set. It is rendered as `\vert` in LaTeX so as not to break markdown table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Optimizers (GCC -O2, LLVM)** use DAGs to perform **Common Sub-expression Elimination (CSE)** and **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)**.
- **Static Analyzers (SonarQube, Coverity)** construct CFGs to detect unreachable code, infinite loops, and null-pointer dereferences.
- **Just-In-Time (JIT) Compilers** in the JVM and V8 engine hot-path rely on CFGs for branch prediction and inlining.
- **Reverse Engineers (Ghidra, IDA Pro)** reconstruct high-level flow from binaries using CFG visualization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constructing a DAG and Generating Three-Address Code

**Source expression:**
$$E = a + a \cdot (b - c) + (b - c) \cdot d$$

We will first produce the **three-address code (TAC)** and then derive the **DAG**.

### Step 1 — Write the TAC using temporaries

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &= b - c \\
t_2 &= a \cdot t_1 \\
t_3 &= a + t_2 \\
t_4 &= t_1 \cdot d \\
t_5 &= t_3 + t_4
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the DAG construction algorithm instruction by instruction

| TAC Statement | $N_y$ (left operand) | $N_z$ (right operand) | Existing `op` node with same children? | Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $t_1 = b - c$ | leaf `b` | leaf `c` | None | Create node $n_1$ (`-`); bind $t_1 \rightarrow n_1$ |
| $t_2 = a \cdot t_1$ | leaf `a` | $n_1$ | None | Create node $n_2$ (`*`); bind $t_2 \rightarrow n_2$ |
| $t_3 = a + t_2$ | leaf `a` | $n_2$ | None | Create node $n_3$ (`+`); bind $t_3 \rightarrow n_3$ |
| $t_4 = t_1 \cdot d$ | $n_1$ | leaf `d` | None | Create node $n_4$ (`*`); bind $t_4 \rightarrow n_4$ |
| $t_5 = t_3 + t_4$ | $n_3$ | $n_4$ | None | Create node $n_5$ (`+`); bind $t_5 \rightarrow n_5$ |

### Step 3 — Identify common sub-expressions

Notice that the sub-expression **$(b - c)$** is computed twice in the *naive* TAC (once as $t_1$ and again implicitly inside $t_4$). In the DAG, this sub-expression corresponds to the *single* node $n_1$, which is shared by two parents: $n_2$ and $n_4$.

The DAG has:
- 4 leaf nodes: $a, b, c, d$
- 5 interior nodes: $n_1(-), n_2(*), n_3(+), n_4(*), n_5(+)$
- Total = 9 nodes, but the same expression in AST form would require 13 nodes (every `b - c` duplicated).

### Step 4 — Re-generate optimized TAC by emitting code from the DAG in **post-order**

We introduce fresh temporaries $s_1, s_2, \ldots$ and walk the DAG bottom-up:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_1 &= b - c \quad \text{(emits } n_1 \text{)} \\
s_2 &= a \cdot s_1 \quad \text{(emits } n_2 \text{)} \\
s_3 &= a + s_2 \quad \text{(emits } n_3 \text{)} \\
s_4 &= s_1 \cdot d \quad \text{(emits } n_4 \text{ — reuses } s_1 \text{!)} \\
s_5 &= s_3 + s_4 \quad \text{(emits } n_5 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The reuse of $s_1$ in $s_4$ is the explicit visible gain of DAG-based code generation. The redundant `b - c` computation is **eliminated**, saving one subtraction at runtime.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Identifying Basic Blocks and Building a CFG

**Given three-address code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1:&\ t_1 = a + b \\
2:&\ t_2 = t_1 \cdot c \\
3:&\ \text{if } t_2 < 100 \text{ goto } 7 \\
4:&\ t_3 = t_2 + d \\
5:&\ t_4 = t_3 \cdot 2 \\
6:&\ \text{goto } 9 \\
7:&\ t_5 = t_2 - 10 \\
8:&\ t_6 = t_5 \cdot 5 \\
9:&\ t_7 = t_6 / 3 \\
10:&\ \text{return } t_7
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Determine the leaders (KTU standard rule)

A statement is a **leader** if:
- (L1) It is the **first statement** of the program, OR
- (L2) It is the **target** of a conditional or unconditional jump, OR
- (L3) It **immediately follows** a conditional or unconditional jump.

| Statement | Reason | Leader? |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | L1 (first statement) | ✅ |
| 2 | – | ❌ |
| 3 | Conditional jump | (counts as jump source) |
| 4 | L3 (follows jump at 3) | ✅ |
| 5 | – | ❌ |
| 6 | Unconditional jump | (jump source) |
| 7 | L2 (target of `goto 7` from stmt 3) | ✅ |
| 8 | – | ❌ |
| 9 | L3 (follows jump at 6) | ✅ |
| 10 | – | ❌ |

**Leaders: { 1, 4, 7, 9 }**

### Step 2 — Form the basic blocks

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1 &: \{1, 2, 3\} \\
B_2 &: \{4, 5, 6\} \\
B_3 &: \{7, 8\} \\
B_4 &: \{9, 10\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Establish CFG edges from the last statements of each block

- $B_1$ ends with `if t2 < 100 goto 7` → edges to $B_2$ (fall-through) and $B_3$ (jump target).
- $B_2$ ends with `goto 9` → edge to $B_4$ (jump target) only; no fall-through.
- $B_3$ ends with a non-jump statement → fall-through to $B_4$.
- $B_4$ ends with `return` → edge to `EXIT`.

### Step 4 — Edge list of the CFG

$$
E = \{ (B_1, B_2),\ (B_1, B_3),\ (B_2, B_4),\ (B_3, B_4),\ (B_4, \text{EXIT}) \}
$$

### Step 5 — Verify with adjacency

| Block | Successors |
|---|---|
| ENTRY | $B_1$ |
| $B_1$ | $B_2,\ B_3$ |
| $B_2$ | $B_4$ |
| $B_3$ | $B_4$ |
| $B_4$ | EXIT |

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU valuation error: students forget the *fall-through* edge from $B_1 \rightarrow B_2$. The conditional branch has **two** successors, not one. Always draw both arrows.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — DAG to TAC Algorithm (Formal Statement)

Given a DAG with node labels:

1. For each leaf whose parent has not yet been assigned a value, generate an assignment of the leaf's name to a fresh temporary.
2. Perform a **post-order traversal** of the DAG.
3. For each interior node $n$ with label `op`, children $L$, $R$, generate: `t = sL op sR`, where `sL` and `sR` are the names bound to $L$ and $R$ in earlier steps.
