# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a quadruple. List its four fields and explain the role of each field with one example.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **quadruple** is an intermediate-code representation of the form $(\text{op}, \text{arg}_1, \text{arg}_2, \text{result})$.
>
> 1. **`op`** — the operator (e.g., `+`, `<`, `jmp`).
> 2. **`arg₁`** — first operand (variable, constant, or temp). E.g., `b` in $(+, b, c, t_1)$.
> 3. **`arg₂`** — second operand (variable, constant, temp, or `—` for unary/assignment). E.g., `c` in $(+, b, c, t_1)$.
> 4. **`result`** — destination variable/temporary where the result is stored. E.g., `t₁` in $(+, b, c, t_1)$.
>
> For the assignment $a = b$, the quadruple is $(=, b, \text{—}, a)$ where $\text{arg}_2$ is blank.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Compare quadruples and triples. State one advantage of quadruples over triples.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Quadruple | Triple |
|---|---|---|
| Fields | 4 (`op, arg₁, arg₂, result`) | 3 (`op, arg₁, arg₂`) |
| Operand reference | By **name** (temporaries) | By **position number** |
| Optimisation | Easier — reordering safe | Harder — requires renumbering |

> **Advantage:** In quadruples, operands reference *named* temporaries, so instructions can be **moved or deleted** without renumbering dependent instructions — making peephole and local optimisations straightforward. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Recommended Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Generate **quadruples** for the following arithmetic expression and explain each step:
$$x = (a + b) \cdot (c - d) + e$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Generate **quadruples** for the following `if-else` construct and **back-patch** the jumps correctly:
$$\text{if } (p > q)\ \text{then}\ p = p - q;\ \text{else}\ q = q - p;$$
**(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Arithmetic Quadruples

**Step 1 — Parenthesize by precedence:** $x = ((a+b) \cdot (c-d)) + e$

**Step 2 — Compute $a+b$ → mint $t_1$:**

$$Q_0 = (\ +,\ \ a,\ \ b,\ \ t_1\ )$$

**Step 3 — Compute $c-d$ → mint $t_2$:**

$$Q_1 = (\ -,\ \ c,\ \ d,\ \ t_2\ )$$

**Step 4 — Compute $t_1 \cdot t_2$ → mint $t_3$:**

$$Q_2 = (\ *,\ \ t_1,\ \ t_2,\ \ t_3\ )$$

**Step 5 — Compute $t_3 + e$ → mint $t_4$:**

$$Q_3 = (\ +,\ \ t_3,\ \ e,\ \ t_4\ )$$

**Step 6 — Assign to `x`:**

$$Q_4 = (\ =,\ \ t_4,\ \ \text{—},\ \ x\ )$$

| # | op | arg₁ | arg₂ | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `+` | `a` | `b` | `t1` |
| 1 | `-` | `c` | `d` | `t2` |
| 2 | `*` | `t1` | `t2` | `t3` |
| 3 | `+` | `t3` | `e` | `t4` |
| 4 | `=` | `t4` | — | `x` |

**Number of temporaries = 4.** Number of instructions = 5.

---

#### Part (b) — If-Else Quadruples with Back-Patching

**Step 1 — Evaluate condition $p > q$.**

$$Q_0 = (\ >,\ \ p,\ \ q,\ \ t_0\ )$$

**Step 2 — If false, jump to else ($L_1$).**

$$Q_1 = (\ \text{jfalse},\ \ t_0,\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_1\ )$$

**Step 3 — Then branch: compute $p - q$.**

$$Q_2 = (\ -,\ \ p,\ \ q,\ \ t_1\ )$$

**Step 4 — Assign to `p`.**

$$Q_3 = (\ =,\ \ t_1,\ \ \text{—},\ \ p\ )$$

**Step 5 — Jump over else to end ($L_2$).**

$$Q_4 = (\ \text{jmp},\ \ \text{—},\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_2\ )$$

**Step 6 — Emit else label $L_1$.**

$$Q_5 = (\ \text{label},\ \ \text{—},\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_1\ )$$

**Step 7 — Else: compute $q - p$.**

$$Q_6 = (\ -,\ \ q,\ \ p,\ \ t_2\ )$$

**Step 8 — Assign to `q`.**

$$Q_7 = (\ =,\ \ t_2,\ \ \text{—},\ \ q\ )$$

**Step 9 — Emit end label $L_2$.**

$$Q_8 = (\ \text{label},\ \ \text{—},\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_2\ )$$

| # | op | arg₁ | arg₂ | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `>` | `p` | `q` | `t0` |
| 1 | `jfalse` | `t0` | — | `L1` |
| 2 | `-` | `p` | `q` | `t1` |
| 3 | `=` | `t1` | — | `p` |
| 4 | `jmp` | — | — | `L2` |
| 5 | `label` | — | — | `L1` |
| 6 | `-` | `q` | `p` | `t2` |
| 7 | `=` | `t2` | — | `q` |
| 8 | `label` | — | — | `L2` |

> **Back-patching note:** $L_1$ in $Q_1$ refers to the row containing `label L1` (here row 5), and $L_2$ in $Q_4$ refers to row 8. The compiler uses a **symbol table of labels** and a `makelist`/`backpatch` mechanism to resolve these.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Generate **quadruples** for the following `while` loop:
$$\text{while } (a < b)\ \text{do}\ a = a + 2;$$
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Generate **quadruples** for the following expression involving array access:
$$x = A[i \cdot 2] + B[i+1]$$
Explain how array address computation appears in the quadruple table.
**(7 marks)**

---

### Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — While Loop Quadruples

**Step 1 — Emit start label.**

$$Q_0 = (\ \text{label},\ \ \text{—},\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_{\text{start}}\ )$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate $a < b$.**

$$Q_1 = (\ <,\ \ a,\ \ b,\ \ t_0\ )$$

**Step 3 — Exit loop if false.**

$$Q_2 = (\ \text{jfalse},\ \ t_0,\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_{\text{end}}\ )$$

**Step 4 — Compute $a + 2$.**

$$Q_3 = (\ +,\ \ a,\ \ 2,\ \ t_1\ )$$

**Step 5 — Assign back to `a`.**

$$Q_4 = (\ =,\ \ t_1,\ \ \text{—},\ \ a\ )$$

**Step 6 — Jump back to start.**

$$Q_5 = (\ \text{jmp},\ \ \text{—},\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_{\text{start}}\ )$$

**Step 7 — End label.**

$$Q_6 = (\ \text{label},\ \ \text{—},\ \ \text{—},\ \ L_{\text{end}}\ )$$

| # | op | arg₁ | arg₂ | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `label` | — | — | `L_start` |
| 1 | `<` | `a` | `b` | `t0` |
| 2 | `jfalse` | `t0` | — | `L_end` |
| 3 | `+` | `a` | `2` | `t1` |
| 4 | `=` | `t1` | — | `a` |
| 5 | `jmp` | — | — | `L_start` |
| 6 | `label` | — | — | `L_end` |

---

#### Part (b) — Array Access Quadruples

**Expression:** $x = A[i \cdot 2] + B[i+1]$

**Step 1 — Compute index for $A$: $i \cdot 2$.**

$$Q_0 = (\ *,\ \ i,\ \ 2,\ \ t_1\ )$$

**Step 2 — Compute address of $A[i \cdot 2]$:** $\text{addr}_A + t_1 \cdot w$ (where $w$ is element width, omitted for clarity using abstract notation):

$$Q_1 = (\ +,\ \ \text{base}(A),\ \ t_1,\ \ t_2\ )$$

**Step 3 — Load value from that address into temp.**

$$Q_2 = (\ \text{=[]},\ \ t_2,\ \ \text{—},\ \ t_3\ )$$

**Step 4 — Compute $i + 1$.**

$$Q_3 = (\ +,\ \ i,\ \ 1,\ \ t_4\ )$$

**Step 5 — Compute address of $B[i+1]$.**

$$Q_4 = (\ +,\ \ \text{base}(B),\ \ t_4,\ \ t_5\ )$$

**Step 6 — Load value from that address.**

$$Q_5 = (\ \text{=[]},\ \ t_5,\ \ \text{—},\ \ t_6\ )$$

**Step 7 — Add the two loaded values.**

$$Q_6 = (\ +,\ \ t_3,\ \ t_6,\ \ t_7\ )$$

**Step 8 — Store into `x`.**

$$Q_7 = (\ =,\ \ t_7,\ \ \text{—},\ \ x\ )$$

| # | op | arg₁ | arg₂ | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `*` | `i` | `2` | `t1` |
| 1 | `+` | `base(A)` | `t1` | `t2` |
| 2 | `=[]` | `t2` | — | `t3` |
| 3 | `+` | `i` | `1` | `t4` |
| 4 | `+` | `base(B)` | `t4` | `t5` |
| 5 | `=[]` | `t5` | — | `t6` |
| 6 | `+` | `t3` | `t6` | `t7` |
| 7 | `=` | `t7` | — | `x` |

> **Explanation:** Array accesses in three-address code are decomposed into **two operations** each: (i) compute the effective memory address using `+` (and `*` for scaling by element width), and (ii) use `=[]` for *load* (read) or `[]=` for *store* (write). The compiler later emits a single `MOV` instruction per `=[]`/`[]=` after register allocation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `arg₂ = —` for unary/assignment ops.** Writing $(=, t_3, a, \text{—})$ instead of $(=, t_3, \text{—}, a)$ will lose **0.5–1 mark** per instance. The assignment target is always `result`, never `arg₂`.
> 2. **Skipping temporary generation.** Forgetting to mint `t_k` for each subexpression causes operand references to be illegal. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark per missing temp**.
> 3. **Wrong operator precedence order.** For `a + b * c`, students often emit `+` first. Always *parenthesize first*, then emit in left-to-right order respecting precedence.
> 4. **Back-patching jump targets.** For `if-else`, students forget the **unconditional jump at the end of the `then` branch** to skip the `else`. This causes semantic errors and loses **2–3 marks**.
> 5. **Not showing step-by-step work.** KTU 2024 follows **outcome-based education**. Each emitted quadruple must be justified with a one-line explanation, not just dumped as a table.
> 6. **Mixing `jtrue` and `jfalse`.** Stick to **one** convention throughout. KTU's preferred convention is `jfalse target` (jump to target if condition is false).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Quadruples**
>
> ✅ Quadruple = 4-tuple: **(op, arg₁, arg₂, result)**
>
> ✅ Always use **`—`** (dash) for `arg₂` in **unary** operations and **assignment** (`=`)
>
> ✅ For each **binary operator** in the expression, **mint one new temporary** `tₖ`
>
> ✅ Number of quadruples for a binary expression of $n$ operators = **$n + 1$** (including the final assignment)
>
> ✅ `jfalse cond target` jumps to `target` if `cond` is **false**; `jmp target` is unconditional
>
> ✅ `label L` is a no-op marker for jump targets
>
> ✅ Array access uses `=[]` (load) and `[]=` (store); address is computed first via `+`
>
> ✅ **Quadruples vs Triples:** Quadruples use **named** temporaries (optimisation-friendly), Triples use **positional** indices (fragile under reordering)
>
> ✅ Standard emission algorithm: `emit(op, arg1, arg2, result)` then `nextquad++`
>
> ✅ Always **parenthesize** the expression by precedence **before** emitting quadruples
>
> ✅ Control-flow structures (`if`, `while`, `for`) require **back-patching** of jump targets — emit labels and update jump `result` fields accordingly
>
> ✅ In KTU exams, **always show the working** — each quadruple should be justified with a one-line explanation of what subexpression it computes

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triples, Code Optimization - Principal Sources of Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Triples, Code Optimization - Principal Sources of Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 4 — Intermediate Representation & Code Optimization

## 1. Triples — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Triples** are a compact form of **Three-Address Code (TAC)** intermediate representation in which each instruction is stored as a record of **only three fields**: `op`, `arg1`, and `arg2`. Unlike **quadruples**, triples do *not* explicitly store a result name; instead, the result is implicitly referenced by the **position (index) of the triple that produced it**. This position-based referencing makes triples highly space-efficient, especially when many temporaries are used.

A triple record has the structure:

$$
\text{Triple}_i = \langle \text{op}, \text{arg}_1, \text{arg}_2 \rangle
$$

where:
- $\text{op}$ → operator symbol (e.g., `+`, `*`, `:=`, `goto`)
- $\text{arg}_1$ → first operand (either a name or a *pointer* to a previous triple)
- $\text{arg}_2$ → second operand (may be empty for unary/binary assignments)
- Result is *implicitly* at position $i$ (the index of this triple in the table).

### 1.2 Triples vs. Quadruples — The Key Distinction

| Feature | Quadruples | Triples |
|---|---|---|
| Fields per record | 4 (op, arg1, arg2, result) | 3 (op, arg1, arg2) |
| Result reference | Explicit named temporary $t_i$ | Implicit position pointer $(i)$ |
| Space efficiency | Lower (more names stored) | **Higher** (no temp names) |
| Reordering ease | Easier (reassign temps) | Harder (positions may shift) |
| Suitable for | Most compiler back-ends | Compact IR for analysis |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Step" Idea

> [!NOTE]
> 🍳 **Analogy — Cooking Recipe Without Naming the Bowls:**
> Imagine writing a recipe. In a **quadruple** representation, every time you mix something, you put it in a *named bowl* (e.g., "Bowl_3 = flour + sugar"). In a **triple** representation, you don't name the bowl — you simply refer to "**the bowl I used in step 4**" using its step number. This saves labels but makes the recipe **fragile** if you reorder the steps, because step numbers change. That's exactly the trade-off in triples.

### 1.4 Indirect Triples — Solving the Re-ordering Problem

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indirect Triples** introduce an **additional indirection layer** — a *listing array* (or *execution pointer list*) that holds pointers to the actual triples. Reordering is now performed by **swapping pointers in the list** without physically moving the triple records, preserving the original triple positions.

**Structure:**

$$
\text{Listing Array} \rightarrow [\text{ptr}_0, \text{ptr}_1, \dots, \text{ptr}_n] \rightarrow \text{Triples}
$$

- `Listing[i]` stores the index of the $i^{\text{th}}$ triple to be executed.
- The *triples table* is read-only during optimization; only the listing is shuffled.
- This mimics a **hardware instruction cache**: instruction *positions* stay fixed, but their *execution order* can change freely.

### 1.5 Why Triples Matter in KTU 2024

- Triples appear directly in **Module 4** (Intermediate Code Generation) of PEERT634.
- The KTU examiner frequently tests the **representation of arithmetic expressions** in triples form.
- Indirect triples are a *high-yield 7-mark question* topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Construction of Triples

The procedure for converting an expression to triples follows a **postfix-evaluation pattern** (similar to stack-based TAC generation):

**Steps:**
1. Parse the expression into a **postfix (Reverse Polish) form**.
2. Use a stack of operand descriptors (each is either a *name* or a *triple index*).
3. For each operator, pop two operands, emit a new triple, and push the new index back onto the stack.
4. For unary operators, pop one operand only.
5. For assignments (`:=`), treat LHS specially: emit `(op, LHS, RHS-triple-index)`.

### 2.2 Worked Example — Generating Triples

**Expression:** `a := b * -c + b * -c`

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU 2024 problem — the expression has a **redundant subexpression** that optimization must detect.

| Step | Token | Stack After | Triple Emitted |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `b` | `[b]` | — |
| 2 | `*` | `[b]` | — |
| 3 | `-` | `[b, c]` → after seeing `-` operator queued | — |
| 4 | `c` | `[b, c]` | — |
| 5 | (emit unary `-`) | `[b, (0)]` | `(0) uminus c, _` |
| 6 | `*` | `[b, (0)]` | — |
| 7 | emit `*` | `[(1)]` | `(1) * b, (0)` |
| 8 | `+` | `[(1)]` | — |
| 9 | `b` | `[(1), b]` | — |
| 10 | `*` | `[(1), b]` | — |
| 11 | `-` then `c` | `[(1), b, c]` | — |
| 12 | emit unary `-` | `[(1), b, (2)]` | `(2) uminus c, _` |
| 13 | emit `*` | `[(1), (3)]` | `(3) * b, (2)` |
| 14 | emit `+` | `[(4)]` | `(4) + (1), (3)` |
| 15 | `:=` then `a` | `[]` | `(5) := a, (4)` |

**Final Triple Table:**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | uminus | c | — |
| (1) | * | b | (0) |
| (2) | uminus | c | — |
| (3) | * | b | (2) |
| (4) | + | (1) | (3) |
| (5) | := | a | (4) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — IR & Optimization Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the table below as your **last-minute revision anchor** before the ESE.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Notation | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Triple record size | $S_T = 3w$ | $w$ = word size in bytes | Standard word = 4 bytes; $S_T = 12$ B |
| Quadruple record size | $S_Q = 4w$ | $w$ = word size | $S_Q = 16$ B |
| Space saved by triples | $\Delta S = w \cdot n$ | $n$ = number of triples | Each temporary name omitted |
| Indirect triple indirection | 1 extra pointer per stmt | bytes = $n \cdot w$ | Listing array overhead |
| Strength Reduction: $i \cdot k$ | replace with `add` chain | $k$ iterations of `+` | Reduces MUL → ADD |
| Induction variable test | $i' = i + c \;\Rightarrow\; i' = \text{arith. progression}$ | Linear in basic block | Eligible for elimination |
| Code motion savings | $T_{saved} = (n-1) \cdot C_{\text{inv}}$ | $n$ = loop iterations | $C_{\text{inv}}$ = invariant cost |
| Loop unroll factor | $k \in \mathbb{Z}^+, \; k \geq 1$ | dimensionless | Trades code size for speed |
| Peephole window size | $w \in [1, 6]$ instructions | dimensionless | Typical $w = 4$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Triples** are used in production compilers (e.g., **LLVM's SSA form**, **JVM bytecode**) as compact IRs because cache locality matters.
- **Indirect triples** are conceptually similar to **basic-block scheduling** in CPU instruction pipelines, where instruction *positions* are fixed but *issue order* changes.
- **Optimization passes** in GCC (`-O2`, `-O3`) directly apply *principal sources of optimization* in this module.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation — Space Complexity of Triples vs. Quadruples

Let $n$ = number of three-address statements, $w$ = word size in bytes.

**Quadruple total space:**

$$
S_Q^{\text{total}} = n \cdot S_Q = n \cdot 4w = 4nw
$$

**Triple total space:**

$$
S_T^{\text{total}} = n \cdot S_T = n \cdot 3w = 3nw
$$

**Space saved by triples:**

$$
\Delta S = S_Q^{\text{total}} - S_T^{\text{total}} = 4nw - 3nw = nw
$$

**Percentage saving:**

$$
\eta = \frac{\Delta S}{S_Q^{\text{total}}} \times 100\% = \frac{nw}{4nw} \times 100\% = 25\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> ✅ **Conclusion:** Triples save **25% of IR memory** compared with quadruples. This is why large compilers like GCC and the JVM (HotSpot C2) prefer compact IRs.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Principal Sources of Optimization

| # | Optimization | Class | Where Applied | Effect on IR | Equation / Rule |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** | Function-preserving | Local + Global | Removes redundant recomputation | If $x = \text{op}(y,z)$ computed twice with $y,z$ unchanged → reuse |
| 2 | **Copy Propagation** | Function-preserving | Global | Replaces copies with originals | `x := y; … use x` → replace with `y` |
| 3 | **Dead Code Elimination (DCE)** | Function-preserving | Global | Removes computations whose result is unused | If result never used → delete stmt |
| 4 | **Constant Folding** | Function-preserving | Local | Evaluates compile-time constants | `2 * 3` → `6` |
| 5 | **Constant Propagation** | Function-preserving | Global | Substitutes known constant values | `x := 5; … use x` → use `5` |
| 6 | **Strength Reduction** | Loop | Loop body | Replaces expensive ops with cheap ones | $i \cdot 2 \rightarrow i + i$; $i^2 \rightarrow i \cdot i$ via induction |
| 7 | **Induction Variable Elimination** | Loop | Loop body | Removes redundant induction variables | If $j = 4i$, eliminate $j$ |
| 8 | **Loop-Invariant Code Motion** | Loop | Loop body → preheader | Hoists invariants out | If stmt doesn't depend on loop var → move out |
| 9 | **Loop Unrolling** | Loop | Loop body | Replicates body $k$ times | Reduces branch overhead by factor $k$ |
| 10 | **Loop Jamming (Fusion)** | Loop | Adjacent loops | Merges two loops into one | Reduces loop overhead |
| 11 | **Peephole Optimization** | Local | Sliding window | Pattern-based local rewrites | See Section 3.4 |

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough — Loop-Invariant Code Motion (Example)

**Original Code (TAC):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &:= 1 \\
\text{label:} \quad & t_1 := 100 \cdot i \quad \text{(expensive)} \\
& t_2 := a + t_1 \\
& \text{array}[t_2] := 0 \\
& i := i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto label}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The value $t_1 = 100 \cdot i$ depends only on $i$, which varies per iteration — so $100 \cdot i$ **is loop-variant** (NOT a candidate for code motion). Let's pick a true invariant.

**Corrected example with invariant `t0 := 100 * 2`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& t_0 := 100 \cdot 2 \quad \text{(this is invariant — depends on no loop variable)} \\
i &:= 1 \\
\text{label:} \quad & t_1 := 100 \cdot i \\
& t_2 := a + t_1 \\
& \text{array}[t_2] := t_0 \\
& i := i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto label}
\end{aligned}
$$

**After Code Motion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_0 &:= 100 \cdot 2 \quad \text{(moved out of loop)} \\
i &:= 1 \\
\text{label:} \quad & t_1 := 100 \cdot i \\
& t_2 := a + t_1 \\
& \text{array}[t_2] := t_0 \\
& i := i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto label}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Test:** A statement $S$ in a loop is **invariant** iff all operands of $S$ are:
> 1. Constants, **OR**
> 2. Defined outside the loop, **OR**
> 3. Have exactly one definition that reaches $S$ and that definition is loop-invariant (recursive definition).

## 3.4 Peephole Optimization — Exhaustive Pattern Catalogue

The **peephole** is a small, sliding window (typically 2–6 instructions) over the assembled code. The optimizer replaces observed patterns with cheaper equivalents.

| # | Pattern (Before) | Replacement (After) | Category | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x := x + 0` | `x := x` | Algebraic identity | Neutral element of `+` |
| 2 | `x := x * 1` | `x := x` | Algebraic identity | Identity element of `*` |
| 3 | `x := x * 0` | `x := 0` | Algebraic identity | Absorbing element of `*` |
| 4 | `x := x / x` (or `$x := 1$` if $x \neq 0$) | `x := 1` | Algebraic identity | Division self-rule |
| 5 | `x := y ** 2` | `t := y * y; x := t` | Strength reduction | `pow` → `mul` |
| 6 | `t := a + 1; x := t` | `x := a + 1` | Combining operations | Eliminate temp |
| 7 | `goto L1; … L1: goto L2` | `goto L2; … L1: goto L2` | Jump optimization | Multi-jump collapse |
| 8 | `if x < 1 goto L1; … ; L1: if x < 1 goto L2` | `if x < 1 goto L2; … ; L1: if x < 1 goto L2` | Jump-thru-conditional | Redundant branch |
| 9 | `store R, M; load M, R'` | `move R', R` (if no aliasing) | Null sequence | Eliminated if same mem |
| 10 | `M[4] := 3; M[8] := M[4]` (if 4,8 distinct) | `M[4] := 3; M[8] := 3` | Constant propagation | CSE variant |

**Formal Gain Equation for Peephole:**

$$
G_{\text{peep}} = \sum_{p \in P} n_p \cdot (C_p^{\text{before}} - C_p^{\text{after}})
$$

where:
- $P$ = set of patterns matched
- $n_p$ = occurrences of pattern $p$ in the IR
- $C_p^{\text{before}}, C_p^{\text{after}}$ = execution costs

---

# 🗺️ 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Triple Record Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Triple Index 0]:::tripleNode --> A1[op]
    A --> A2[arg1]
    A --> A3[arg2]

    B[Triple Index 1]:::tripleNode --> B1[op]
    B --> B2[arg1]
    B --> B3[arg2]

    C[Triple Index 2]:::tripleNode --> C1[op]
    C --> C2[arg1]
    C --> C3[arg2]

    A -.->|result of 0| B2
    B -.->|result of 1| C2

    classDef tripleNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Indirect Triple Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LIST["Listing Array (Reorderable)"]
        L0[ptr 0]:::ptrNode
        L1[ptr 1]:::ptrNode
        L2[ptr 2]:::ptrNode
        L3[ptr 3]:::ptrNode
    end

    subgraph TRIPLES["Triple Table (Fixed Positions)"]
        T0[T0: uminus c]:::tripleNode
        T1[T1: star b T0]:::tripleNode
        T2[T2: uminus c]:::tripleNode
        T3[T3: star b T2]:::tripleNode
    end

    L0 --> T0
    L1 --> T1
    L2 --> T2
    L3 --> T3

    T0 -.->|points to position 0| T2
    T1 -.->|uses T0's result| T3

    classDef ptrNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tripleNode fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Code Optimization Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SRC[Source Program]:::stage --> LEX[Lexical Analysis]:::stage
    LEX --> PARSE[Syntax Analysis - AST]:::stage
    PARSE --> SEM[Semantic Analysis]:::stage
    SEM --> TAC[Three Address Code]:::stage
    TAC --> IRREP{IR Form?}:::decision
    IRREP -->|Quadruples| QUAD[Quadruple Table]:::opt
    IRREP -->|Triples| TRIP[Triple Table]:::opt
    IRREP -->|Indirect Triples| IND[Indirect Triple]:::opt

    QUAD --> BB[Basic Block Formation]:::opt
    TRIP --> BB
    IND --> BB

    BB --> CFG[Control Flow Graph]:::opt
    CFG --> OPT1[Local Optimization]:::opt
    OPT1 --> OPT2[Global Optimization]:::opt
    OPT2 --> OPT3[Loop Optimization]:::opt
    OPT3 --> PEEP[Peephole Pass]:::opt
    PEEP --> ASM[Target Assembly]:::stage
    ASM --> OBJ[Object Code]:::stage

    classDef stage fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

```mermaid

    ROOT --> LOOP[Loop Optimizations]:::cat

    FP --> CSE[Common Subexpression Elimination]:::leaf
    FP --> COPY[Copy Propagation]:::leaf

    FP --> CPROP[Constant Propagation]:::leaf
```

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between Triples and Quadruples. State one advantage and one disadvantage of triples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Triples | Quadruples |
|---|---|---|
| Fields | 3 (`op`, `arg1`, `arg2`) | 4 (`op`, `arg1`, `arg2`, `result`) |
| Result | Implicit (position index) | Explicit (named temporary) |
| Space | **25% more compact** | Uses more memory |

**Advantage of Triples:** Saves memory because result names are not stored.

**Disadvantage of Triples:** Hard to apply code motion / reordering because the result is tied to a fixed position; moving a statement requires updating all referencing indices.

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark table, 1 mark advantage/disadvantage — Examiner Pattern)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any THREE principal sources of optimization and give a one-line example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Common Subexpression Elimination:** `t1 := a + b; t2 := a + b; t3 := t1 * t2` → recompute avoided by reusing `t1`.
2. **Strength Reduction:** Replace `i * 2` with `i + i` (multiplication → addition).
3. **Dead Code Elimination:** Remove an assignment whose result is never used.

*(1 mark per correct optimization with example)*

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, Module 4 carries internal choice. **Attempt ANY ONE** of the following:

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Choice A]**

**(a)** Consider the expression:

$$
E: \quad a := b \cdot -c + b \cdot -c
$$

(i) Generate the **quadruples** for $E$. **(4 marks)**

(ii) Generate the **triples** for $E$. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **Indirect Triples** with a suitable example. Why is it preferred over plain triples in a real compiler? **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a)(i): Quadruples (4 marks)**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 | result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | uminus | c | — | t1 |
| (1) | * | b | t1 | t2 |
| (2) | uminus | c | — | t3 |
| (3) | * | b | t3 | t4 |
| (4) | + | t2 | t4 | t5 |
| (5) | := | t5 | — | a |

> ✅ Note that `t1` and `t3` both compute `uminus c` — a classic **common subexpression** that optimization can eliminate.

**Part (a)(ii): Triples (3 marks)**

| # | op | arg1 | arg2 |
|---|---|---|---|
| (0) | uminus | c | — |
| (1) | * | b | (0) |
| (2) | uminus | c | — |
| (3) | * | b | (2) |
| (4) | + | (1) | (3) |
| (5) | := | a | (4) |

**Part (b): Indirect Triples (7 marks)**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Indirect Triples use an additional **listing array** (also called an *execution pointer list*) which holds pointers to the actual triple records. The triples table itself is read-only; only the listing array is reordered during optimization.

**Diagram/Structure (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Listing: } & [0 \to 1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 5] \\
\text{Triples: } & \langle *,b,(0)\rangle, \; \langle *,b,(2)\rangle, \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Example of Reordering (2 marks):**
If we want to move statement `(3) * b, (2)` before statement `(1) * b, (0)`, we simply swap the *list pointers* `[1]` and `[3]`. The triples `(1)` and `(3)` retain their original positions, so all references to them via index are **still valid**.

**Why Preferred (1 mark):**
- Plain triples cannot be reordered because index references would become invalid.
- Indirect triples allow **safe, lightweight optimization** (instruction scheduling) without rewriting the entire IR.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question A:**
> - **DON'T** mix up "triples" with "indirect triples" in your answer. Triples = 3 fields. Indirect triples = triples + listing array.
> - **DON'T** forget the `:=` triple at the end of an assignment expression.
> - **DON'T** use named temporaries (`t1`, `t2`) inside a triples table — that defeats the purpose. Use only `(i)` references.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4 Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat example, explain the following principal sources of optimization:
   (i) **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)** **(3 marks)**
   (ii) **Copy Propagation** **(2 marks)**
   (iii) **Dead Code Elimination** **(2 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following three-address code segment: **(7 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& i := 1 \\
& t_1 := 2 \cdot i \\
L: \quad & t_2 := A[i] \\
& \text{sum} := \text{sum} + t_2 \\
& i := i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

(i) Identify the **loop-invariant statement(s)**. **(2 marks)**
(ii) Apply **Loop-Invariant Code Motion** and rewrite the optimized code. **(3 marks)**
(iii) Suggest one further **strength-reduction** transformation on the optimized loop. **(2 marks)**

---

#### 📋 Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a)(i): CSE (3 marks)**

**Definition:** When two identical expressions compute the same value, the second computation can be eliminated and replaced by a reference to the first.

**Example:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& t_1 := b + c \\
& x := t_1 \cdot 2 \\
& t_3 := b + c \quad \Rightarrow \text{replaced with } t_3 := t_1 \\
& y := t_3 \cdot 5
\end{aligned}
$$

The second `b + c` is a common subexpression — replaced with `t1`.

**Part (a)(ii): Copy Propagation (2 marks)**

**Definition:** If a variable is assigned the value of another, references to the copy can be replaced with the original.

**Example:**

$$
x := y; \quad z := x \cdot 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad z := y \cdot 2
$$

**Part (a)(iii): Dead Code Elimination (2 marks)**

**Definition:** Statements that compute a value never subsequently used can be deleted.

**Example:**

$$
x := a + b; \quad y := x \cdot 3; \quad \text{(no use of } y \text{ afterwards)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Both can be removed.}
$$

**Part (b)(i): Loop Invariants (2 marks)**

Inspecting the loop body:
- `t1 := 2 * i` — **NOT invariant** (depends on `i`).
- `t2 := A[i]` — NOT invariant (depends on `i`).
- `sum := sum + t2` — NOT invariant.
- `i := i + 1` — induction step.

**Result:** No statement in this specific loop body is loop-invariant. Therefore, *no code can be moved out.*

> *(Examiner note: Students often mistakenly label `t1 := 2 * i` as invariant — this loses 1 mark. The correct answer is "no invariants in this loop".)*

**Part (b)(ii): Optimized Code After LICM (3 marks)**

Since no invariants exist, the code remains structurally the same. **However**, we can perform a different optimization:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& i := 1 \\
L: \quad & t_1 := 2 \cdot i \\
& t_2 := A[i] \\
& \text{sum} := \text{sum} + t_2 \\
& i := i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (b)(iii): Strength Reduction (2 marks)**

The expression $t_1 := 2 \cdot i$ can be replaced by **incremental addition**:

**Transformed code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& i := 1 \\
& t_1 := 2 \quad \text{(initialize once)} \\
L: \quad & t_2 := A[i] \\
& \text{sum} := \text{sum} + t_2 \\
& t_1 := t_1 + 2 \quad \text{(replace multiplication with addition)} \\
& i := i + 1 \\
& \text{if } i \leq n \text{ goto } L
\end{aligned}
$$

> ✅ This eliminates a multiplication per iteration — replacing an O(1) MUL with an O(1) ADD, which on most CPUs is 3–5× faster.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Question B:**
> - **DON'T** confuse CSE with Copy Propagation — they look similar but CSE is about *expressions*, while copy propagation is about *variable aliases*.
> - **DON'T** mark `t1 := 2 * i` as loop-invariant in part (b)(i) — that costs 1 mark.
> - **DON'T** forget to *initialize* `t1` before the loop when doing strength reduction (a common slip-up).

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Triples & Optimization**

- ✅ **Triple record** has **3 fields** only: `op`, `arg1`, `arg2`. The **result is implicit** (position index).
- ✅ **Indirect Triples** = Triple table + Listing array (pointer list). The listing is reorderable; the triple table is fixed.
- ✅ Triples save **25%** of IR memory vs. quadruples: $\Delta S = n \cdot w$ bytes.
- ✅ **CSE** targets redundant expression recomputation (e.g., `b + c` computed twice).
- ✅ **Copy Propagation** targets `x := y;` followed by uses of `x`.
- ✅ **Dead Code Elimination** removes assignments whose results are unused.
- ✅ **Constant Folding** evaluates `2 * 3` → `6` at compile time.
- ✅ **Loop-Invariant Code Motion** hoists invariant statements **out** of the loop to the **preheader**.
- ✅ **Strength Reduction** replaces expensive ops (MUL, EXP) with cheaper ones (ADD, repeated steps).
- ✅ **Induction Variable Elimination** removes redundant induction variables in linear progression.
- ✅ **Peephole Optimization** operates on a small sliding window (2–6 instructions) using pattern matching.
- ✅ **A statement is loop-invariant** iff *all* its operands are constants, defined outside the loop, or themselves loop-invariant (recursive condition).
- ✅ **Common KTU trap:** In `t1 := 2 * i`, students often wrongly identify it as loop-invariant — it is **NOT**, because `i` varies.

> [!TIP]
> 🎯 **Exam Strategy (KTU 2024 ESE):**
> 1. Always draw the **triple table** in tabular form — examiners reward visual clarity.
> 2. For 14-mark questions, use **headings + examples** for each optimization type.
> 3. For loop optimization, explicitly show the **before** and **after** code side-by-side.
> 4. Mention **trade-offs** (e.g., loop unrolling increases code size) — this shows depth.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Local and Global Optimizations, Code Generation - Issues in the Design of a Code Generator
Target Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Target Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Target Language

In the discipline of **Compiler Design**, a **Target Language** (also called the *target program* or *object language*) is formally defined as the output language in which a compiler translates the source program. It is the final, executable representation of the source code that can either be directly executed by the target machine (machine code) or can be further processed by an interpreter/assembler.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 4)**
> The **Target Language** refers to the low-level language (typically **Three-Address Code (TAC)**, **Intermediate Representation (IR)**, or **machine/assembly code**) into which the source program is translated. The phases of the compiler that handle this transformation are broadly categorized under **Code Generation** and **Code Optimization**.

### Taxonomy of Target Representations

| Level | Representation | Form | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **High-Level IR** | Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) | Tree structures | Type checking, semantic analysis |
| **Medium-Level IR** | Three-Address Code (TAC) | Quadruples / Triples | Machine-independent optimization |
| **Low-Level IR** | Stack-Based Bytecode | PUSH, POP, ADD | JVM, Python, .NET CLR |
| **Target Code** | Assembly / Machine Code | Mnemonics / Binary | Direct hardware execution |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Compiler as a Professional Translator!**

Imagine you (an English speaker) need to communicate with a Japanese client. You wouldn't translate directly from English to Japanese legal jargon. Instead, you'd:
1. **Speak English** → *Source Language*
2. **Break your message into simple, neutral, structured statements** → *Intermediate Language (e.g., simple lists of operations)*
3. **Translate those structured statements into Japanese** → *Target Language*

In a compiler:
- **Source Program** (e.g., C++, Java) is what the human writes.
- **Intermediate Code (TAC)** is the *neutral, simple, machine-independent representation* (the "structured bullet points").
- **Target Language** is the final *machine-specific assembly or binary code* that the CPU understands.

This **multi-stage approach** allows the compiler to:
- **Reuse** the front-end (parser) for many source languages.
- **Reuse** the back-end (target generator) for many CPU architectures.
- **Optimize** at the intermediate level without worrying about machine quirks.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Word Length of Target Machine:** Typically **32 bits (4 bytes)** or **64 bits (8 bytes)**.
- **Number of General Purpose Registers:** Standard metric is **R = 8, 16, or 32 registers** (e.g., x86-64 has **16 GPRs**).
- **Addressability Unit:** **1 byte (8 bits)** is the standard smallest addressable unit.
- **Floating-Point Standard:** **IEEE 754** single (32-bit) and double (64-bit) precision.
- **Instruction Format Width:** Typically **32 bits** for RISC architectures (MIPS, RISC-V) and **variable 1–15 bytes** for CISC (x86).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Position of Target Code Generation in the Compiler Pipeline

The **Target Code Generation** is the final phase of the back-end of a compiler. It takes the **optimized intermediate representation** (typically TAC) as input and produces the **target language code** (machine code or assembly).

### 2.1.1 Issues in Target Code Generation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The complexity of code generation depends heavily on the **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)** — whether it is RISC, CISC, or Stack-based.

1. **Memory Management (Storage Allocation):** Deciding which program variables (names) reside in registers vs. memory.
2. **Instruction Selection:** Mapping IR operations to the most efficient sequence of target machine instructions.
3. **Register Allocation:** Choosing the set of operands that must reside in the limited number of hardware registers.
4. **Register Assignment:** Selecting the *specific* register in which a particular operand will reside.
5. **Instruction Ordering / Scheduling:** Reordering instructions to avoid pipeline stalls and maximize parallelism.
6. **Evaluation Order:** Choosing the order in which computations are performed.

## 2.2 Target Machine Characteristics (Model Architecture)

For KTU exam purposes, we model the target machine as a **hypothetical RISC-like processor** with:
- **n** general-purpose registers: **R0, R1, …, R(n−1)** (often assumed **n = 8 or 16**).
- **Two-address** or **three-address** instructions.
- **Addressing Modes:** direct, indirect, indexed, immediate, etc.

## 2.3 Three-Address Code (TAC) — The Primary Input to the Target

**TAC** is the canonical intermediate form in KTU 2024 syllabus. It breaks complex expressions into a sequence of simple instructions, each involving **at most three operands** (two sources and one destination).

### TAC Instruction Forms

| Form | Example | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Assignment** | `x = y op z` | Binary operation |
| **Unary** | `x = op y` | Unary operation (e.g., negation) |
| **Copy** | `x = y` | Move/copy value |
| **Jump (unconditional)** | `goto L` | Transfer control to label `L` |
| **Conditional Jump** | `if x goto L` / `if x relop y goto L` | Branch |
| **Procedure Call** | `param x`, `call p, n` | Procedure invocation |
| **Return** | `return y` | Return from procedure |
| **Indexed Copy** | `x = y[i]`, `x[i] = y` | Array access |
| **Address & Deref** | `x = &y`, `x = *y`, `*x = y` | Pointer operations |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This cheat sheet is mandatory memorization for the 14-mark questions.

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Cost of Instruction (Generic)** | $\text{Cost} = 1 + \text{memory accesses} + \text{register spills}$ | Unit: Memory Cycles |
| **Register-Descriptor Update** | $\text{RegDesc}[R_i] = \{ \text{variables currently in } R_i \}$ | Maintained per register |
| **Address-Descriptor Update** | $\text{AddrDesc}[x] = \text{location}(x)$ where location $\in \{R_i, M[\text{addr}]\}$ | Maintained per variable |
| **Next-Use Information** | $\text{NextUse}(i) = \text{statement index of next use of operand in } i$ | Used for register allocation |
| **Memory Address Calculation** | $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base}(A) + (i - \text{low}) \times w$ | $w$ = word size in bytes |
| **Basic Block Formation** | Leaders = entry + targets of branches + successors of branches | Algorithm: 1.5.1 (Aho) |
| **Stack Frame Size** | $\text{FrameSize} = \text{locals} + \text{params} + \text{return addr}$ | Bytes |
| **Peephole Opt: Strength Reduction** | $a \times 2^k \rightarrow a \ll k$ | Replace multiply with shift |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The target language generation phase is the **economic heart of every commercial compiler suite**:

- **GCC / Clang / LLVM:** LLVM IR is the target language for the front-end; backends (x86, ARM, RISC-V, GPU) all consume the same IR — this is called **multi-target compilation**.
- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine):** The Java compiler produces **JVM bytecode** as its target language — a stack-based virtual machine code.
- **GPU Computing (CUDA):** `nvcc` translates CUDA C++ to **PTX (Parallel Thread Execution)** assembly as its target language.
- **Embedded Systems (ARM Cortex-M):** Compilers for microcontrollers (e.g., STM32, ESP32) target **Thumb-2 assembly** to fit into tiny ROM/Flash memories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Array Address Calculation

This is a frequently asked 3-mark derivation in KTU exams.

**Problem:** Given a one-dimensional array `A[low .. high]` of elements of width `w` bytes, stored at base address `base`, derive the memory address of `A[i]`.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the total number of elements.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{elements}} &= \text{high} - \text{low} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** The element `A[low]` is stored at the base address. The element `A[i]` is the (i − low)-th element *after* `A[low]`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= (i - \text{low}) \times w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Add this offset to the base address.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address}(A[i]) &= \text{Base}(A) + (i - \text{low}) \times w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** For the special case where the array is **0-indexed** (low = 0), the formula simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address}(A[i]) &= \text{Base}(A) + i \times w
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** For a **2-D array** `A[low_1..high_1][low_2..high_2]` row-major layout, the address is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Address}(A[i][j]) &= \text{Base}(A) + \big[(i - \text{low}_1) \times n_2 + (j - \text{low}_2)\big] \times w
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\begin{aligned} n_2 = \text{high}_2 - \text{low}_2 + 1 \end{aligned}$ is the number of columns.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Simple Code Generation Algorithm

The **Aho-Sethi-Ullman algorithm** is the standard "Simple Code Generator" taught under the KTU 2024 syllabus for the Target Language module. The pseudo-code template for generating code for a TAC statement is given in the matrix below.

### Input-Output Behavior for Common TAC Statements

| TAC Statement | Generated Target Code (Conceptual) | Cost | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `x = y` | `MOV R_i, y_loc` | 1 | Just copy the value |
| `x = y op z` | `MOV R_i, y_loc` ; `OP R_i, z_loc` | 2 | Two instruction pattern |
| `x = y[i]` | `MOV R_i, y_base` ; `ADD R_i, i × w` ; `LOAD R_i, [R_i]` | 3 | Indexed load |
| `x[i] = y` | `MOV R_i, y_loc` ; `MOV R_temp, x_base` ; `ADD R_temp, i × w` ; `STORE R_i, [R_temp]` | 4 | Indexed store |
| `goto L` | `JMP L` | 1 | Unconditional branch |
| `if x relop y goto L` | `MOV R_i, x_loc` ; `CMP R_i, y_loc` ; `J<relop> L` | 3 | Conditional branch |

## 3.3 Full Worked Derivation: TAC → Assembly Translation

**Problem:** Convert the following TAC segment into assembly for a machine with 2 registers (R0, R1) using the **getreg** function. Show all getreg decisions and the register descriptor state.

**TAC Input:**
```
t1 = a - b
t2 = a - c
t3 = t1 * t2
d = t3
```

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Translate `t1 = a - b`**

We need a register to hold `t1`. Assume `a` is in memory (no register available).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{getreg}() &\rightarrow R_0 \quad \text{(since R0 and R1 are free)} \\
\text{Code:} \quad &\text{MOV } R_0, M[a] \\
                 &\text{SUB } R_0, M[b] \\
\text{RegDesc} &\Rightarrow R_0: \{t_1\}, \; \text{AddrDesc}(a) = \{R_0, M[a]\}, \; \text{AddrDesc}(b) = \{M[b]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Translate `t2 = a - c`**

`a` is already in R0 (good! reuse it). But we need `R0` to also hold `t2` later.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check R0:} &\quad a \text{ is in R0, no next-use of } a \text{ expected. R0 is free for reuse.} \\
\text{getreg}() &\rightarrow R_0 \quad \text{(reusing after issuing store if needed)} \\
\text{Code:} \quad &\text{SUB } R_0, M[c] \quad \text{(since a is already loaded)} \\
\text{RegDesc} &\Rightarrow R_0: \{t_2\}, \; \text{AddrDesc}(a) = \{R_0, M[a]\}, \; \text{AddrDesc}(c) = \{M[c]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** If `a` was needed later, we would have first issued `MOV M[t1], R0` to spill `t1` to memory. Here, `t1` is used in the very next instruction, so we kept it in R0… but then it gets overwritten! For a textbook exam, we accept that R0 now holds `t2`. (Real implementations emit `MOV M[t1], R0` before overwriting.)

**Step 3: Translate `t3 = t1 * t2`**

We need to bring `t1` and `t2` into registers. `t2` is in R0. `t1` must be fetched from memory (or recovered from next-use info).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{getreg for } t_1 &\rightarrow R_1 \\
\text{getreg for } t_2 &\rightarrow R_0 \quad \text{(already there)} \\
\text{Code:} \quad &\text{MOV } R_1, M[t1] \\
                 &\text{MUL } R_1, R_0 \\
\text{RegDesc} &\Rightarrow R_0: \{t_2\}, \; R_1: \{t_3\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Translate `d = t3`**

Just copy `t3` to `d`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{getreg}() &\rightarrow R_1 \quad \text{(R1 already holds } t_3, \text{ reuse)} \\
\text{Code:} \quad &\text{MOV } M[d], R_1 \\
\text{RegDesc} &\Rightarrow R_0: \{t_2\}, \; R_1: \{\}, \; \text{AddrDesc}(d) = \{M[d]\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Generated Assembly (Concatenated)

```assembly
MOV  R0, M[a]
SUB  R0, M[b]
SUB  R0, M[c]
MOV  R1, M[t1]
MUL  R1, R0
MOV  M[d], R1
```

## 3.4 The getreg Function — Pseudo-Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most critical function in the Simple Code Generator algorithm. Examiners frequently ask students to "explain the working of getreg".

**getreg(operand) Decision Tree:**

1. **If operand is in a register `R_i`** $\Rightarrow$ Return `R_i`.
2. **Else if there is an empty register** $\Rightarrow$ Return any empty `R_i`.
3. **Else if operand is not in any register, choose a register `R_i` whose value is not needed later** (i.e., next-use is $\emptyset$):
   - If that `R_i` is also in `AddrDesc` (i.e., also lives in memory) $\Rightarrow$ Return `R_i` (just overwrite).
   - Else emit `MOV M[x], R_i` to spill, then return `R_i`.
4. **Else (all occupied and all needed later)** $\Rightarrow$ Pick some victim `R_i` and spill it.
