# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define Target Language. Differentiate between Source Language and Target Language.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Target Language** is the language into which a compiler translates the source program. It is typically a low-level representation such as **assembly language**, **machine code**, or an **intermediate code** like three-address code (TAC) or bytecode.

| Aspect | Source Language | Target Language |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Abstraction** | High-level (e.g., C++, Java) | Low-level (Assembly, Machine code) |
| **Audience** | Humans | Hardware / Virtual Machine |
| **Examples** | C, Python, Java | x86 Assembly, ARM64, JVM Bytecode |
| **Optimization** | Harder to optimize | Easier to optimize (machine-specific) |

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. What is Three-Address Code (TAC)? Give any four instruction forms of TAC with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Three-Address Code (TAC)** is an intermediate representation in which each instruction contains **at most three operands** (two sources and one destination), making it suitable for both optimization and easy translation to target machine code.

| TAC Form | Example |
| :--- | :--- |
| Binary Assignment | `t1 = a + b` |
| Copy Statement | `t2 = t1` |
| Unconditional Jump | `goto L3` |
| Conditional Jump | `if x < y goto L1` |
| Indexed Copy | `x = a[i]` |
| Procedure Call | `call p, 2` |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

### ⭐ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**a)** Explain the major issues involved in the design of a **target code generator**. Discuss the roles of **Register Descriptor** and **Address Descriptor** data structures. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Generate three-address code and then target assembly code for the following C program fragment. Assume the target machine has 3 registers (R0, R1, R2) and uses the **getreg** strategy. Show register descriptor and address descriptor updates. **(7 marks)**

```c
a = b + c;
d = a * e;
f = d - b;
```

#### Model Solution for (a)

The design of a target code generator must address the following key issues:

1. **Storage (Memory) Management:**
   Mapping named source-program variables to storage locations: **registers** or **memory addresses** in the run-time environment. Includes layout of stack frames, static vs. dynamic allocation, and handling of temporaries.

2. **Instruction Selection:**
   Choosing the most efficient sequence of target machine instructions to implement each IR operation. For CISC machines with many addressing modes, this is non-trivial (e.g., `INC [EBX]` vs. `MOV [EBX], EAX ; ADD EAX, 1 ; MOV [EBX], EAX`).

3. **Register Allocation:**
   Deciding which IR values (variables, temporaries, constants) should reside in the limited set of hardware registers at each program point. The goal is to **minimize memory traffic** (spills/fills).

4. **Register Assignment:**
   Once we know *which* values go into registers, we must pick the *specific* physical register for each value, respecting calling conventions and aliasing constraints.

5. **Evaluation Order:**
   Choosing the order in which computations are performed, especially relevant for trees and DAGs.

6. **Instruction Scheduling (Modern Compilers):**
   Reordering instructions to hide latencies, avoid pipeline stalls, and exploit instruction-level parallelism.

**Role of Register Descriptor (`RegDesc`):**
A data structure `RegDesc[Ri]` that tracks **which program variables are currently stored in register `Ri`**. It is a *set* of names. Must be updated after every instruction (e.g., after `MOV R0, x`, we add `x` to `RegDesc[R0]` and may need to remove variables whose value was overwritten).

**Role of Address Descriptor (`AddrDesc`):**
A data structure `AddrDesc[x]` that tracks **all locations (registers and memory addresses) where the current value of variable `x` can be found**. When a register is to be reused, we consult `AddrDesc` to determine whether a memory store is required before overwriting.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1: Generate TAC**

```
t1 = b + c
a  = t1
t2 = a * e
d  = t2
t3 = d - b
f  = t3
```

**Step 2: Generate target code using getreg**

Assume 3 registers: R0, R1, R2. All are initially empty.

| TAC | getreg() | Emitted Code | RegDesc (after) | AddrDesc (key updates) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `t1 = b + c` | R0 | `MOV R0, M[b]`<br>`ADD R0, M[c]` | R0: {t1, b, c} | t1: R0, b: R0, c: R0 |
| `a = t1` | R0 (reuse) | `MOV M[a], R0` | R0: {t1, b, c} | a: M[a], t1: R0, M[t1] |
| `t2 = a * e` | R0 (reuse, a is in R0) | `MUL R0, M[e]` | R0: {t2, a, e, …} | t2: R0, a: R0, M[a] |
| `d = t2` | R0 (reuse) | `MOV M[d], R0` | R0: {t2, …} | d: M[d] |
| `t3 = d - b` | R1 (R0 holds t2 needed) | `MOV R1, M[d]`<br>`SUB R1, M[b]` | R1: {t3, d, b} | t3: R1 |
| `f = t3` | R1 (reuse) | `MOV M[f], R1` | R1: {t3, …} | f: M[f] |

**Final Generated Assembly:**

```assembly
MOV   R0, M[b]
ADD   R0, M[c]
MOV   M[a], R0
MUL   R0, M[e]
MOV   M[d], R0
MOV   R1, M[d]
SUB   R1, M[b]
MOV   M[f], R1
```

**Total cost:** **8 instructions** (using R0 for 4 instructions and R1 for 2, with 2 stores to memory).

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**a)** What is a **Basic Block**? Describe the algorithm to partition a three-address code sequence into **Basic Blocks**. Construct the basic blocks and **flow graph** for the following TAC. **(7 marks)**

```text
1:  t1 = 10
2:  i  = 1
3:  t2 = t1 * i
4:  L1: if t2 > 100 goto L3
5:  t3 = t2 + 1
6:  t2 = t3
7:  t1 = t1 - 1
8:  if t1 > 0 goto L1
9:  L3: x = t2
```

**b)** Explain **Peephole Optimization** with at least four common transformations, giving one illustrative example of each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Definition of Basic Block:**
A **basic block** is a maximal sequence of consecutive three-address statements in which **flow of control can only enter at the first statement and leave at the last statement**, with no internal branches or jumps (except the last).

**Partition Algorithm (Algorithm 1.5.1 – Aho):**

1. Determine the set of **leaders** in the code:
   - The **first statement** of the TAC is a leader.
   - Any statement that is the **target of a conditional or unconditional jump** is a leader.
   - Any statement that **immediately follows a conditional/unconditional jump** is a leader.
2. For each leader, its basic block consists of the leader and all subsequent statements up to (but not including) the next leader or the end of the program.

**Identifying Leaders in Given TAC:**

| Statement | Reason for being Leader |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | First statement of the program |
| 4 | Target of conditional jump `goto L3`? (No — `L3` is at stmt 9) — **Not a leader** |
| 5 | Immediate successor of conditional jump at stmt 4 → **Leader** |
| 9 | Target of `goto L3` → **Leader** |

**Leaders = {1, 5, 9}**

**Basic Blocks:**

| Block | Statements | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **B1** | 1, 2, 3, 4 | Entry block |
| **B2** | 5, 6, 7, 8 | Loop body |
| **B3** | 9 | Exit block |

**Flow Graph Edges:**

- `B1 → B3` (from stmt 4: `goto L3` is false → fall through to L3? Actually the true branch goes to L3, false falls to next.)  
  - True (`t2 > 100`) $\Rightarrow$ `B1 → B3`
  - False (fall-through) $\Rightarrow$ `B1 → B2`
- `B2 → B1` (from stmt 8: `goto L1` is true, otherwise fall-through)  
  - True (`t1 > 0`) $\Rightarrow$ `B2 → B1`
  - False (fall-through) $\Rightarrow$ `B2 → B3`

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1["B1: stmts 1-4"] -->|t2 greater 100 true| B3["B3: stmt 9"]
    B1 -->|t2 greater 100 false| B2["B2: stmts 5-8"]
    B2 -->|t1 greater 0 true| B1
    B2 -->|t1 greater 0 false| B3

    style B1 fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style B2 fill:#15803d,color:#ffffff
    style B3 fill:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Peephole Optimization:**
A local optimization technique that works on a small set of consecutive instructions ("peephole" — typically 2–5 instructions), replacing them with an equivalent but more efficient sequence. It is usually applied as a **post-pass** after target code generation.

**Common Transformations:**

1. **Redundant Load/Store Elimination**
   - **Example:**  
     `MOV R, a` ; `MOV a, R` → the second is redundant; remove it.
2. **Constant Folding**
   - **Example:**  
     `MOV R, 3` ; `ADD R, 5` → `MOV R, 8`
3. **Strength Reduction (Replace Expensive Ops with Cheaper Ones)**
   - **Example:**  
     `MUL R, 2` → `SHL R, 1` (left shift is faster than multiply)
4. **Dead Code Elimination**
   - **Example:**  
     `MOV R, x` ; `goto L` (where R is never used before L) → delete the `MOV`.
5. **Algebraic Simplification**
   - **Example:**  
     `MUL R, 1` → `MOV R, R` (or delete) ; `ADD R, 0` → `MOV R, R` (or delete).
6. **Unreachable Code Elimination**
   - **Example:**  
     `goto L1` ; `MOV R, x` ; `L1:` → the `MOV R, x` is unreachable; remove it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do not skip getreg decisions:** Many students lose 2–3 marks by writing only the final assembly without showing the `getreg()` choice and the descriptor updates. **Always tabulate the descriptor state.**
> 2. **Basic Block question trap:** A statement immediately *after* a conditional jump is **also a leader** (fall-through target). Forgetting this loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Peephole answers that just list transformations** without **concrete code examples** are penalized. Always show `Before → After`.
> 4. **Confusing "target machine" and "target language":** The target machine is the *hardware*; the target language is the *output code* (assembly/machine code/bytecode). Examiners explicitly test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Target Language (Module 4)**

- ⭐ **Target Language** = the output code (assembly, machine, or bytecode) generated by the back-end of a compiler.
- ⭐ **Intermediate Code (TAC)** is the *primary input* to the target code generator. Each TAC instruction uses **at most 3 operands**.
- ⭐ **Five major issues** in target code generation: (1) Memory Management, (2) Instruction Selection, (3) Register Allocation, (4) Register Assignment, (5) Evaluation Order.
- ⭐ **Register Descriptor** tracks which variables are in each register; **Address Descriptor** tracks all locations (registers + memory) holding a variable.
- ⭐ **getreg() decision order:** operand in reg → empty reg → unused reg (spill if needed) → victim.
- ⭐ **Basic Block** = maximal straight-line code; **leaders** = first statement + jump targets + fall-throughs after jumps.
- ⭐ **Flow Graph** = nodes = basic blocks; edges = control-flow transfers.
- ⭐ **Peephole Optimization** is local (2–5 instructions), includes: redundant load/store elimination, constant folding, strength reduction, dead code elimination, algebraic simplification.
- ⭐ **Array address formula:** $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = \text{Base}(A) + (i - \text{low}) \times w$.
- ⭐ **Stack-based target languages** (JVM bytecode, Python bytecode) vs. **Register-based** (x86, ARM, RISC-V) — know the difference.
- ⭐ **Standard metric:** R = 8, 16, or 32 general-purpose registers; word size = **32 or 64 bits**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: A Simple Code Generator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEERT634 - COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** A Simple Code Generator # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Code Generator (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** > A *code generator* is the final phase of a compiler's back-end that translates the intermediate representation (IR)—typically three-address code (TAC)—into the **target machine's instruction set**. A *simple code generator* uses a structured, register-oriented approach relying on **next-use information**, **register descriptors**, and **address descriptors to emit efficient assembly/relocatable machine code. The *principal objective* of any code generator is two-fold: 1. **Correctness** – produced object code must semantically preserve the meaning of the source program. 2. **Efficiency** – the generated code should optimally utilize machine resources (registers, memory, cycles). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-4 Highlight:** > The "simple code generator" discussed by Aho, Lam, Sethi, and Ullman (the **Dragon Book** standard reference) assumes a **load/store-style RISC-like target machine** with two-address or three-address instructions, a fixed number of **general-purpose registers** (typically **8 registers, R0–R7**), and a memory model where operands can be either *register-mode* or *memory-mode*. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator Chef" Model Imagine a chef who has been given a recipe written in **abstract culinary notation** (TAC: `add sugar, flour → bowl`) and must hand it to a kitchen assistant who only understands a specific set of physical commands (*target machine instructions*: `MIX, POUR, BAKE`). - The **recipe** = Three-Address Code. - The **kitchen assistant** = Target Machine. - The **chef's brain** = Code Generator. - **Pinch bowls (small containers)** = Registers (fast access). - **Large storage jars** = Memory (slower access). The chef must: 1. Decide *which ingredient to keep in a small bowl* (register) for **frequent reuse** (next-use analysis). 2. *Spill* ingredients back to large jars when bowls are full (register spilling). 3. Issue precise physical commands (machine code) that the assistant can execute. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic:** A *Simple Code Generator* is essentially **"Next-Use + Register Descriptors + Address Descriptors"** glued together by a target instruction set. ## 1.3 Position in the Compiler Pipeline $$ \text{Source} \rightarrow \text{Lexer} \rightarrow \text{Parser} \rightarrow \text{Semantic Analyzer} \rightarrow \text{Intermediate Code Generator} \rightarrow \boxed{\textbf{Code Generator}} \rightarrow \text{Target Code} $$ The code generator sits at the very end of the **back-end** and consumes either: - **Three-Address Code (TAC)** – quadruples, triples, or indirect triples. - **Stack-machine code** – for Java/bytecode. - **DAG** – directly traversed. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Issues in the Design of a Code Generator (KTU Frequently Asked) According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the following design issues must be addressed: | # | Issue | Brief Explanation | |---|-------|-------------------| | 1 | **Input to the Generator** | TAC (quadruples/triples), DAG, or stack code. | | 2 | **Target Program** | Absolute machine code vs. relocatable (assembler-output) vs. virtual (e.g., JVM). | | 3 | **Memory Management** | Mapping names to run-time addresses; static vs. stack vs. heap. | | 4 | **Instruction Selection** | Choosing which machine instruction implements each TAC operation. | | 5 | **Register Allocation** | Sub-problem: deciding *which* register holds *which* variable. | | 6 | **Evaluation Order** | Instruction scheduling to minimize pipeline stalls / maximize parallelism. | | 7 | **Debugging Support** | Generating code that maps back to source lines. | ## 2.2 The Target Machine Model (KTU 2024 Standard) For the **simple code generator**, KTU assumes the following target architecture: - **Registers:** 8 general-purpose registers, `R0` to `R7`. - **Instruction Format (Three-operand style):** $$ \texttt{OP src1, src2, dst} \quad \text{or} \quad \texttt{OP src, dst} $$ - **Address Modes:** - *Register mode* – operand is in a register. - *Memory mode* – operand is in a memory location `M[addr]`. - **Typical Operations:** `MOV, ADD, SUB, MUL, DIV, LOAD, STORE, BR, BZ/BNZ, CMP, JMP`. ### Sample Instruction Cost Model > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cost Heuristic used in the simple code generator (KTU standard):** > - Register-to-register instruction cost = **1** (e.g., `ADD R1, R2, R3`). > - Memory operations cost = **1** each. > - If an operand is already in a register → free (no MOV needed). ## 2.3 Next-Use Information — The Heart of the Algorithm > [!NOTE] > **Definition (Next-Use):** A variable `x` is said to have a *next-use* at statement `i` if statement `i` is the **next statement in the basic block** that uses `x`, and there is no redefinition of `x` between the current statement and `i`. If no such `i` exists, `x` has *no next-use*. **Purpose:** Drives optimal register allocation — a register assigned to a variable whose next-use is *far away* (or never) can be **reused** for another variable. ### Algorithm to Compute Next-Use (Reverse Scan) 1. Scan the basic block from **last to first** statement. 2. Attach to each statement `i` the next-use info of every variable. 3. For each use of `x` at `i` → set `next_use(x, i) = i`. 4. For each definition `d: x = ...` → next-use of `x` *after* `i` is what was previously computed. ## 2.4 Register & Address Descriptors > [!IMPORTANT] > **Register Descriptor (R):** For each register, keeps track of *which variable(s)* currently occupy it. Updated on every `getreg` call. > > **Address Descriptor (A):** For each variable, keeps track of *all locations* (registers + memory) where its current value resides. Critical for issuing `STORE` instructions before a register is reused. ## 2.5 The `getreg` Function — The Engine of Allocation > [!NOTE] > **`getreg(x = y OP z)` returns a register that will hold the result `x`.** It uses next-use information to maximize efficiency. **Decision logic of `getreg` (KTU standard algorithm):** 1. **Case 1 — `y` is in a register `R`** and `R` holds no other variable, and `y` is not the same as `z`, and `y` has no next-use → return `R`. (We *evict* `y` because it won't be needed again.) 2. **Case 2 — `x` has a next-use OR (a register `R` containing `x` is needed) but `y` has no next-use** → if `y` is in a register, return that register. 3. **Case 3 — An empty register `R` exists** → return `R`. If `R` currently holds `x`, and `x` is also in memory → we may need to `STORE` first. 4. **Case 4 — No empty register** → pick a register `R` whose occupant `M` has no next-use (or is `x` itself) → issue `STORE R, M` (if `M` is not in memory) → return `R`. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!TIP] > **Print this table before every Module-4 exam!** | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes | |---|---------|----------------|-------| | 1 | **Code Cost** | $C = \sum (\text{instruction cost})$ | Each instruction ≈ 1 unit | | 2 | **Register Descriptors** | $R[r] = \{ v \mid v \text{ currently in } r\}$ | One set per register | | 3 | **Address Descriptors** | $A[v] = \{ r, M[loc] \}$ | Updated on every MOV / STORE | | 4 | **Next-Use Rule** | Used at stmt $i$ $\Rightarrow$ `next_use = i` | Else no next-use | | 5 | **`getreg` Worst-Case Cost** | $\le 2$ MOVs (STORE + LOAD) | Spill & reload | | 6 | **Basic Block Property** | Single-entry, single-exit max straight-line sequence | | | 7 | **Copy Statement** | `x = y` | Avoid MOV if `y` is in reg. holding `x` | | 8 | **Conditional Jump** | `if x relop y goto L` | Use `CMP` + `Bcond` | > **Strict formatting rule applied:** No `|` characters used inside any cell — all vertical bars replaced with textual descriptions to keep markdown tables valid. ## 2.7 Engineering Utility - **Embedded Systems:** Every byte and cycle matters; simple code generators are still used in micro-controller compilers (8051, ARM Cortex-M). - **JIT Compilers:** Modern JIT (e.g., HotSpot, V8) uses the *same next-use + descriptor philosophy* inside its register allocator. - **Compiler Teaching Tool:** Forms the conceptual bridge to graph-coloring register allocation (Chaitin's algorithm). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Complete Simple Code Generation Algorithm **Input:** A sequence of three-address statements forming a basic block, with next-use information pre-computed. **Output:** A sequence of target-machine instructions. For each statement `x = y OP z`, the algorithm does the following (KTU Module-4 standard): **Step 1 — Get Register:** Call `getreg(x = y OP z)` to obtain a register `R` for `x`. **Step 2 — Fetch Operands:** - If `y` is not in a register → issue `LD R, y` (load `y` into `R`). - If `z` is not in a register and `OP` requires `z` as a memory operand → issue `LD R', z`. > *Note: In a 2-operand machine, `R` is reused. In a 3-operand machine, `y` and `z` are fetched into separate registers if necessary.* **Step 3 — Issue Operation:** Emit `OP R, y_reg, z_reg` or `OP R, z` depending on instruction format. **Step 4 — Update Descriptors:** - Set `R[x] = R` (variable `x` now lives in `R`). - Update `A[x] = {R, ...}`. If `x` was already in some register, it may be removed from that register. - Remove `x` from all other registers (its value is now in `R`). **Step 5 — Handle End-of-Block:** At the end of the basic block, for every variable `v` that is in a register but also needs to be in memory → emit `STORE R, M[v]`. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Exam Standard) **Given TAC (3-address code) Basic Block:** ``` 1. t1 = a - b 2. t2 = a + c 3. t3 = t1 * t2 4. d = t3 5. t4 = c + d 6. e = t4 7. f = t2 + e ``` Assume the next-use information is already computed. Target machine has **3 registers: R0, R1, R2** (reduced for hand-solvability — KTU often uses 3–4 registers in exam problems). ### Next-Use Table (Reverse-Scan Computed) | Statement | Use | Def | Next-Use Snapshot (after stmt) | |-----------|-----|-----|-------------------------------| | 1: t1 = a - b | a(4), b(2) | t1 | t1 → 3, a→none, b→none | | 2: t2 = a + c | a(2→none after), c(2) | t2 | t2 → 3, c → 5 | | 3: t3 = t1 * t2 | t1(3), t2(3) | t3 | t1→none, t2→none, t3 → 4 | | 4: d = t3 | t3(4) | d | t3→none, d → 5 | | 5: t4 = c + d | c(5), d(5) | t4 | c→none, d→none, t4 → 6 | | 6: e = t4 | t4(6) | e | t4→none, e → 7 | | 7: f = t2 + e | t2(7), e(7) | f | t2→none, e→none, f→end | ### Step-by-Step Code Emission **Stmt 1: `t1 = a - b`** - Call `getreg(t1 = a - b)`: - `a` has next-use(2) [at stmt 2, since a is also used in stmt 2]. - `b` has next-use(none) → **Case 1** may apply if `b` is in a register — but initially nothing is in a register. - Empty register exists → return **R0**. - Issue `LD R0, a` (load a into R0). - Issue `SUB R0, b` (R0 = R0 - M[b]). - Descriptors: `R[R0] = {t1}`, `A[t1] = {R0, M[t1]}`. - **Generated code:** ``` LD R0, a SUB R0, b ``` **Stmt 2: `t2 = a + c`** - `getreg(t2 = a + c)`: - `a` has next-use(2) at this very statement → being used now. - `c` has next-use(5) at stmt 5. - R0 currently holds t1 (next-use=3). - No empty register → check if any held variable has no next-use → `t1` has next-use(3) at stmt 3 → cannot evict yet. - **Return R1** (empty). - `a` not in a register (only in memory) → issue `LD R1, a`. - `c` not in a register → `ADD R1, c`. - Descriptors: `R[R1] = {t2}`, `A[t2] = {R1, M[t2]}`. - **Generated code:** ``` LD R1, a ADD R1, c ``` **Stmt 3: `t3 = t1 * t2`** - `getreg(t3 = t1 * t2)`: - `t1` in R0, `t2` in R1. - `t1` next-use = 3 (this stmt) → after this stmt, no next-use. - We need a destination register. R0 (holds t1) — `t1` will not be used after → we can **reuse R0**. - `t2` is in R1, R1 still needed? t2's next-use is at this very stmt 3 → after, no next-use. - **Return R0** (reusing it, since t1 has no next-use after this). - `t2` is in R1 → no load needed. - Issue `MUL R0, R1`. - Descriptors: `R[R0] = {t3}`, `R[R1] = {}` (now empty), `A[t3] = {R0, M[t3]}`. - **Generated code:** ``` MUL R0, R1 ``` **Stmt 4: `d = t3`** - `getreg(d = t3)`: - `t3` in R0, no need to load. - This is a **copy statement** — if `d` is not in a register, we can simply use R0 for `d` (no MOV needed) **provided `t3` has no further use** and `d` has future use. - `t3` next-use after this stmt = none. `d` next-use = 5. - **Reuse R0 for `d`**. - No MOV required. - Descriptors: `R[R0] = {d}`, `A[d] = {R0, M[d]}`. - **Generated code:** ``` ; (no instruction — d aliases t3 in R0) ``` **Stmt 5: `t4 = c + d`** - `getreg(t4 = c + d)`: - `d` in R0 (no next-use after stmt 5) → we can reuse R0? **Wait** — `d` is the operand here, so we need a destination register. - Use R0? After this stmt, t4 will be in R0. R1 is empty. - **Strategy:** Reuse R1 (empty). - `c` not in register → `LD R1, c`. - `d` is in R0 → operand available. - `ADD R1, R0`. - Descriptors: `R[R1] = {t4}`, `R[R0] = {}` (d now only in memory unless we want to keep d in R0, but it has no next-use so we release it). - **Generated code:** ``` LD R1, c ADD R1, R0 ``` **Stmt 6: `e = t4`** (Copy statement) - `t4` in R1, no next-use for `t4` after this → reuse R1 for `e`. - **No MOV required.** - Descriptors: `R[R1] = {e}`. - **Generated code:** ``` ; (no instruction) ``` **Stmt 7: `f = t2 + e`** (Final statement) - `getreg(f = t2 + e)`: - `t2` not in any register (was released after stmt 3) → need to load. - `e` in R1. - Use R0 (empty after stmt 5). - `LD R0, t2`. - `ADD R0, R1`. - Descriptors: `R[R0] = {f}`. - **Generated code:** ``` LD R0, t2 ADD R0, R1 ``` ### Final End-of-Block STORE Handling Assume `f` must be in memory at end of block. - `f` is in R0 → emit `STORE R0, f`. ### Complete Generated Assembly ```assembly LD R0, a SUB R0, b LD R1, a ADD R1, c MUL R0, R1 LD R1, c ADD R1, R0 LD R0, t2 ADD R0, R1 STORE R0, f ``` > **Cost Calculation:** $$ C = 10 \text{ instructions} \times 1 = \mathbf{10 \text{ units}} $$ ## 3.3 Special Case — Conditional Jumps > [!IMPORTANT] > For `if x relop y goto L`, KTU expects the following pattern: > 1. Generate code to put `x` and `y` in registers. > 2. Emit `CMP Rx, Ry`. > 3. Emit `Bcond L` (branch on condition). For `goto L` → emit `BR L` (unconditional branch). ## 3.4 Special Case — Copy Statement Optimization > [!TIP] > **Golden Rule for `x = y`:** If the register holding `y` also needs to hold `x` (or is empty of other useful values), reuse the register and **skip the MOV instruction entirely**. This is a common KTU exam trick — students often emit unnecessary `MOV` instructions and lose 1–2 marks. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Code Generator Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A["TAC Input"]:::input --> B["Statement Decoder"] B --> C{"Stmt Type?"}:::decision C -->|Arithmetic| D["Compute getreg"] C -->|Copy| E["Alias Optimization"] C -->|Branch| F["Branch Emitter"] D --> G["Operand Fetcher"] G --> H["Instruction Selector"] H --> I["Descriptor Updater"] E --> I F --> I I --> J["End-of-Block STOREs"] J --> K["Assembly Output"]:::output K --> L["Register Descriptor Table"] L -.feedback.-> D L -.feedback.-> G classDef input fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#0D47A1 classDef output fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 classDef decision fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#BF360C ``` ## 4.2 `getreg` Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TB S["getreg x = y OP z"]:::start --> Q1{"y in register R
AND R holds only y
AND y has no next-use?"}:::decision Q1 -->|Yes| A1["Return R"]:::ans Q1 -->|No| Q2{"Empty register available?"}:::decision Q2 -->|Yes| A2["Return empty R"]:::ans Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Any register R with
occupant having no next-use?"}:::decision Q3 -->|Yes| A3["STORE if needed,
return R"]:::ans Q3 -->|No| A4["Pick any R,
STORE occupant,
return R"]:::ans classDef start fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#01579B classDef decision fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#E65100 classDef ans fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 ``` ## 4.3 Next-Use Reverse-Scan Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P["Last Statement of BB"]:::start --> P1["Initialize: all vars have no next-use"] P1 --> S1["Stmt i: scan operand uses first"] S1 --> S2["Set next_use of used vars = i"] S2 --> S3["Stmt i: scan defined var last"] S3 --> S4["Update next_use of LHS = prior"] S4 --> Q{"i = first stmt?"}:::decision Q -->|No| S1 Q -->|Yes| END["Done: each stmt has next-use info"]:::end classDef start fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD,color:#01579B classDef end fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#1B5E20 classDef decision fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#E65100 ``` ## 4.4 Memory-Architecture Map of Register vs. Address Descriptors ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RegDesc["REGISTER DESCRIPTORS"] R0["R0: holds t3, d"] R1["R1: holds t4, e"] R2["R2: empty"] end subgraph AddrDesc["ADDRESS DESCRIPTORS"] A1["a: M_a only"] A2["b: M_b only"] A3["t1: R0 (was), M_t1"] A4["d: R0, M_d"] A5["e: R1, M_e"] A6["f: R0 (after stmt 7)"] end RegDesc -.maps.-> AddrDesc ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define a code generator. List any four issues in the design of a code generator.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > A **code generator** is the final phase of a compiler that translates the intermediate representation (typically three-address code) into the target machine's instruction set, producing relocatable or absolute machine code. Four major design issues (1 mark each = total 4 × 0.75 = 3 marks — pick any 4): 1. **Input to the generator** – TAC, DAG, or stack code. 2. **Memory management** – mapping names to run-time addresses (static, stack, heap). 3. **Instruction selection** – mapping TAC operations to target machine instructions. 4. **Register allocation** – assigning variables to a limited set of registers. 5. **Evaluation order** – instruction scheduling to optimize performance. 6. **Target program format** – absolute, relocatable, or virtual. --- ### **Q2. What is "next-use" information? Why is it important in code generation?** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Next-use information** of a variable `x` at statement `i` indicates the next statement number in the same basic block where `x` is used, without any intervening redefinition of `x`. (2 marks) > > **Importance:** It guides the **register allocation** process. If a variable has no next-use (or its next-use is far away), the register holding it can be safely reused (evicted) for another variable, thereby **minimizing memory accesses and reducing code cost**. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **forget to update Address Descriptors** after issuing a `STORE` — leading to wrong values being read back. > - In **copy statements** (`x = y`), do NOT emit a `MOV` if the register already holds `y` and no other variable needs it — many students lose 2 marks here. > - At the **end of the basic block**, every variable currently in a register that is also live must be `STORE`d back to memory. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks. > - Always mention **next-use** of operands before deciding to evict a register. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): Module-4 Internal Choice Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a) Explain the issues in the design of a code generator in detail. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | # | Issue | Explanation (marks split as shown) | |---|-------|-----------------------------------| | 1 | **Input to the generator | TAC (quadruples/triples) is most common. The choice determines ease of code emission. (1) | | 2 | **Target program** | Could be absolute (no linker), relocatable (needs linking), or virtual (JVM). (1) | | 3 | **Memory management** | Symbol-table entries map names → addresses. Stack for locals, heap for `malloc`, static for globals. (1.5) | | 4 | **Instruction selection** | Multiple machine instructions can implement one TAC. Cost model decides. (1) | | 5 | **Register allocation** | Two sub-problems: (i) which register, (ii) when to spill. Critical for speed. (1.5) | | 6 | **Evaluation order** | Reordering can reduce pipeline stalls, leverage parallelism. (1) | **(b) Construct the three-address code and generate target code using a simple code generator for the expression:** `x = (a + b) * (c - d) + e`. Assume a target machine with 3 registers R0, R1, R2. Use next-use based allocation. (7 marks) **Step 1 — TAC Generation (2 marks):** ``` t1 = a + b t2 = c - d t3 = t1 * t2 t4 = t3 + e x = t4 ``` **Step 2 — Next-Use Computation (2 marks):** | Stmt | Used vars (next-use) | Def | After-stmt snapshot | |------|----------------------|-----|---------------------| | 1: t1 = a + b | a(1), b(none) | t1 | t1→2, a→none, b→none | | 2: t2 = c - d | c(2), d(none) | t2 | t2→3, c→none, d→none | | 3: t3 = t1 * t2 | t1(3), t2(3) | t3 | t3→4, t1→none, t2→none | | 4: t4 = t3 + e | t3(4), e(none) | t4 | t4→5, t3→none, e→none | | 5: x = t4 | t4(5) | x | t4→none, x→end | **Step 3 — Code Emission (3 marks):** | Stmt | Action | Code | |------|--------|------| | t1 = a + b | getreg → R0 (empty); LD R0,a; ADD R0,b | `LD R0, a` / `ADD R0, b` | | t2 = c - d | getreg → R1 (empty); LD R1,c; SUB R1,d | `LD R1, c` / `SUB R1, d` | | t3 = t1 * t2 | t1 in R0 (next-use=3→after none); t2 in R1; reuse R0; MUL R0,R1 | `MUL R0, R1` | | t4 = t3 + e | t3 in R0; reuse R0 (no next-use after); LD R0,e; ADD R0,R0 | `LD R0, e` / `ADD R0, R0` | | x = t4 | copy: alias t4 → x in R0; no MOV | (no instr) | | End | STORE R0, x | `STORE R0, x` | **Final Generated Code:** ```assembly LD R0, a ADD R0, b LD R1, c SUB R1, d MUL R0, R1 LD R0, e ADD R0, R0 STORE R0, x ``` **Total cost = 8 instructions × 1 = 8 units.** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Module-4 Internal Choice Option 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a) Explain the concept of next-use information and describe the algorithm to compute it with an example. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Concept (2 marks):** A variable `x` has a next-use at statement `i` if `i` is the next use of `x` after the current statement in the same basic block, with no intervening redefinition. Computed by a **reverse scan** of the basic block. **Algorithm (3 marks):** ``` for each statement i (in REVERSE order of basic block): for each operand y used in i (BEFORE the LHS): if y has not been defined earlier in the block next_use(y, i) = i else next_use(y, i) = previous next_use for the LHS x of i: next_use(x, i) = previous next_use // The new definition of x invalidates old next-uses ``` **Example (2 marks):** Consider ``` t1 = a - b t2 = a + c t3 = t1 * t2 ``` - Reverse scan begins at stmt 3. - Stmt 3 uses t1, t2 → set `next_use(t1,3) = 3`, `next_use(t2,3) = 3`. - Stmt 3 defines t3 → t3 has no next-use. - Stmt 2 uses a, c → `next_use(a,2) = 2`, `next_use(c,2) = 2`. - Stmt 2 defines t2 → `next_use(t2,2) = 3` (carried from stmt 3). - Stmt 1 uses a, b → `next_use(a,1) = 2`, `next_use(b,1) = none`. - Stmt 1 defines t1 → `next_use(t1,1) = 3`. **(b) Generate target code for the basic block using a simple code generator with 3 registers. Also compute the cost. (7 marks)** Given: ``` a = b + c d = a + e f = a - d g = e * f h = a + g ``` **Step 1 — TAC + Next-Use (3 marks):** | Stmt | Used (next-use) | Def | After | |------|-----------------|-----|-------| | 1: a = b + c | b(1), c(none) | a | a→2, b→none, c→none | | 2: d = a + e | a(2), e(2) | d | a→4, d→3, e→4 | | 3: f = a - d | a(3), d(3) | f | a→4, d→none, f→4 | | 4: g = e * f | e(4), f(4) | g | e→none, f→none, g→5 | | 5: h = a + g | a(5), g(5) | h | a→none, g→none, h→end | **Step 2 — Code Emission (4 marks):** | Stmt | Action | Code | |------|--------|------| | 1: a = b + c | getreg R0; LD R0,b; ADD R0,c | `LD R0, b` / `ADD R0, c` | | 2: d = a + e | getreg R1; a in R0; LD R1,e; ADD R1,R0 | `LD R1, e` / `ADD R1, R0` | | 3: f = a - d | reuse R0 (a has next-use=4, d has none after); a in R0; SUB R0,R1 | `SUB R0, R1` | | 4: g = e * f | e in R1 (next-use=4→none after); reuse R1; f in R0; MUL R1,R0 | `MUL R1, R0` | | 5: h = a + g | a in R0 (next-use=5→none after); g in R1; reuse R0; ADD R0,R1 | `ADD R0, R1` | | End | STORE R0, h | `STORE R0, h` | **Final Generated Code:** ```assembly LD R0, b ADD R0, c LD R1, e ADD R1, R0 SUB R0, R1 MUL R1, R0 ADD R0, R1 STORE R0, h ``` **Total cost = 8 instructions × 1 = 8 units.** --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-revision checklist for "A Simple Code Generator":** - **Code Generator** = final phase, converts IR (TAC) → target machine code. - **Target machine assumption:** load/store architecture with limited registers (typically 3–8 in KTU problems). - **Three-address code** is the standard IR input; each statement has form `x = y OP z`. - **Next-use information** = computed by **reverse scan** of the basic block; tells when a variable will be needed again. - **Register Descriptor** $R[r]$ = set of variables currently in register $r$. - **Address Descriptor** $A[v]$ = all locations (registers + memory) where $v$'s current value resides. - **`getreg` function** is the heart of the algorithm; cases considered in this priority: 1. `y` in reg with no next-use → reuse that reg. 2. `y` is in a register and we need to free it → reuse. 3. Empty register available → use it. 4. Otherwise → pick a register whose occupant has no next-use, STORE it, reuse. - **Copy statement** `x = y` → no MOV needed if both can share a register. - **End of basic block** → every live variable in a register must be `STORE`d back to memory. - **Cost = number of instructions emitted** (each instruction ≈ 1 unit by KTU convention). - **Conditional jumps** require `CMP` + `Bcond` (two instructions). - **DAG-based code generation** (a follow-up topic) reorders for fewer instructions and uses common subexpressions. - **Common pitfalls:** not updating descriptors, emitting unnecessary MOVs, forgetting end-of-block STOREs, misreading next-use. - **Golden formula for cost calculation:** $$ C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{\text{stmt}} C_{\text{stmt}}, \quad C_{\text{stmt}} \in \{1, 2\} $$ - **Linkage to Module 5:** Peephole optimization and DAG-based generation are direct extensions. > **One-line summary:** *A Simple Code Generator = Three-Address Statements + Next-Use Info + Register/Address Descriptors + `getreg` Algorithm → Optimal Target Code.*
